File.Name: 8430b_8427-072.jpg
Notes: Pretty little Suncup flowers - I guess Cammisonia intermedia - in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family. As this prostrate stem keeps growing to the left, new yellow flowers will open and turn red with age as they are left behind. Like the flower in my [Previous] photo, the seeds develop in the ovary below the flowers. But the little flowers don't fade with age - they ripen! (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2008)

File.Name: 8430c_8427-092.jpg
Notes:
Delicate new flowers of Canyon Clarkia (Clarkia epilobioides) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family. They start with nodding buds, and the small white flowers turn pink with age. Alas, the common name for the genus is Farewell-to-Spring, and it already feels that way in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2008)

We have several showy Clarkia species in the mountains with larger more colorful flowers. This one is uncommon. It has four petals like the poppies in my [Previous] two photos, but it only has eight stamens, and the seeds form in the tube of the inferior ovary below the flowers rather than above.

File.Name: 8501a_8501-223.jpg
Notes:
Aaahhh...

I imagine that's the sound of this Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) with mating colors getting it's belly rubbed by kids on my after-school Hike Club today. You really can hypnotize these lizards by rubbing their belly. They seem to dig it! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2008)

File.Name: 8501b_8501-228.jpg
Notes:
I spotted this interesting butterfly chrysalis (Lepidoptera) hanging from a branch in the woods today. You can make out the primordial butterfly within the covering of the chrysalis! I can see eyes and long spotted antennae and wings and abdomen. There's a stumper - is it facing UP or DOWN? (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2008)

I guess the butterfly is a Checkerspot (genus Euphydryas in the family Nymphalidae). I also guess it's facing UP. We found this chrysalis on my after-school Hike Club adventure today. I didn't have my macro lens or a tripod, so I did the best I could with a tight crop. It's not quite sharp, but good enough!
Locality: genus Euphydryas in the family Nymphalidae). I also guess it's facing UP. We found this chrysalis on my after-school Hike Club adventure today. I didn't have my macro lens or a tripod

File.Name: 8502a_8502-091.jpg
Notes:
We had our school "Piece of Cake" bike ride today down and back Santa Rosa Road along the Santa Ynez River between Buellton and Lompoc. The ride is 40 miles or so - I'm tired tonight!

That's part of our road under the oak trees at the bottom, with the hills covered with brilliant yellow Wild Mustard (Brassica sp.) plants in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family. I guess they are mostly Black Mustard (B. nigra). (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 May 2008)

The legend is that the mustard plants were introduced by Franciscan Father Serra to mark the road between the California missions. I guess he didn't have to do an "Environmental Impact Analysis" about spreading those alien seeds! The wild mustards are pretty, but they cover the hillsides like this and inhibit the natives. I prefer diversity.

File.Name: 8502b_8502-103.jpg
Notes:
We had our school "Piece of Cake" bike ride today down and back Santa Rosa Road along the Santa Ynez River between Buellton and Lompoc. We had a wonderful ride on a perfect day through the beautiful agricultural country, including new vineyards like this. (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 May 2008)

Note that the rows go to the left, but you can see many other clear paths through the stakes. This was my stumper for the week:

Marc's stumper for the week: It's almost time to
pedal out Santa Rosa Road, but don't worry - it's a
piece of cake bike ride! This is beautiful agricultural
land. Look at the new vineyards and walnut groves as
you pedal by. You will see the clear rows between the
stakes that seem to move along with you. These are
the real plowed rows, but you can also see many clear
diagonal rows to the sides, and many more very thin
rows between them. It's a hypnotic effect, so watch
the road too! How many different orchard rows can
you see as you ride by? What math is at work here?

File.Name: 8503a_8503-013.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) near the creek. These butterflies usually stay near the willows and cottonwoods along the creek. They remind me of hummingbirds - they seem to spend most of their day chasing others of their kind away! (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2008)

Beautiful butterflies - but my favorite photo today is two clicks [Previous]:

File.Name: 8503b_8503-021.jpg
Notes: Uncommon female Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working new flowers of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Mint (Labiatae) family in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2008)

File.Name: 8503c_8502-168.jpg
Notes:
Turkey Vulture (Cathartes aura) bird riding the updrafts over a hillside along Santa Rosa Road during our school "Piece of Cake" bike ride yesterday. Sure it's a lucky shot, but I did take 400+ photos yesterday. It's that kind of luck. (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 May 2008)

Ah, my friends from the prison, they ask unto me,
"How good, how good does it feel to be free?"
And I answer them most mysteriously,
"Are birds free from the chains of the skyway?"

File.Name: 8504a_8503-010.jpg
Notes:
The beautiful Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly in my [Previous] photo took wing just as I pushed the button for my final photo. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2008)

The exposure is 1/80 @ f/14 @ ISO 400 - not fast enough to stop motion. It's interesting to flip between this photo and the last to try to figure out what is what!

File.Name: 8505a_8503-003.jpg
Notes:
Little immature Katydid (family Tettigoniidae in the order Orthoptera) - maybe a Fork-tailed Bush Katydid (Scudderia furcata)? I found several of these long-horned critters on a native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) shrub in the Asteraceae family growing on the wild edge of the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2008)

I must have got too close with my camera and tripod and scared the FRASS out of it! ("Frass" is a useful word - look it up!)

This is the busiest time of my school year. I post nature photos here, but I also take thousands of photos at the school where I teach. Now I'm busy organizing the rest of my photos for the annual school slide show due at the end of the month. Busy times - I'll do the best I can!

File.Name: 8506a_8502-088.jpg
Notes: Last Friday, we did our school bike ride from Buellton to Lompoc along Santa Rosa Road. We road past several tent-size swarms of buzzing flies (order Diptera), so of course I stopped to take a photo of the swarm. I was biking with my camera on my neck, no tripod. I cropped in tight, and I can see that these fliers are mostly biplanes! The flies are flying in tandom, mating in the air! (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 May 2008)

File.Name: 8507a_8503-043.jpg
Notes:
This is a flower of native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rose (Rosaceae) family in the woods - but everything is wrong about it. It's been infected with Orange Blackberry Rust caused by a fungus - maybe Arthuriomyces peckianus or Gymnoconia nitens or Phragmidium violaceum fungi.

The infection causes those brilliant orange pustules (aecia) mostly under the leaves, but it also changes the way the plant grows. The infected growth is weak and spindly, the leaves are small and too far apart and there are usually no spines. I've been seeing this more and more over the last few years. (See my 2004 photo at
   http://www.fotolog.com/mykologos/7703199)

Usually the infected plants in the canyon don't flower, so I was surprised to find this plant with a flower - but the flower is wrong too. Usually the blackberry flowers have five large petals and almost white fat stamens, but this infected flower has more petals and skinny yellow stamens. I wonder if those infected "leaves" are altered sepals of the flower? Compare a healthy flower at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/33921718

This is in our woods, but it's also a disease of commercial blackberries. *Sigh* It may be a new "invasive species" to worry about. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2008)
CommonNames: California Blackberry; Rose; Orange Blackberry Rust
ScientificName: Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Arthuriomyces peckianus; Gymnoconia nitens; Phragmidium violaceum; aecia

File.Name: 8508a_8503-038.jpg
Notes:
First new flower of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the canyon - it's a kind of upside-down Mariposa Lily in the Liliaceae family. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2008)

Put your hands together to make a cup. Then add one more hand to complete the circle. That's how these flowers are made. There are outward-pointing fringes on the petals, but there are inward-pointing hairs inside that make it easy for an insect to enter between the petals, but hard to leave without picking up lots of pollen. Nature is tricky like that!

File.Name: 8508b_8503-050.jpg
Notes: Beautiful flowers of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis var. redivivus - now given species rank as Styrax redivivus) - in the Storax (Styracaceae) family. They are at the peak of flowering in the woods this week, but they won't last long. The perfumed flowers aren't particularly showy from a distance, but they are elegant up close - maybe my favorite of all local native flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2008)
ScientificName: Styrax officinalis; redivivus; Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae
associatedTaxa: Styrax officinalis; redivivus; Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae

File.Name: 8509a_8509-123.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful new flowers of Elegant Clarkia (Clarkia unguiculata) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family. I got this photo in the woods near Lake Cachuma on the way to school this morning in the fog . In the photo, the flower reminds me of a squid in the ocean! (Santa Ynez Valley, 9 May 2008)

The petals look like someone took a scissors to the base, but that's how they are. They have four short stamens with pollen, but the other four long male stamens seem to be reaching for the female stigma. One way or another, this pretty flower will be pollinated!

These flowers and their kindred are also known as Farewell-to-Spring - it's starting to feel that way as we move from wet winter to dry summer in our Mediterranean climate here in central California.

File.Name: 8509b_8509-139.jpg
Notes:
Perfect flower of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. I captured this photo in the wild part of our garden after school today in the woods when the sun was low. It's pretty much right from my camera with no fiddling with contrast or saturation. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2008)

This is a native plant in the woods, but we've been encouraging it on the dry side of the garden, and it does fine. I guess it's a hummingbird flower, but it also offers the broad lower lip of the flower as a landing platform for large bees. The flowers are tasty, and the fragrant leaves are good for tea.

File.Name: 8510a_8510-024.jpg
Notes:
Tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) ovipositing on a spent flower of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family in the canyon. The larvae of these flies are preditors of aphids and other pests. Look close, and you can see one green aphid on the wing of the flower below the fly's eyes, and maybe another at the base of the flower. (Or is it just a husk?) (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2008)

What's interesting is that there were many aphids on nearby flowers, but the fly was not interested. I guess it's following the old wisdom to "go where it's going, not where it's been". Maybe I captured one of its eggs in my [Previous] photo.

I have a few photos in the archive that show the larvae in action:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/10519445
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/10527125
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/10556701
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Canyon Lupine; Pea

File.Name: 8510b_8510-023.jpg
Notes:
Interesting Aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) covering the end of a flower stalk of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family in the canyon. I saw a few green aphids, but most of them are dusted with a white covering like this. I didn't see any ants tending these aphids. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2008)

LOOK! The top aphid is releasing a bubble from its rear-end that looks like it will float away like it's underwater. I guess it's a drop of sweet honeydew that will drip back down over the aphid's back adding to the white deposit.

LOOK! Below the head of the greenish aphid just right of center, you can see a white egg. Maybe it's an egg from the Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) in my [Next] photo? The larvae of these flies are preditors of the aphids, and there's a feast here.
CommonNames: Aphids; Canyon Lupine; Pea; Syrphid Flower Fly
ScientificName: Aphididae; Homoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Syrphidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Aphididae; Homoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Syrphidae; Diptera

File.Name: 8510c_8510-011.jpg
Notes:
Interesting Aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) covering a single flower of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family in the canyon. Flowers are so ephemeral - here today, gone tomorrow. I expect aphids to work the stems, but here they are on the flowers. I can't recall seeing these aphids on our lupines before. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2008)

They remind me of that scene in the movie Aliens 2 where no one notices the aliens covering the walls until too late!

File.Name: 8510d_8510-001.jpg
Notes: This little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) looks like it's made of gold! I caught this pretty fly resting on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae family in the canyon trying to catch some morning sun to warm up after the cold fog. See my [Next] few photos to see what these golden flies are up to. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2008)

Notes: Slug-like green caterpillar of a Blue or Hairstreak butterfly (family Lycaenidae) on a flower spike of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family in the canyon. There are interesting Aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) on this flower stalk as well. See my [Previous] photos.

File.Name: 8512a_8510-030.jpg
Notes: Here's a closer shot of the head-end of the pretty caterpillar (order Lepidoptera) in my [Previous] photo. It's resting on a twig of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon. I reckon this caterpillar is carying its doom around its neck. Those white egg-like sacks behind the head are the real eggs of some parasitic Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae, order Diptera) - or something equally deadly - that will consume the caterpillar before it emerges as an adult. All insect larvae are vulnerable to attack by preditors and parasites. Nature is like that. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2008)

File.Name: 8512b_8510-032.jpg
Notes: This is some kind of pretty caterpillar (order Lepidoptera) on a twig of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon. I couldn't find it when I flipped through my "Caterpillars in the Field and Garden" book, so I guess it the larva of a moth. It's carrying some extra baggage around its next on the right. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2008)

File.Name: 8512c_8510-040.jpg
Notes: Fanciful caterpillar of a Western Tussock Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Lymantriidae, maybe Orgyia vetusta or kin) hiding under the leaves of native Wild SweetPea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family on the dry native side of the garden. I bumped up the exposure to bring out the colors in the shade of the leaves. The adult moths are nondescript, and the females are wingless. The clown-colored caterpillar has a scorpian look! (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2008)

File.Name: 8513a_8510-019.jpg
Notes:
This "blue" is brown!

I guess this is a female Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working a flower of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Mint (Labiatae) family. The "host plants" of these butterflies for the larvae are legumes and buckwheats, but the adults like the sages. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2008)

No time for new photos, I'm busy making my school slide show. You can see some of my school photos here:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/electronlinda/sets/72157602824768327/

File.Name: 8514a_8508-103.jpg
Notes: Beautiful Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) with mating colors on a wall in the garden. It's remarkable how the more or less flat scales cover the curves of its body, especially around the circular ear tympanum between it's eye and leg. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2008)

File.Name: 8515a_8515-064.jpg
Notes:
Perfect new flower (and bud) of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) growing on the dry side of our garden at home. The flowers are a kind of upside-down Mariposa Lily in the Liliaceae family. They are a "tender trap" - easy for insects to crawl into, but hard to get out without carrying a load of pollen to pass on to other flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2008)

Sure I'm still working on the school slide show, but there was still light in the garden when I got home from school today, so I couldn't resist getting a few photos.

File.Name: 8515b_8515-069.jpg
Notes: Bristly flowers of native Rock Phacelia (Phacelia imbricata) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family on the dry edge of our garden. Look close - even the stamens have bristles! The curled flower heads are known as a "scorpioid inflorescence" - coiled like a scorpian's tail or the neck of a violin - so that new flowers keep appearing up the stalk as the coil unwinds. They look like fat caterpillars! (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2008)

File.Name: 8515c_8503-041.jpg
Notes: Native Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus) flower in the canyon. This is a different kind of wild blackerry in the Rose (Rosaceae) family. They are common and almost weedy in the Sierras and Oregon, but they are scarce here in the Santa Barbara mountains. I've never found the edible but thin fruit here, so I reckon these plants are hanging on as remnants of wetter times. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2008)

File.Name: 8516a_8516-087.jpg
Notes:
Treebeard's Photos is my place for photos of natural life in the woods, but I guess it's ok to show an occasional photo of the photographer! That's me leading the warm-up lap at my school's All-Valley Track Meet today. We invite kids from many schools all around the Santa Ynez Valley. It's remarkable to look back over my shoulder and see hundreds of middle school kids chasing me around the track! (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 May 2008)

Those are the Santa Ynez Mountains in the background. I take most of my photos at home about 15 miles to the left.

We had our hottest day of the year just in time for the track meet today. Despite 100 degree Fahrenheit temperatures, all went well, though I do feel a bit sun-sick tonight. I hope there's a good classic movie on TV tonight.

File.Name: 8517a_8517-038.jpg
Notes:
A frisky pair of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies on a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae family near the creek today. I guess the upper butterfly is female. She is wagging her abdomen to attract the attentions of the male. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2008)

Did you spot the Ladybug on the butterfly's wing?

File.Name: 8517b_8517-035.jpg
Notes: Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae family near the creek. Note the Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera). The ladybugs are converging today! (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2008)

File.Name: 8517c_8517-050.jpg
Notes:
Here are Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) near the creek in our canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2008)

There were thousands of these pretty beetles in the air today! Hard to get a photo, but I captured these on a leaf of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family near the creek.

Last year I found these Lady Beetles converging in October and again in February. I've never seen them swarm so late. It's like a secret rave party - how do they all get the date and location right?

Notes:
Perfect Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae family near the creek today.

Busy, busy - I'm working on my school slide show, and I'm off on a San Francisco road trip tomorrow with our eighth grade graduating kids for the week. Then comes the slide show and graduation. I'll do what I can for photos when I return.

*Sigh* Almost summer!

File.Name: 8518d_8517-006.jpg
Notes: Perfect Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) resting on a blade of native Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2008)

File.Name: 8518e_8517-011.jpg
Notes:
A swarm of Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) near the creek in our canyon yesterday. I went back today expected them to be settled on the vegetation, but they are still flying around like this.

Last year the ladybugs converged in September and again in December and February. This is the first convergence I've seen this year.

How do all the ladybugs network to know when the date of the rave is changed?

File.Name: 8528a_8530-015.jpg
Notes: Tattered and threadbare Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a Coreopsis (Asteraceae) flower in the garden today. The wings have lost their fairy dusk and are almost transparent, but this butterfly is still working its shift. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2008)

File.Name: 8529a_8530-006.jpg
Notes:
California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly working the new flowers of a native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family growing on the edge of the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2008)

The little flowers don't look like much, but they must produce lots of nectar as they attract buzzing swarms of insects.

File.Name: 8530a_8530-022.jpg
Notes:
Pretty butterfly with a pretty name - this is a Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly working the non-native Thyme (Thymus vulgaris or kin in the Lamiaceae family) flowers in the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2008)

I've been busy with school stuff. I was in San Francisco for a week with our graduating 8th grade class. Then I came home to finish working on our school slide show - 1,831 photos (mostly mine) plus some video clips all sequenced into a one hour video of 23 segments with music. I organize all the photos and then work with my friend Nick to produce a DVD using Final Cut Pro.

You can see some of my school photos here:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/electronlinda/sets/72157602824768327/

The slide show was last night, and our graduation ceremony was today. It was a pleasure to get out in the garden with my camera this afternoon to chase butterflies!

File.Name: 8531a_8531-056.jpg
Notes:
Fat caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on its usual host plant - non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family. The fennel is introduced, and a bad weed in the mountains. These native butterflies also use other native Apiaceae) species like like Sanicula and Lomatium and Tauschia, but they seem to prefer the invasive fennel. We try to control these weeds, but we always leave a few for the caterpillars, and try to remember to pull them before they make seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2008)

There's an orange line on the segment above the caterpillar's head that is the lid for a surprise jack in the box. I tried tickling the caterpillar with a grass stem hoping to see its brilliant orange "osmetarium" - no luck, but see my photo at:    http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/14098442
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Apiaceae; Sanicula; Lomatium; Tauschia
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Apiaceae; Sanicula; Lomatium; Tauschia

File.Name: 8531b_8531-045.jpg
Notes:
Little "Blue" butterfly - I guess an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) - resting on the chip seal gravel road in our canyon. A pair of these blues were courting and being frisky. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2008)

I find many butterflies along the road in our canyon. I guess they get a lift from the warm air over the road, like hang-gliders and Turkey Vulture birds cruising along the ridge tops.

File.Name: 8531c_8531-060.jpg
Notes: California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly working the new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the canyon. You can its typical hairstreak "tails" on its hindwings. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2008)

File.Name: 8601a_8531-020.jpg
Notes:
Looking eye-to-eyes with a newly-emerged Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working flowers of a native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) shrub in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family growing on the edge of the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2008)

You can see its double-barrelled proboscis in action.

I tried to post these photos last night, but I couldn't get past the "Unexpected Error" message. That's frustrating when you have photos ready to go.

File.Name: 8601b_8531-021.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful newly-emerged Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working flowers of a native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) shrub in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family growing on the edge of the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2008)

This is a true "Brush-footed" (subfamily Nymphalinae) butterfly. It perches on just four legs, and you can see the two reduced reddish-colored front legs below the eyes, T-Rex style. I don't understand the advantage of this, but these are the most common butterfly in the woods by far, so it works.
Locality: subfamily Nymphalinae) butterfly. It perches on just four legs
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Rhamnus tomentella; Rhamnaceae; Nymphalinae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Rhamnus tomentella; Rhamnaceae; Nymphalinae

File.Name: 8601c_8531-072.jpg
Notes: Perfect Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working flowers of a native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) shrub in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family growing on the edge of the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2008)

File.Name: 8601d_8531-074.jpg
Notes: Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working flowers of a native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) shrub in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family growing on the edge of the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2008)

File.Name: 8602a_8601-089.jpg
Notes:
I spotted this beautiful butterfly chrysalis hanging from a twig near the creek. I guess this is the pupal stage of the same Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies that I've been showing in [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2008)

The caterpillars are web-spinners, and you can see some webbing in the photo. Also there is a native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Diplacus longiflorus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family just above this twig where I've been seeing the caterpillars that only feed on "scrophs".

Look close at the chrysalis. I guess the butterfly is about to emerge, and the red part at the upper end is waste material after its transformation. At the bottom, you can make out a wing and one of the striped antennae and a bit of an eye. The chrysalis is hanging belly out, curved along its back.
Locality:
Scrophulariaceae) family just above this twig where I've been seeing the caterpillars that only feed on "scrophs".

Look close at the chrysalis. I guess the butterfly is about to emerge
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Bush Monkey Flower; Figwort
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: 8602b_8601-083.jpg
Notes:
I know I sometimes try to get too close to the butterflies and other insects I love photographing. You know, like framing a photo of your companion at the Grand Canyon so tight that you can't see the canyon!

Here's a photo of one of the same newly-emerged Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies that I've been showing in my [Previous] photos. This one is working new flowers of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae family in the native part of the garden. The butterfly looks perfect, like it is posing to become a museum specimen! (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2008)

File.Name: 8603a_8531-051.jpg
Notes:
We're having a convergence of Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) near the creek in our canyon. These beetles are converging on leaves of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae family. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2008)

You can't tell these beetles by counting spots, since they are all different. Look close at the beetle just above the center of the photo - it's the Happy Face Lady Beetle!

What's strange is that this is the first lady beetle convergence I've seen this year, and it's summer. Usually they converge in mid-winter between December and February. I have many photos in the archive:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/18527346 (3 Feb 2007)
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/17585542 (16 Dec 2006)
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/16230121 (7 Oct 2006)
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/5623817 (29 Jan 2004)
ScientificName: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae

File.Name: 8603b_8531-048.jpg
Notes: A dogpile of Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) near the creek in our canyon last weekend. These lady beetles usually converge in mid-winter, so I'm surprised to find them now at the beginning of summer. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2008)

File.Name: 8604a_8530-002.jpg
Notes:
This looks like a spider in space with an Ion Propulsion Drive!

Nah. I got too close to this Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with my camera in the garden, and it fell away. Here it is building a new escape web with its three (pairs of) spinnerets. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2008)

File.Name: 8604b_8531-041.jpg
Notes: Cute little fly (order Diptera) working new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the canyon. I have no time to look it up tonight, but here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2008)

File.Name: 8604c_8531-068.jpg
Notes: Long-horned Beetle (Cerambycidae in the order Coleoptera) looming over new flowers of a native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) shrub in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family growing on the edge of the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2008)

File.Name: 8605a_8606-123.jpg
Notes:
I'm not sure if these little red "things" are eggs or larvae. I guess the flower is California Chicory (Rafinesquia californica) in the Asteraceae family. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2008)

I notice some white exudate coming from the tiny critters. I wonder if these are larvae of Mealybugs?

File.Name: 8605b_8606-124.jpg
Notes:
I noticed this little ball of clay hiding behind a leaf of flowering native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family near home. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2008)

I'm pretty sure this is the mud nest of a solitary Potter Wasp (order Hymenoptera, family Vespidea, genus Eumeninae). Compare my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/23078194
ScientificName: Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Hymenoptera; Vespidea; Eumeninae
associatedTaxa: Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Hymenoptera; Vespidea; Eumeninae

File.Name: 8606a_8606-119.jpg
Notes:
There's a lot happening in this photo - look close!

Here's a little caterpillar working new flowers of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon Traskiae) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) plant family growing in the mountains near home. It's an endemic local shrub named after the remarkable naturalist Blanch Trask. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2008)

The caterpillar's head is to the left, so we're looking at its rear end in this photo. It sure looks like it's spewing out that white foamy mass below, but I don't believe it.

That white bubbly froth of slime must be from some kind of Spittle Bug (family Cercopidae in the order Hemiptera). Look close and you can see tiny orange Thrips (order Thysanoptera) riding the foam.

It's been a very busy week. My school slide show and graduation happened last week, then grades and comments, then my slide show/presentation about local butterflies at the local library last night, and a butterfly photography workshop/hike tomorrow. All well, so far. It almost feels like summer!
Locality:
order Thysanoptera) riding the foam.

It's been a very busy week. My school slide show and graduation happened last week
CommonNames: Trask's Yerba Santa; Waterleaf; Spittle Bug; Thrips
ScientificName: Eriodictyon Traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Cercopidae; Hemiptera; Thysanoptera
associatedTaxa: Eriodictyon Traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Cercopidae; Hemiptera; Thysanoptera

File.Name: 8606b_8606-103.jpg
Notes: Beautiful Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working new flowers of the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2008)

File.Name: 8606c_8606-102.jpg
Notes: New butterflies working the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. Here is a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in front, with a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly in the back. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2008)
CommonNames: Butterfly Bush; Variable Checkerspot; Painted Lady
ScientificName: Buddleia davidii; Euphydryas chalcedona; Vanessa cardui
associatedTaxa: Buddleia davidii; Euphydryas chalcedona; Vanessa cardui

File.Name: 8606d_8606-110.jpg
Notes: Beautiful Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly working non-native Black Mustard (Brassica nigra) flowers near home. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2008)

File.Name: 8607a_8607-018.jpg
Notes:
Here's a mating pair of little Bee Flies (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera). I reckon that's the skinny male on the left, and the plump (with eggs) female on the right. They were easy to follow in the air as I chased them. You gotta wonder which way they went when they took off - answer, to the left. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2008)

I led a workshop/hike for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society (http://www.syvnature.org/) today as a follow-up to my slideshow/talk at the library on Thursday night. We were looking to photograph butterflies - and we found many. So today's photos are everything except butterflies - this is all good stuff!

Now it really feels like summer with work done!

File.Name: 8607b_8607-003.jpg
Notes:
This is the mature stage caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly crawling out of its previous instar caterpillar husk. The plant is non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family. I guess that's close enough to anise. The fennel is introduced, and a bad weed in the mountains, but these butterflies are helping to control it. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2008)

Yes, that large caterpillar really did fit into the shed skin behind. Think lycra! Look very close in the lower right and you can see that the old-stage caterpillar has sunk two claws into the plant stem so that the new-stage caterpillar can pull away free!

Also note that the mature caterpillar has three pairs of true legs up front, but it also has four pairs of "prolegs" in the back. The prolegs of this caterpillar have a neat black spot, and you can see the same pattern on the shed-skin instar behind!

File.Name: 8607c_8607-032.jpg
Notes:
Look close!

Here is a beautiful Dragonfly (order Odonata, suborder Anisoptera) hanging close to the ground from a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) shrub in the mountains. Maybe this is a Pacific Spiketail (Cordulegaster dorsalis dragonfly? It let me get close before it lost patience and took off. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2008)
ScientificName: Odonata; Anisoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Cordulegaster dorsalis
associatedTaxa: Odonata; Anisoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Cordulegaster dorsalis

File.Name: 8607d_8607-034.jpg
Notes:
Pretty little Beetle (order Coleoptera) on a furry leaf of the endemic native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon Traskiae) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) plant family growing in the mountains near home. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2008)

Getting these photos is like casting a net into the ocean and bringing in a fish. I know nothing about the creature except that it was here, today. The real challenge is to understand their lives.
ScientificName: Coleoptera) on a furry leaf of the endemic native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon Traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae

File.Name: 8607e_8607-044.jpg
Notes: Handsome little Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) hugging a grass stem in the woods and checking out the photographer. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2008)

File.Name: 8607f_8607-065.jpg
Notes:
New summer flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delpinum cardinale) in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family. They look like little fish swimming upstream! (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2008)

These native flowers are uncommon in our mountains, but the old saying for uncommon flowers is that "rare" means rarely common. There are many of these flowers starting to flower on the upper end of the Snyder trail, on the way to Knapp's Castle.

File.Name: 8608a_8607-078.jpg
Notes:
You can't really tell from this photo, but I believe this is a plain Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly in the woods. It's like a hairstreak butterfly without tails, working the new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family growing by our mailbox. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2008) (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2008)

This is butterfly season in the mountains, but the challenge is to find butterflies other than the ubiquitous Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies that are everywhere.
CommonNames: Brown Elfin; California Buckwheat; Buckwheat; Variable Checkerspot
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona
associatedTaxa: Callophrys augustinus; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona

File.Name: 8608b_8608-085.jpg
Notes:
It's a fact that many of the "Blues" - the butterflies in subfamily Polypmmatinae of the Gossamer-wing butterflies (family Lycaenidea) - don't show much blue color. At least they hide it on the upper surface of their wings, and keep their wings closed when they land to take nectar.

I captured light from this butterfly visiting flowers of Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) shrub in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family growing in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2008)
ScientificName: Polypmmatinae; Gossamer-wing; Lycaenidea; Rhamnus tomentella; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Polypmmatinae; Gossamer-wing; Lycaenidea; Rhamnus tomentella; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: 8608d_8608-099.jpg
Notes:
I thought this little butterfly was a Copper (sub-family Lycaeninae in the family Lycaenidae), but now I'm not sure. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2008)

Maybe this ia a Dotted Blue (Euphilotes enoptes) or Square-spotted Blue (E. battoides)? It's working new flowers of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) shrub in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family growing in the canyon.
CommonNames: Copper; Dotted Blue; Square-spotted Blue; Coffee Berry; Buckthorn
ScientificName: Lycaeninae; Lycaenidae; Euphilotes  enoptes; E. battoides; Rhamnus tomentella; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: 8608e_8608-105.jpg
Notes: Handsome little "cruciform" (cross-like) Plume Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Pterophoridae) working a composite flower of non-native Feverfew (Tanacetum parthenium) flower in the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2008)

File.Name: 8608f_8607-019.jpg
Notes: This is the male Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) on the left in my [Previous] photo after its mate disconnected and flew away. It looks forlorn. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2008)

File.Name: 8609a_8609-130.jpg
Notes: I guess this is a Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly working the little Blue Marguerite (Felicia amelloides) composite daisies in our garden today. The upper wings of the butterfly have more dark colors than I expect, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2008)

File.Name: 8609b_8609-124.jpg
Notes:
There are so many Variable Checkerspot Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies in the woods right now that it takes a moment to recognize something different. I'm pretty sure this is a Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly working the non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) flowers in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2008)

The Variable Checkerspots' antennae are bright orange, but this smaller butterfly only has orange antenna tips. It really is different.
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Chlosyne gabbii; Coreopsis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Chlosyne gabbii; Coreopsis; Asteraceae

File.Name: 8609c_8609-116.jpg
Notes: Tiny little Blue (genus Euphilotes) butterfly perched on the thistle-like flower head of non-native Tocalate ( Centaurea melitensis) that is invading our mountains. This little butterfly would fit wings-spread on my thumbnail. The dark wingspots are not iridescent, so maybe it's a Lupine Blue (Plebejus lupinus). (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2008)
ScientificName: Euphilotes; Centaurea melitensis) that is invading our mountains. This little butterfly would fit wings-spread on my thumbnail. The dark wingspots are not iridescent, so maybe it's a Lupine Blue (Plebejus lupinus

File.Name: 8609d_8609-114.jpg
Notes: Checkered White (Pontia protodice) butterfly working new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) near home. The caterpillar "host plants" of these butterflies are wild mustards in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family, but they feed on many different flowers like these composites. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2008)

File.Name: 8609e_8607-042.jpg
Notes: Of course I picked up the Giant Puffball (Calvatia gigantea) mushroom pushing up through dry ground shown in the [Previous] photo. It fell apart. The spore-mass inside looks like what collects from your vacuum cleaner and smells like a diaper pail! These mushrooms are said to be edible and delicious when fresh. No thanks. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2008)

File.Name: 8609f_8607-040.jpg
Notes: Softball-size Giant Puffball (Calvatia gigantea) mushroom pushing up through dry ground in the mountains near home. Of course you want to pick it up or kick it like a soccer ball! See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2008)

File.Name: 8610a_8609-108.jpg
Notes:
This is native Fringed Indian Pink (Silene laciniata ssp. major in the Pink (Caryophyllaceae) family) starting to flower in the canyon. The species name "laciniata" sounds like "lacerate" - irregularly slashed and jagged as if torn. Freddy Krueger comes to mind, but I'd rather think of Henri Matisse in his later years. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2008)

Sometimes I increase the color saturation of my photos, but for this native summer flower I had to bring it down!

I'm always busy with school stuff when spring wildflowers are at their peak. I try to make up for it by finding interesting summer flowers like this that are just starting to show their stuff in our summer-dry woods.

D'oh - I have to report for jury duty tomorrow morning. I hope it doesn't interfere with the Live Oak Music Festival this weekend - Party on, Treebeard!

File.Name: 8610b_8610-159.jpg
Notes: Strange flowers of Fish's Milkwort (Polygala cornuta var. fishiae). This is an uncommon native shrub with odd flowers and seeds. It's a singleton in it's own Milkwort (Polygalaceae) family in our mountains. I found these flowers growing on a shady hillside by the river today. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2008)

File.Name: 8610c_8610-150.jpg
Notes:
I believe this is native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family growing on of the dry wash of the Santa Ynez River. The plants have an intense peppermint smell. A few years ago there were hundreds of plants here, but today there are just a few, and no pollinators.

Every year is different, which means that nature has a history.
Locality: Lamiaceae) family growing on of the dry wash of the Santa Ynez River. The plants have an intense peppermint smell. A few years ago there were hundreds of plants here

File.Name: 8610d_8610-154.jpg
Notes: Beautiful summer flowers of Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family growing on dry ground along Paradise Road near the 2nd Crossing of the river today. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2008)

File.Name: 8610e_8606-118.jpg
Notes: Sticky Snapdragon (Antirrhinum multiflorum) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family growing near home. This is a native that grows in disturbed places in our mountains. It's a real snapdragon - you can squeeze the base of the flower and the jaws open! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2008)

File.Name: 8610f_8607-060.jpg
Notes: Pretty summer flowers of Yellow-throated Phacelia (Phacelia brachyloba) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. This plant is a "fire follower" growing in a place that was cleared as a firebreak last summer, but it never burned. This shows that some fire-follwer seeds only need sunlight and heat to flower where chaparral brush once stood. I also noticed seed heads of Fire Poppy (Papaver californicum) nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2008)

File.Name: 8611a_8608-080.jpg
Notes:
New summer flowers of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Honeysuckle (Caprifoliaceae) family catching a beam of sunlight in the shady north-slope woods. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2008)

I did my duty and was thankfully excused from jury duty today. Now it's time to get ready for the three-day Live Oak Music Festival starting Friday just down the road near Lake Cachuma in the Santa Ynez Valley!

Check out http://www.liveoakmusic.org/ to see what you're missing!

File.Name: 8611b_8608-101.jpg
Notes: Handsome new flower of California Wild Rose (Rosa californica) in the canyon. Look close in the upper-left and you might spot the pretty little dark red Weevil (family Curculionidae in the order Coleoptera) doing some damage to the petals. It got away when I tried to move in for a close shot. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2008)

File.Name: 8611c_8610-146.jpg
Notes:
New flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family growing on the dry side of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2008)

Look at the strange shape of the flower on the right. When a large Bumblebee (Bombus) or a Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa) lands on the front petal, it acts like a fulcrum to bring the stamens down onto its back to transfer pollen.

There is a little black bee in this photo above the center that has been collecting pollen right from the stamens. It's free-loading because it's not heavy enough to work the fulcrum.
CommonNames: White Sage; Mint; Bumblebee; Carpenter Bee

File.Name: 8611d_8608-059.jpg
Notes: Pretty flowers of native Whispering Bells (Emmenanthe penduliflora) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. It's a fire-follower growing in a place that was hand-cleared for a fire break last summer. Some of these fire-follower plants need the heat of real fire to germinate, but apparently this one just needs the heat of the sun after the chaparral brush cover is removed. The flowers are arranged in coiled "cymes" - new flowers keep opening as the coil unwinds - like my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 08 June 2008)

File.Name: 8611e_8606-126.jpg
Notes:
Exquisite summer flowers of native succulent Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Stonecrop (Crassulaceae) family. These flowers look like they were sculpted from wax. (San Marcos Pass, 08 June 2008)
Note how the flowers are arranged in a coiled "cyme" - new flowers keep opening as the coil unwinds.

File.Name: 8612a_8608-088.jpg
Notes:
I can always recognize this little mosquito-size native fly (order Diptera) in the mountains - maybe it's a Dance Fly in the family Empididae, but I really don't know. It's easy to recognize by its behavior. It flies up to a flower and hovers back and forth several times before landing, like a dog turning in circles before lying down. The flower in this photo is a native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 08 June 2008)

Our kids and friends are starting to assemble for the three-day Live Oak Music Festival starting tomorrow just down the road near Lake Cachuma in the Santa Ynez Valley. We already have out car in line to get our favorite camp site in the morning.

Check out http://www.liveoakmusic.org/ to see what you're missing!
CommonNames: fly; Dance Fly; Coffee Berry; Buckthorn

File.Name: 8612b_8601-085.jpg
Notes: Furry little Bumblebee (family Bombus, order Hymenoptera) working new summer flowers of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae family in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 07 June 2008)

File.Name: 8612c_8607-012.jpg
Notes: I've been chasing the butterflies in our woods, but there are also many diurnal (day-flying) Moths in the same order Lepidoptera. Here's a pretty little unknown (to me) day-tripper moth working flowers of native composite Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) near home. (San Marcos Pass, 07 June 2008)

File.Name: 8701a_8620-187.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly ovipositing - laying her eggs - on new flowers of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family growing near home. You can see a clutch of new golden butterfly eggs below her abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2008)

I didn't fall off the edge of the earth, but we did lose our internet connection for two weeks, and that's pretty much the same thing. Maybe you remember the old Lily Tomlin TV sketch from Laugh-In and Saturday Night Live: "We don't care, we don't have to... we're the phone company." That's how the last two weeks have been. We've switched to a new DSL provider that is not #$@!%Verizon. It's still not working, but we're getting close, and at least my dial-up is working again.

I haven't been posting for two weeks, but I have been taking photos every day with my new Nikon D300 camera. I'll catch up when I can.

File.Name: 8704a_8704-320.jpg
Notes:
Who needs fireworks to celebrate Independence Day with an airshow like this? This is one of the fire bombers dropping a load of phosphate fire retardant on the "Gap Wildfire" not much more than a mile from home. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2008)

I've been taking lots of photos - especially of the airplanes - but it's been hard to upload them because we lose our power every evening. More tomorrow, I hope.

File.Name: 8704b_8703-280.jpg
Notes:
There's a wildfire in the mountains.

This is the view looking south (towards the ocean and the cityof Goleta) from West Camino Cielo road looking across the canyon and the highway towards East Camino Cielo. For scale, note the two-story white house at the bottom of the photo. The flames can't be much more than a mile from home as the embers fly. I don't think we've been officially evacuated yet, though the communities of West Camino Cielo and Painted Cave have been.

So far, the fire has burned 6,000+ acres stretching along the ridge as far as Lizard Mouth. The good news is that it hasn't crossed over the summit yet We're watching the flames very close. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2008)

File.Name: 8705a_8705-009.jpg
Notes:
There are fire bombers in the air, but there is also new life in the woods. I took a walk today.

I found this beautiful little humpbacked mosquito-size fly (order Diptera) working new summer flowers of native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. I guess this is a Dance Fly in the family Empididae, but I'm really not sure. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2008)

Julie calls it the Kokopelli fly! (Look it up if you don't know.)

The Gap Wildfire is threatening our home again tonight, but life does go on in the canyon. The wind shifted today, and the fire is moving slowly towards us in the mountains. We're watching close.
Locality:
Look it up if you don't know.)

The Gap Wildfire is threatening our home again tonight
CommonNames: fly; Fleabane Daisy; Composite; Dance Fly

File.Name: 8705b_8703-217.jpg
Notes:
Three huge double-rotor "Chinook" type helicopters doing a water drop on the Gap Fire that is threatening our home. I got this photo from a couple of miles away using my 500 mm telephoto lens and cropping deep. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2008)

I thought of Apocalypse Now, and I could almost hear "Ride of Valkyries" from their speakers!

File.Name: 8705c_8703-236.jpg
Notes:
There have been three (or four?) of these huge double-rotor "Chinook" type helicopters doing water drops on the Gap Fire that is threatening our home. Look close at the bottom-left of the photo to spot the fire crew who must be glad for the precision drop. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2008)

My first thought is that the bucket is pretty small for such a large chopper. Someone at our usual look-out point said these are U.S. Marine helicopters. I have also seen huge U.S. Airforce C-130 Hercules cargo planes dropping fire retardant. They rattle the windows as they circle over.

File.Name: 8705d_8704-345.jpg
Notes:
There have been many different air tankers - aka fire bombers - dropping fire retardant on the Gap Fire that is threatening our house... but this is something different!

This is "Tanker 910" - a converted DC-10 wide-body jet air tanker that can drop 12,000 gallons (45,600 liters) of fire retardant in 8 seconds. I was hoping it would drop its load on the fire near home, but instead it dumped on the distant western end of the wildfire. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2008)

There's some info about Tanker 910 at Wikipedia:
   http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tanker_910

I can't show anything familiar in this photo for a sense of scale, except that this plane is about FIVE MILES away from my camera with 500 mm telephoto lens, and then I deeply cropped!

File.Name: 8705e_8703-215.jpg
Notes:
Sun to the right and black smoke behind - air tanker 48 drops its load of red fire retardant on the Gap Fire just a mile or so across the canyon from my home. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2008)

I've been taking lots of photos of the air tankers - aka fire bombers - working this wildfire that is closing in on us. Now I know them all by number and recognize them as they circle over our house to drop their loads. This plane is one of my favorites with that yellow color and clear nose cone in front. I believe it is a converted Lockheed P-2H Neptune. I've also seen numbers 12 and 45 working our fire which appear to be the same plane.

There should be a fotolog group for fire bomber photos. (Maybe there already is?) I'll bet number 48 - and all the rest - have been fighting wildfires all across the country. I wonder if individual pilots go with their planes?

File.Name: 8706a_8706-032.jpg
Notes:
Remarkable Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) working new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. I got this photo (and the rest) along East Camino Cielo - away from the fire - at the summit near the FAA radio tower between the pass and Arroyo Burro saddle. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 6 July 2008)

These furry lepidoptera really are daytime moths with clear wings. They remind me more of hummingbirds than butterflies.

Gap Wildfire update:

The Gap Fire that has been threatening our home looks much better tonight on our eastern end of the fire. Thanks to an amazing effort by the ground crews and air support, no houses have been lost, including that white house at the bottom of my photo here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/37369803

But I don't want to jinx it by being too optimistic. The wildfire is still active on the western end near Lizard Mouth. If it breaches the summit, it could burn down the upper end of San Jose Creek back towards us. We're watching.
CommonNames: Snowberry Clearwing Moth; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Mint
ScientificName: Hemaris diffinis; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Hemaris diffinis; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; lepidoptera

File.Name: 8706b_8706-036.jpg
Notes:
A furry mandala with bees!

Remarkable native summer flower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Lily (Lilium) family. I've also heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily". (Santa Ynez Mountains, 6 July 2008)

There's nothing weedy about this flower. It's remarkable for its beauty and for being a real lily flowering in our summer-dry mountains when most flowers are done.

The bees are interesting. I think I see six in this photo, and every flower had them. See my [Previous] photo.
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Lilium
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Lilium

File.Name: 8706c_8706-058.jpg
Notes: This is a close shot of one of the Wild Bee (family Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) on a new summer flower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Lily (Lilium) family. The bee is working one of the nectar glands at the base of the petals. I don't recall seeing this kind of bee anywhere else. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 6 July 2008)

File.Name: 8706d_8706-027.jpg
Notes:
Small but mighty Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera) perched on a new summer flower of native Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Lily (Lilium) family. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 6 July 2008)

These little preditors can bring down prey much larger than they are. See my [Previous] two photos for an example.

I believe these bugs are color-shifters, though I haven't seen this mentioned in my books. This critter has definate yellow tints that match the yellow hairs of the flower. Compare the critter in my [Previous] photo - if you can find it! - that is almost pure white to match the buckwheat flowers.

File.Name: 8706f_8706-064.jpg
Notes:
This is the first Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti - on flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family growing by our mailbox. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2008)

I chased the butterfly around the flowers for a while trying to get a photo when it suddenly went flat like this and stopped moving except for an occasional twitch. I knew something was wrong. See my next two photos.

Notes:
Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly visiting a Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2008)

I know what this butterfly has been doing! Note the powdery orange pollen on it's wing. This butterfly has been visiting native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) flowers in the canyon, but I don't know where.

See my photo at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/7999731

ALL the Humboldt Tiger Lilies I know of in the canyon have been parasitized this year and have no flowers. I guess I have to explore further down the canyon to find the flowers!

File.Name: 8707b_8707-090.jpg
Notes:
I believe this is a little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working a new summer flower of composite Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2008)

The "blues" have been scarce this year - that's the butterflies, not the mood. Wildfire and no internet have been good cause for the blues!

File.Name: 8707c_8703-153.jpg
Notes: Male Checkered White (Pontia protodice) butterfly working new flowers of White Sweet Clover (Melilotus albus) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family in the canyon. This plant is not native, but at least the butterflies like it. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2008)
CommonNames: Checkered White; White Sweet Clover; Pea

File.Name: 8707d_8701-110.jpg
Notes:
I guess this is a Punctate Blister Beetle (Epicauta puncticollis) in the family Meloidae of the insect order Coleoptera. I've also seen these beetles on flowers of native Tarweed (Hemizonia) and Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) - all in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. Note the short wing covers and protruding abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2008)

These beetles have interesting natural history and cultural history. The larvae go through more larval instars than most insects. They look for egg cases of grasshoppers (Orthoptera) as a host, and then go through further stages before becoming adults. I don't understand the details.

I've read that these beetles are the source of the original "Spanish Fly" - the stuff of legend when I was in high school in the 1960's. Some of these blister beetles (probably not this one) can secrete defensive fluids containing the chemical cantharidin that causes irritation and blistering of human skin. Sounds horrible.
CommonNames: Punctate Blister Beetle; Tarweed; Fleabane Daisy; Composite
ScientificName: Epicauta puncticollis; Meloidae; Coleoptera; Hemizonia; Erigeron foliosus; Asteraceae; Orthoptera; cantharidin

Note the interesting Wild Bees (family Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) that I always find on the flowers. I'm not sure if they are social bees, but they seem to be socializing.

File.Name: 8708a_8708-103.jpg
Notes:
Impressive Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera holding its Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) prey. It was hiding in a shadow, so I over-exposed by two stops. I like the white background - like a hospital room, or the end of the movie 2001. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2008)

Maybe this is the Bee Assassin - Apiomerus spissipes?

The rest of today's photos are the photos I meant to post yesterday - before we lost our power for the night. The good news is that we got our DSL internet connection working again today. Wahoo!

Gap Wildfire update:

The Gap Fire that has been threatening our home looks quiet tonight on our eastern end of the fire. The western end of the fire is still burning beyond Lizard's Mouth, over the ridge away from us. I don't want to jinx it, but it looks good. I have no doubt that the fire crews and air support saved our house. Thanks!

File.Name: 8709a_8708-107.jpg
Notes:
Cut little tan-colored Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) hugging a stem of non-native Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. I got this photo (and the [Previous] two) on the high divide between San Marcos Pass and Arroyo Burro, a few miles from home. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 July 2008)

I'm worried about the shrub Spanish Broom that is becoming invasive in our mountains. It mostly grows along roads, but it follows the roads far into the mountains. I hope this grasshopper develops a taste for it!

File.Name: 8709b_8625-160.jpg
Notes:
I noticed this handsome Robber Fly (family Asilidae in the order Diptera) perched on a catkin of Tanbark Oak (Lithocarpus densiflorus in the Fagaceae family) a few feet from the acorn in my [Previous] photo.

These flies are preditors that catch their insect prey in the air like little dragonflies. I don't have many photos of these flies because they don't visit flowers to feed. It's sheer luck to find one perched in the woods like this. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 July 2008)

File.Name: 8709c_8708-110.jpg
Notes:
Fat acorn of native Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus). These beautiful not-quite-real-oak-trees in the Oak (Fagaceae) family have erect catkins and Chestnut-like bristles on the acorn cups. I got this photo (and the next) on the high divide between San Marcos Pass and Arroyo Burro, a few miles from home. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 July 2008)

The acorns take two years to mature, so I guess the big acorn started last year, and the little rosette of bristles to the right of it is this year's acorn that will mature next year.

File.Name: 8709d_8709-126.jpg
Notes: Some kind of Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Homoptera) perched on a twig of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon. These woodland cicada don't sing, though they make a clicking noise by rubbing their wings together. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2008)
ScientificName: Cicadidae; Homoptera) perched on a twig of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Cicadidae; Homoptera) perched on a twig of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 8709e_8709-140.jpg
Notes: Mating pair of Buprestid beetles (family Buprestidae in the order Coleoptera finding no privacy on a leaf of Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the canyon. Usually I find these little beetles munching on flower pollen, but it sure looks like these beetles have been munching on the leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2008)

File.Name: 8709f_8706-073.jpg
Notes:
Large California Prionus (Prionus californicus) beetle in the Cerambycidae family of the Coleoptera. I got this photo on the screen door at night with flash. The screen mesh is 1/16 inch (~ 2 mm), so the beetle is about 2 inches (50 mm) long. It was gone in the morning. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2008)

The large white grubs live in dead oak logs. I sometimes find them while splitting firewood.

File.Name: 8710a_8710-166.jpg
Notes:
Oak galls are started by tiny wasps as living homes for their larvae, but the larvae are also prey to other tiny wasps as a living home for their larvae!

There's a lot happening in this photo. The shrub is a native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the family Fagaceae) in the woods today. The strange reddish growths on the leaf are galls of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii aka Cynips douglasii) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2008)

But the tiny Wasp (order Hymenoptera) visiting the gall is something else. Note the long ovipositor that looks like it's made to probe inside the gall for the larva inside. It's a preditor!

Gall wasps insert their eggs and somehow re-program the plant to produce the abnormal gall growth that nourishes the larvae within. These wasps also have a complicated life cycle with alternating bisexual and unisexual generations forming different kinds of galls on different parts of the same plants. Nature is like that!
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Spined Turban Gall Wasp; Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Antron douglasii; Cynips douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Antron douglasii; Cynips douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 8710b_8707-095.jpg
Notes:
Strange fruit hanging from an oak leaf in the woods!

I believe this is a mature cluster of wasp galls of the tiny Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus chrystallinus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera in the summer-dry woods today. The oak is native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the family Fagaceae). The wasps are specific to this particular species of oak. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2008)

Look at my [Previous] two photos and you can see the stages of this strange fruit, beginning with single wasp galls that contain one egg and larva. The galls grow together to make a different kind of social nest. I wonder what the benefits of being together are for these tiny wasps?
ScientificName: Andricus chrystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus chrystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: 8710c_8710-186.jpg
Notes:
This looks like something underwater at some tropical coral reef, but here are wasp galls of the tiny Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus chrystallinus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera in the summer-dry woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2008)

I found these galls on the underside of a leaf of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the family Fagaceae). You don't have to travel far to find "strange nature" - it's just out the door if you look close!

I believe these are the same kind of wasp galls as my [Previous] and [Next] photos.
ScientificName: Andricus chrystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus chrystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: 8710d_8710-184.jpg
Notes:
Little wasp galls on the underside of a leaf of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the family Fagaceae) in the woods today. The closer you look, the stranger they are! (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2008)

I believe these are new galls of the tiny Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus chrystallinus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. See my [Next] photos.

These are the galls of the summer unisexual generation that is all female and fertile. They will emerge and make different galls in spring that produce both male and female wasps that will mate and produce more galls like these next summer.
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus chrystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus chrystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 8710e_8707-098.jpg
Notes:
I believe this is the "Pinched" form of a "Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp" on a leaf of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia - Fagaceae) in the woods today. I'm sure the gall is made by a Cynipid Wasp in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera). the gall is described in my book - Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls of California and other Western States" (2006) - but it apparently has not yet been scientifically described and named. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2008)

Sounds like a great science fair project!
CommonNames: Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Cynipid Wasp

File.Name: 8710f_8710-153.jpg
Notes:
I believe this is the gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera) on a twig of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia - Fagaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2008)

I apoligize for all the Latin names, but insect galls really are that complicated. They are intimate relations between insect and plant, and species must be just right.

It was breezy today, so I looked close on the Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the family Fagaceae) shrubs that weren't swaying so much in the wind. I found many surprising new galls.
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Scrub Oak

File.Name: 8711a_8710-172.jpg
Notes:
I managed to capture the two worst pests in our mountains in one photo - but at a cost of blood! I'm trying to hold my left arm steady in front of my camera with macro lens on my tripod as the fly digs in. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2008)

I believe the larger gray fly on top is a Biting Snipe Fly (order Diptera, family Rhagionidae, maybe genus Symphoromyia). It has a painful bite that you notice. I believe they make an incision to draw blood and then slurp it up - kind of like vampire bats. You can see a drop of my blood between its legs in this photo from another bite while I fiddled with my camera.

We also have a different Biting Snipe Fly in our mountains, though they haven't been bad (yet) this year. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/10256086

I believe that other little fly slurping the blood drop is what I call an "Oak Gnat" - maybe Amiota picta in the family Drosophilidae. I can swat a biting fly, but these little flies drive me nuts as they fly around my face never landing. I call these the worst pests in our summer mountains.

File.Name: 8711b_8701-089.jpg
Notes:
This clown-colored caterpillar of a Western Tussock Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Lymantriidae, maybe Orgyia vetusta or kin) has been caught in a spider web. It wasn't struggling, so I guess the spider did it in. I guess the spider is a Cobweb Spider aka Combfooted Spider (Theridiidae). A day later, the spider and caterpillar were both gone. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2008)

I was more interested in the little black insect visible in the upper-right climbing down a spider web. I first thought it was a little beetle, but see my [Previous] photo to learn the truth.
CommonNames: Western Tussock Moth; Cobweb Spider; Combfooted Spider

File.Name: 8711c_8701-093.jpg
Notes:
The tiny black insect in my [Next] photo is a "True Bug" (order Hemiptera). You can't see it in this photo, but it has a stout proboscis like an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family.

What's most amazing is the insect's antennae that are thickened and dark at the base. Could it be that this is an adaptation to disguise its antennae as extra legs to look (or feel?) like an 8 legged spider? These little bugs were perfectly at home on the spider's web, and the spider didn't seem to notice as they crawled back and forth towards the dead caterpillar in the web. This could be amazing camouflage - but do spiders really count legs? So many questions...

See my [Next] photo to see the caterpillar. Interesting how the caterpillar's tail hairs look like feathers this close. I've been taking photos in our woods for many years, and nature is still full of surprises!
Locality: or feel?) like an 8 legged spider?  These little bugs were perfectly at home on the spider's web

File.Name: 8711d_8619-170.jpg
Notes:
Sad story - but I guess it's good for the spider!

This mature-stage caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly has just crawled out of its previous instar caterpillar husk into the web of a spider. On the third segment up from the bottom, I can see an unnatural green patch that may be where the spider first got it. I guess the spider is a Cobweb Spider aka Combfooted Spider (Theridiidae). A day later, the spider and caterpillar were gone. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2008)

The cartoonist B. Kliban once gave good advice - never eat anything bigger than your head! I guess this spider didn't get the lesson. And I wonder - if newly emerged caterpillars are that vulnerable, why don't spiders get more of them?
CommonNames: Anise Swallowtail; Cobweb Spider; Combfooted Spider

File.Name: 8711e_8627-217.jpg
Notes:
This is the same kind of honeybee-size fly as my [Previous] photo, though the iridescent eyes look blue rather than green. I caught this fly in the air hoolding steady in one place. Look how its legs are arranged like it's body surfing the air waves! (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2008)

See my [Previous] photo. My first thought was that this is a Deer Fly (order Diptera, family Tabanidae). But I've also seen Soldier Flies in the Stratiomyidae family hover like this.

That's my guess. See my photo at: http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/10372501

File.Name: 8711f_8625-147.jpg
Notes:
With those bright green iridescent eyes that grow together on its face, I figured this honeybee-sized fly is a Deer Fly (order Diptera, family Tabanidae). It's working new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2008)

Now I'm not so sure. I've seen many of these flies in the woods, but they have never shown any interest in biting me like deer flies do, and look at my [Next] photo of the same kind of fly hovering in the air like a Soldier Fly in the Stratiomyidae family. This photo shows the wing venation clearly. When I find some time, I'll try to figure it out. I should know this large beautiful fly.
