File.Name: 8712a_8709-135.jpg
Notes:
Tiny Wasp (order Hymenoptera) on an immature fruit of a Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) shrub in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family in the canyon. There are many of these tiny wasps - only 2 or 3 mm long - on the fruit. Some of them appear to be ovipositing - laying eggs - though they have a very short ovipositor that can't go very deep. I don't know what they are or what they're doing, but here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2008)

No time for photos today - I had an event to attend. But I have many good photos from the "missing weeks" in June when our internet was down!

File.Name: 8712b_8709-137.jpg
Notes:
Strange gall on a fruiting branch of a Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) shrub in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. Given the location of this gall among normal fruit, I have to assume it's a galled flower of fruit. Some of the shrubs have dozens of these foliose galls, and others have none. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2008)

Galls are abnormal plant growth induced by insects or other critters. They are a kind of practical genetic engineering worth study. It's remarkable that each insect (etc.) makes a unique gall, and the galls also show traits of the host plant, like the tiny barbs on the end of these bracts.

This gall is not in my book - Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls of California and other Western States" (2006) - but maybe it is made by a Ceanothus Gall Midge (Asphondylia ceunothi in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera). It could also be something new.

I get to walk the woods and take photos during summer, but when school starts in the fall I'm back to work as science teacher at Dunn Middle School. In the spring, we have our annual science fair, and this year I'm collecting photos to make a database of plant galls. Expect many more!
Locality:
Asphondylia ceunothi in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera). It could also be something new.

I get to walk the woods and take photos during summer
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Buckthorn; Ceanothus Gall Midge
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Asphondylia ceunothi; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Asphondylia ceunothi; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: 8712c_8629-048.jpg
Notes:
These strange red growths on the leaves of Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family are insect galls - abnormal plant growth induced by insects or other critters.

These galls are caused by an aphid rather than a wasp. They are caused by the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the order Homoptera). If you peel one of the galls open, you can see the tiny aphids inside. There are also other aphids on the leaves, but they are probably something else. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2008)

What's strange is that I didn't find any of these aphid galls last year during our record drought summer. That was the first year I haven't seen them. I'm glad they're back just to show that the woods aren't broken!
CommonNames: Big-berry Manzanita; Heath; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera

File.Name: 8712d_8626-199.jpg
Notes:
The orange flower on the right is a native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Diplacus longiflorus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. They are common in the mountains.

What's amazing is that golden bee-like fly (order Diptera) with the huge proboscis in the air above! I'm sure this is a Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the family Acroceridae. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2008)

I want to know more about these uncommon flies. My books say the larvae are internal parasites of burrowing spiders. I usually hear their high-pitched buzzing sound before I see them. They are one of the most difficult local insects to photograph. They leave one flower and fly around randomly before digging into another. I have many photos of their butts sticking out of the flowers, but very few of the whole insect. I'll keep trying.

I've also seen these flies working flowers of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum), a native shrub in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family.
CommonNames:
Bush Monkey Flower; Figwort; fly; Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the family Acroceridae. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2008)

File.Name: 8712e_8623-076.jpg
Notes:
Handsome Robber Fly (family Asilidae in the order Diptera) perched on a summer frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) near home. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2008)

Look at those abs on its mighty thorax! These flies are preditors that catch their insect prey in the air like little dragonflies. Note the bristles on its legs to hold their prey!

I don't have many photos of these flies because they don't visit flowers to feed - and hold still for a photo. It's sheer luck (and good eyes) to find one perched in the woods like this.

File.Name: 8712f_8706-070.jpg
Notes:
Here's another insect critter that landed on my hand and posed for a photo. I'm sure this is a Click Beetle (family Elateridae in the order Coleoptera. These beetles can suddenly *snap* with an audible "click" sound that sure startles an intruder (like me). This one is such a deep blue/indigo that it's almost black. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2008)

That's my gold wedding ring in the background.

File.Name: 8713a_8713-012.jpg
Notes:
This is just another unknown fly in the order Diptera. I won't try to guess what it is. I found it perched on a folded dead leaf in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2008)

The arrangement of bristles and hairs on this little fly is a mark of real beauty where we least expect it. Nature is like that.

File.Name: 8713b_8713-002.jpg
Notes: Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly picking up some color from a brilliant Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) flower in the garden this morning - like a campfire glow! (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2008)

File.Name: 8713c_8713-017.jpg
Notes:
This is a Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) butterfly perched on a leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2008)

This is said to be our most common chaparral hairstreak, though this is my first photo. I've collected photos of 50+ species of butterflies near home on San Marcos Pass - so far!

File.Name: 8713d_8713-023.jpg
Notes: Pretty little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly working flowers of White Sweet Clover (Melilotus albus) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family along the road in the canyon. These flowers are not native, but at least the butterflies like them. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2008)

File.Name: 8713e_8623-092.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly working a new flower of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2008)

It's a butterfly secret - most "Blue" butterflies are mostly not blue!

File.Name: 8713f_8703-146.jpg
Notes:
These caterpillars are kin, but probably from different parent butterflies of Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon). The host plant is non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family. It's a bad invasive weed, but we always leave a few for the butterflies and try to remember to cut the flowers before they make seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2008)

The little caterpillar on the left is the "bird turd" stage which molts into the "big brother" form on the right. It sure looks like Big Brother is watching out for its little bro', but those black "eyes" are just painted on. I don't think it sees - or cares - much.

File.Name: 8714a_8714-067.jpg
Notes:
This photo is remarkable for several reasons.

That "wasp" is really a remarkable Clearwinged Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Sesiidae) working an acorn of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the family Fagaceae)

It's also remarkable that the black acorn is skewed off-center. Compare the normal acorn in the background.

Why would any moth or wasp visit an acorn? This black acorn has been galled by some critter that causes the acorn cup to produce a sweet honeydew exudate under enough pressure to push the galled acorn out of its cup to the ground.

I did my duty as a naturalist and tasted the exudate - it is sweet and sticky like honey or molasses. See my [Previous] photos. I saw a few bees vising the acorns, but I'm puzzled that I saw no ants.

My reference book - Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls of California and other Western States" (2006) mentions a Mottled Acorn Gall (Callirhtiss carmelensis) on "Black Oaks" - like Coast Live Oak - but this must be something different on this "White Oak" scrub oak.
Locality:
Quercus berberidifolia in the family Fagaceae)

It's also remarkable that the black acorn is skewed off-center. Compare the normal acorn in the background.

File.Name: 8714b_8714-074.jpg
Notes:
This is a close shot of the remarkable Clearwinged Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Sesiidae) working a sweet-galled acorn of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the family Fagaceae) in my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2008)

This moth is a perfect wasp-mimic, but look close and you can see that it really is in the order Lepidoptera with the other moths and butterflies. Nature is strange like that.
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Sesiidae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Sesiidae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: 8714c_8714-044.jpg
Notes:
Two normal acorns on the right and two galled acorns on the left. The plant is native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the family Fagaceae) growing near home.

The froth shows that the galled acorns are under pressure that is pushing out the froth - and will push out the galled acorns. See my [Previous] photos.

I did my duty as a naturalist and tasted it - the froth is sweet and sticky like honey or molasses. Honey bees and other insects were working the acorns. See my [Next] photos.

My book - Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls of California and other Western States" (2006) mentions a Mottled Acorn Gall (Callirhtiss carmelensis) on Black Oaks, but this must be something else on this White Oak scrub oak.

File.Name: 8714d_8714-050.jpg
Notes:
These young acorns of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the family Fagaceae) have the gall that pushes the acorns out from the cup. See my [Next] and [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2008)

My guess is that that the acorn is the gall, but the action is in the acorn cup that produces a sweet honey exudate under pressure that pushes the acorn/gall out of the cap to fall on the ground.

File.Name: 8714e_8714-052.jpg
Notes:
This photo will make more sense if you start with my first photo today and work backwards. It's a series and this is the end.

This native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the family Fagaceae) has pushed its acorn/gall out, but it's still producing the sweet exudate that pushed the acorn out of its cup.

Scrub Oak honey? Really, I did my duty as a naturalist and tasted it - it is sweet and sticky like honey or molasses.
Locality: Quercus berberidifolia in the family Fagaceae) has pushed its acorn/gall out

File.Name: 8714f_8714-083.jpg
Notes:
I'm not sure if this is a gall, or a scale insect, or some pupa growing in the "armpit" of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon. Whatever it is, the ants are interested and protective - I reckon they're harvesting sweet honeydew, though I can't see it. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2008)

Flowers and insects are beautiful by themselves, but I'm fascinated by the interactions between them. There are the simple 2-way interactions like bee pollinates flower, but it gets more interesting with 3-way interactions like wasp makes gall on plant and ants tend it. When you consider the "inquiline" critters that share the gall and the "parasitoid" critters that prey on the life within, nature becomes strange and more complicated than we can imagine.

I reckon these are the natural relations that are at risk as the natural world gets simpler with disturbance and development. We won't even know what we're missing!

File.Name: 8718a_8718-160.jpg
Notes:
Handsome Beetle (order Coleoptera) in my hand today. I found it on a plant of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae family. Is that a scary face on its back? (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2008)

I had a little stroke on Tuesday afternoon. They let me out of the hospital yesterday afternoon. I kind of forgot how to talk, though I expect it to come back with some help by the speech therapists. Fortunately I didn't forget how to take photos - though it taking longer than usual to write comments. And fortunately I didn't forget my sense of humor!

File.Name: 8722c_8722-081.jpg

Beautiful little humpbacked mosquito-size flies (order Diptera) working summer flowers of native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. Several friends have asked how it's going after my stroke two weeks ago. I'm feel fine, though I get tired every day about now. My speech is improving slowly, though my writing is better. Doing these fotolog photos every day is my best therapy!

File.Name: 8731b_8731-140.jpg
Notes:
Pretty little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly working flowers of native Common Aster (Aster chilensis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in the garden. The plant is a California native despite the foreign-sounding species name. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2008)

It's an interesting story. The plant was first collected by the Bohemian (Czech) naturalist Tade·ö Haenke - aka Tadeo or Thaddeus Haenke - who served as the botanist on a Spanish naval scientific voyage under the command of Captain Alejandro Malaspina that sailed up the Pacific coast of the Americas from Santiago de Chile to Alaska between 1790 and 1792. Haenke collected thousands of plant specimens on this voyage - including Redwood (Sequoia sempervirens). He never made it back to Europe - he died in Bolivia in 1817 - but his plant collections did make it back to Spain for study.

Haenke's herbarium specimen of this aster was finally named "Aster chilensis" because it was thought to come from Chile. According to the "The International Code of Botanical Nomenclature, The St. Louis Code of 1999" the earliest name takes precedence. And so the "Chilean Aster" is a California native. It's a mistake we must live with!

I took a drive today up the potholed East Camino Cielo road to the summits of our mountains to see what was happening - it turns out that lots is happening!

File.Name: 8804a_8804-081.jpg
Notes:
There a name for every one of those veins on this flies wings, and a name for every enlosed space! The way the R1 and Sc are close together and the open anal cell tell me that this is a Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera). Syrphids have a closed anal cell and a "spurious" vein crossing the r-m vein between R4+5 and M1+2.

Just kidding - or almost kidding! This is a pretty little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) in the garden today. After a while you just know what they are. (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2008)

File.Name: 8804b_8802-001.jpg

Can you see the spider? The unfortunate little wasp didn't, and this is what happened!

It a life-and-death struggle between the little Wasp (order Hymenoptera) and a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that's mostly hidden in the Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. The spider won. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2008)

File.Name: 8807a_8807-115.jpg
Notes:
A male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working flowers of summer flower of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family - it's showing the "dog face"! (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2008)

Showing the "dog face" must be the holy grail of local butterfly photographers, since the butterflies always close their wings when they land on a flower. Even my butterfly books fudge this with a museum specimen.

It's a beautiful butterfly, with a brilliant iridescent violet sheen that is only revealed in the air when the butterfly takes wing. This is the official State Butterfly of California, but I reckon only lepidopterists know why!
CommonNames: California Dogface; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Mint

File.Name: 8807b_8807-116.jpg
Notes: A male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working flowers of summer flower of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. It's showing the "dog face"! (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2008)
CommonNames: California Dogface; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Mint

File.Name: 8807c_8807-123.jpg
Notes: I guess this is another Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly, but it's perched by a different plant. This little blue is near native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family, whereas yesterday's is near California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family. The butterflies look almost identical, but this is the true Acmon Blue that uses legumes whereas the Lupine Blue (P. lupinus) (oddly enough) prefers buckwheat. Bah! I call them both Acnom Blues. I don't think they're distinct. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2008)
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; Deerweed; Pea; California Buckwheat; Buckwheat; Lupine Blue
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; P. lupinus

File.Name: 8808a_8701-102.jpg
Notes:
I found this dead Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis or kin in the order Hymenoptera) in the garden yesterday. I didn't think much about it - they just seem to just wear themselves out. Then I looked closer at the photo and had one of those "Ah hah" moments, like in the movie Blow Up.

Do you see little white things that look like eggs? They're actually Mites (Acari). I did a blow up of one mite and you can clearly see legs and even tiny eye spots. The mites killed the bee! I hope this isn't the start of a problem. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2008)

File.Name: 8808b_8807-102.jpg
Notes: Little humpbacked fly (order Diptera) working a new summer flower of the non-native Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. This mosquito-size fly may be a Dance Fly in the family Empididae, but I'm really not sure. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2008)

File.Name: 8808c_8807-125.jpg
Notes: This is a tiny Mugwort Weevil - at least that what it should be called. It a tiny weevil in the family Curculionidae in the order Coleoptera that occurs on leaves of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae family. What a strange elephant-like face! (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2008)

File.Name: 8809a_8809-010.jpg
Notes: The California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly is a remarkable butterfly even with wings closed. This is a male butterfly working summer flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2008)
CommonNames: California Dogface; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Mint

File.Name: 8809b_8809-017.jpg
Notes: Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly perched in the summer-dry woods. It looks like a moth when it flies over the dry grasses in the canyon, but it is revealed as a butterfly when it finally comes to rest. These butterflies have California summer colors - golden dry grass and fallen leaves - they are part of the "golden rolling hills of California" that Kate Wolf sang about so eloquently. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2008)

File.Name: 8809c_8809-014.jpg
Notes:
A pretty Grass-Skipper (Hesperia) butterfly working a wildflower of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family flowering in the canyon. With the long-exterted stamens, the flower seems to be expecting a much larger insect. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2008)

I know it's not quite sharp, but it's a nice shot anyway.

File.Name: 8810a_8810-055.jpg
Notes:
A pretty Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti - working flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Santa Ynez River, 10 August 2008)

I went to Paradise today - the section of the Santa Ynez River known as Paradise. It's only a few miles from home as the condor flies. The late summer attraction is the Scale Broom which is just starting to flower of the dry river bed. This shrub in the Composite (Asteraceae) family is a magnet for pollinators like these little butterflies. Unfortunately, it's always windy, even early in the morning.

File.Name: 8810b_8810-035.jpg
Notes: Pretty Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working the new summer flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. These butterflies rub their hindwings slowly together as they feed. (Santa Ynez River, 10 August 2008)

File.Name: 8810c_8810-050.jpg
Notes: I'm pretty sure this is a little Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly. It's a very plain butterfly, though it has a powdery coat of many colors that is rather pretty. Note the two little "tails" at the angle of the hindwing - it's a little hairstreak. working the new summer flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Santa Ynez River, 10 August 2008)

File.Name: 8811a_8810-041.jpg
Notes:
This beautiful Vespid Wasp (family Vespidae , order Hymenoptera) has desert colors, which suits its choice of habitat on the dry wash of the river. Here it is working new summer flowers of a native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub. Other than its taste for these flowers, I don't know anything about these pretty wasps. (Santa Ynez River, 10 August 2008)

It's an interesting paradox that our wettest habitat - the river bed - also becomes our driest habitat in late summer. The late-summer flowering plants do very well and become a magnet for pollinators like butterflies and these wasps.

File.Name: 8811b_8811-064.jpg
Notes: This pretty Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly is working flowers of composite Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum in the Asteraceae) in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2008)

File.Name: 8811c_8810-060.jpg
Notes: A pretty Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti - with wings spread on flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Santa Ynez River, 10 August 2008)

File.Name: 8812a_8812-071.jpg
Notes:
Like natural stained glass that flies! This is our "other" swallowtail butterfly - an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon), working flowers of the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden this morning. (It's flowering again after a pruning.) (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2008)

These butterflies are distinctive because they are a "black swallowtail - the black band runs along the front wing instead of across the wing like our other swallowtails.

I've been expecting these swallowtails for some time as it was a good year for caterpillars. See my photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/37785820
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/36221893
among many others. This is the first adult I've seen the year.

File.Name: 8813a_8813-119.jpg
Notes: Pretty Orange Sulfur (Colias eurytheme) butterfly with diaphanous wings showing the dark border on the inside of the wings. These are also known as Alfalfa Butterflies because they sometimes occur in great numbers in the fields. I rarely see them in the mountains. Maybe it got lost? (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2008)

File.Name: 8813b_8813-139.jpg
Notes: Humble Cabbage White (Pieris rapae working non-native Catnip (Nepeta cataria) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. It gets its name for wild mustards that it prefers for its host plant, but its not so picky with its food plants. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2008)

File.Name: 8813c_8812-078.jpg
Notes: Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. Some years these butterflies are very common, but this year they're scarce for some reason. This is the first one I've seen since early June. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2008)

File.Name: 8814a_8814-151.jpg
Notes: This pretty little fly in the garden is a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae, order Diptera) despite the absence of bristles. I'm pretty sure this is genus Gymnosoma. Typical tachinids are bristly, but this one is different. All these flies are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2008)

File.Name: 8814b_8813-135.jpg
Notes: I'm pretty sure this interesting fly in the garden is genus Copestylum of the family Syrphidae, order Diptera. It's pretty big, nearly honeybee size. I don't usually try to identify flies to genus, but those forked antennae are so interesting that I put in some time at bugguide.net and I think I found it. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2008)

File.Name: 8814c_8812-093.jpg
Notes: Interesting dark "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) perched on an unusual flower of Rough-leaved Aster (Aster radulinus) in the Asteraceae family. This plant is growing - and flowering! - on bone dry soil in a place that was cleared for a firebreak last summer. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2008)

File.Name: 8815a_8812-101.jpg
Notes:
I believe this is the gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera) on a twig of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia - Fagaceae) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2008)

I understand that galls are "tumorlike growths of plant tissue produced by a host plant in responce to chemical and/or mechanical stimuli of invading organisms" - but why are they so darned beautiful???
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: 8815b_8812-109.jpg
Notes: Maybe these wasp galls are the same Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus chrystallinus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera as my [Previous] photo, but the color is brilliant yellow rather than red. They are growing on a branch of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the family Fagaceae. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2008)
ScientificName: Andricus chrystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus chrystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: 8815c_8812-111.jpg
Notes: Oaks have acorns, so this must be something else growing on a branch of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the family Fagaceae). Here are strange wasp galls of the tiny Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus chrystallinus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's really a mass of separate galls each of which contains a single wasp larvae. Note that this is a gall and not (just) a nest. The tissue is part of the oak tree, though it doesn't like any normal part of the tree! (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2008)
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus chrystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus chrystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 8816a_8816-028.jpg
Notes:
I believe these are the galls of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. The galls produce honeydew which attracts ants. (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2008)

There are many questions here. Oaks make sweet honey? I've seen it before, e.g. the acorn gall at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/37845974
and [Next] from that.

I understand that galls are "tumorlike growths of plant tissue produced by a host plant in responce to chemical and/or mechanical stimuli of invading organisms" - the invading gall insect also changes the whole chemistry of the plant that is invaded!

File.Name: 8816b_8816-034.jpg
Notes:
I believe this is a group of galls of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii aka Cynips douglasii) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2008)

This is one of our galls with a complex history. The wasps inside are unisexual - females only. In the early spring, the ladies emerge and produce different bisexual galls containing both males and females which produce these galls again. And so it goes, alternating unisexual and bisexual generations.
ScientificName: Antron douglasii; Cynips douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Antron douglasii; Cynips douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 8816c_8816-038.jpg
Notes: A reminder that insect galls are not fruit. This acorn look-alike is growing with old acorn cups, but it does not itself have a cup, so it can't be an acorn. The shape and the faint color identify it as the mature gall of the same Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera) as my [Previous] photo. It's growing on a twig of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia - Fagaceae) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2008)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: 8817a_8817-069.jpg
Notes:
This is a giant dragonfly-size Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera) wasp working new flowers of a native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. This is our largest wasp - one of the biggest in the world. (Santa Ynez River, 17 August 2008)

I believe this is the male wasp with impressive golden antennae. I have another photo of the female at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/38736241

I went to the river today to a place called Paradise. It is! Unfortunately it's ALWAYS windy here, but I did ok.
ScientificName: Pepsis; Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Pepsis; Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 8817b_8817-048.jpg
Notes:
It may take a few seconds to get your bearing on this photo...

A Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) has fallen victim to a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae). Life is sometimes harsh for the bees - but good for the spiders! (Santa Ynez River, 17 August 2008)

File.Name: 8817c_8817-053.jpg
Notes: Little Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) has an even littler Beetle (order Coleoptera) for lunch! (Santa Ynez River, 17 August 2008)

File.Name: 8818a_8818-090.jpg
Notes:
This Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera has skewered its prey with it's nasty proboscis. This drama took place today in the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family growing by our mailbox. (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2008)

Summer is coming to an end - que lastima - and I'm starting to see more preditors in the woods to pick off the pollinators. It's nature's way.

File.Name: 8818b_8818-085.jpg
Notes:
Wow, this is almost too much green...

Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera) - with breakfast! I see these little flies hanging out on leaves in the garden, but I never understood what they were doing. Now I know. They are hunting for critters almost too small to see. (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2008)

File.Name: 8818c_8817-046.jpg
Notes:
These are fearsome predators that can bring down prey many times their size - but even these Ambush Bugs (family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera) need to snuggle to make more little ambush bugs. (Santa Ynez River, 17 August 2008)

These are the same native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) flowers as my [Previous] photo. That should give a sense of scale for both critters!

File.Name: 8819a_8819-120.jpg
Notes:
I found this Praying Mantis (order Orthoptera, family Mantidae) perched in a Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. It's a native mantid, not one of the big green introduced species. It's probably an immature, as it has no wings, though we have at least one species with wingless females. Is it a prAying mantis (as in showing devotion) or a prEying mantis (as in finding prey for dinner)? Either way this critter is pretty cool! (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2008)

Summer is coming to an end. Today's photos - like yesterday's - feature some more preditors in the woods that will pick off the pollinators. It's nature's way.

File.Name: 8819b_8819-099.jpg
Notes: I spotted this Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) perched in a native Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri) in the garden this morning. Like many late-season spiders, it has a scary face on its abdomen! (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2008)
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Oenothera elata; hookeri
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Oenothera elata; hookeri

File.Name: 8819c_8818-089.jpg
Notes: Another photo of the Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera that has skewered its prey with it's nasty proboscis. See my [Previous] photo. It's on the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family growing by our mailbox. It's hard to believe there's enough room for a brain in that narrow head - but it's doing very well by itself! (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2008)

File.Name: 8820a_8816-005.jpg
Notes: Golden Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) flower in the garden. Yes, it's tiny. (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2008)

File.Name: 8820b_8817-063.jpg
Notes: This diurnal (day-flying) Moth is in the same Lepidoptera order as the butterflies, but it is different. Structurally, butterflies have knobs on their antennae that moths lack, and moths have a "frenulum" that locks their wings together. Sure, butterflies are mostly brightly colored and fly by day whereas moth are mostly dull and fly by night. Of course there are always exceptions like this critter that is obviously a moth, working the daytime flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) flowers by the river. I wish I had another lifetime to study moths! (Santa Ynez River, 17 August 2008)

File.Name: 8820c_8819-112.jpg
Notes: Another photo of the Praying Mantis (order Orthoptera, family Mantidae) from yesterday. perched in a Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. It's a native mantid, not one of the big green introduced species. Pretty good camouflage - it would be easy to walk right past this critter without noticing! (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2008)

File.Name: 8821a_8821-141.jpg
Notes: Incoming Wild Bee (family Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) - not a honey bee - about to disturb a gathering of beetles on a flower of late-season native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. The milk-aster is just starting to flower in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2008)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Sunflower

File.Name: 8821b_8819-107.jpg
Notes: A pretty Grass-Skipper (Hesperia) butterfly working summer flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. The monardellas are just about finished for the year, and I hate to see them go. (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2008)
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Mint

File.Name: 8821c_8821-146.jpg
Notes: A pretty Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti, but it might be ssp. mormo with such dark wings. It's working California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2008)
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; mormo with such dark wings. It's working California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; mormo with such dark wings. It's working California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: 8822a_8822-150.jpg
Notes:
I went back to the same Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) where I found a Praying Mantis (order Orthoptera, family Mantidae) three days ago, and it was still there! It's only about 1 1/4 inches long, about 30 mm. Here it is with my thumb for scale. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2008)

This ia a petite native mantid, not one of the big green introduced species. It's probably an immature, as it has no wings, though we have at least one species with wingless females.

File.Name: 8822b_8822-159.jpg
Notes: I went back to the same Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) where I found a Praying Mantis (order Orthoptera, family Mantidae) three days ago, and it was still there! It's only about 1 1/4 inches long, about 30 mm. It's resting on the knuckle of my thumb. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2008)

File.Name: 8822c_8822-172.jpg
Notes: When I first spotted this little Praying Mantis (order Orthoptera, family Mantidae) perched on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae family, I thought it was just a cast off skin - a molt. Nope, it's alive and well. It's definately a different individual from the mantid in my [Next] photos, though the plant is nearby. It has very pale skin, and I still wonder if it just molted? (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2008)

File.Name: 8823a_8823-036.jpg
Notes:
Who says "cute fly" is an oxymoron?

Beautiful golden Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) working a summer flower of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. After spending some time with the experts at bugguide.net, I guess this critter is subfamily Bombyliinae and maybe genus Systoechus. I really don't care - here it is! (Santa Ynez River, 23 August 2008)

Yep, I did another trip to Paradise today! That's the local name for a section of the river just a few miles from home as the condor flies. There's lots of insect life on the summer-dry river wash.
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Bombyliinae; Systoechus
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Bombyliinae; Systoechus

File.Name: 8823b_8823-020.jpg
Notes:
This remarkable large Robber Fly (family Asilidae in the order Diptera) has caught a Yellow Jacket Wasp (Vespula pensylvanica or kin). The familiar wasp gives a good sense of scale for this impressive preditor. (Santa Ynez River, 23 August 2008)

These flies are preditors that catch their insect prey in the air like little dragonflies. Note the bristles on its legs to hold their prey! I don't have many photos of these flies because they don't visit flowers to feed - and hold still for a photo. It's sheer luck (and good eyes) to find one perched in the woods like this.

File.Name: 8823c_8823-012.jpg
Notes: Bristly Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae, order Diptera) on a flower cluster of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) by the river. These flies look nasty, but they leave us alone. The larvae are beneficial preditors and parasites of moth and other insect larvae - think the movie Alien! The adults work the flowers. (Santa Ynez River, 23 August 2008)

File.Name: 8824a_8822-155.jpg
Notes: I can't resist one more photo of the little Praying Mantis (order Orthoptera, family Mantidae) I found a couple of days ago. Here he is perched on my thumb. Something about the eyes seems very intelligent. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2008)

File.Name: 8824b_8822-165.jpg
Notes: This tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has set a trap on a new flower of California Goldenrod aka Oreja de Liebre (Solidago californica) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. I expect these crab spiders to match the color of the flowers they sit upon. Maybe they only turn color after a certain molt? (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2008)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; California Goldenrod; Oreja de Liebre; Composite

File.Name: 8824c_8824-046.jpg
Notes: This is a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae, order Diptera) despite the absence of bristles. It's perched on a non-native Mint (Lamiaceae) that's fowering in the garden. Typical tachinids are usually bristly, but this one is different. From looking at the pictures at bugguide.net, I'm think this might be genus Gymnosoma. Adult flies visit flowers, but their larvae are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. They are beneficial! (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2008)

File.Name: 8825a_8825-054.jpg
Notes:
Pug-nose Grass-Skipper (Hesperia) butterfly working a succulent flower in the garden. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2008)

I'm trying to ignore it, but school is catching up to me with preparations and meetings and kids next week. This will be a challenge after my stroke this summer and resulting "mild aphasia" which doesn't seem so mild at all! But I am speaking better, and writing much better. I've been doing speech therapy three days a week, but I know I've gotten the most benefit from posting these photos with written commentary every day. I'm thankful my stroke didn't effect my ability to take photos, and I give thanks to the Fotolog community for encouraging support!

File.Name: 8825b_8823-017.jpg
Notes: A beautiful Seed Bug - family Lygaeidae in the the order Hemiptera. It looks a lot like a Whitecrossed Seed Bug (Neacoryphus bicrucis), though I don't usually go to species - or care. Many of the brightly colored Lygaeids are milkweed bugs which have bright colors to warn preditors that they have a bad taste from feeding on milkweed. But I found this critter feeding on the native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. Maybe it's a mimic? (Santa Ynez River, 23 August 2008)
CommonNames: Seed Bug; Whitecrossed Seed Bug; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Lygaeidae; Hemiptera; Neacoryphus bicrucis; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Lygaeidae; Hemiptera; Neacoryphus bicrucis; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 8825c_8822-169.jpg
Notes: This is a tiny Mugwort Weevil - at least that what it should be called. It's a tiny weevil just 2-3 mm long in the family Curculionidae of the order Coleoptera that occurs on leaves of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae family. What a strange elephant-like face and trunk! (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2008)

File.Name: 8826a_8826-062.jpg
Notes:
I found this big Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) in the canyon today. Like middle school kids, it's still a waiting for its true wings to fly! (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2008)

Today I worked at home, with time for a walk. Tomorrow is a full day at school, including dinner. *Sigh*

File.Name: 8826b_8825-048.jpg
Notes: Maybe this is a Plant Bug in the family Miridae? For sure, it's some kind of "True Bug" in the order Hemiptera in the garden yesterday. I like the early morning light. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2008)

File.Name: 8826c_8826-065.jpg
Notes:
Another Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia) butterfly working a new flower cluster of California Goldenrod aka Oreja de Liebre (Solidago californica) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2008)

New flowers keep showing up in the woods even though we haven't had a drop of rain for many months, and the soil is bone dry. I have a theory about that: these late-bloomers start to flower at the end of our dry summer to keep the insects alive. It's an adaptation of the whole ecosystem!
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; California Goldenrod; Oreja de Liebre; Composite

File.Name: 8827a_8827-074.jpg
Notes:
Little Wild Bee (family Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) on the business end of a beautiful new flower of California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2008)

I took Rich Seibert's advice and politely excused myself from dinner at school tonight. I really was exhausted after trying to talk all day - and mostly managing! When I got home I tried to take a nap, but I was too wound up. Better to take photos!
CommonNames: Wild Bee; California Fuschia; Evening Primrose
ScientificName: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Epilobium canum; angustifolium; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Epilobium canum; angustifolium; Onagraceae

File.Name: 8827b_8827-071.jpg
Notes: This large black Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis or kin in the order Hymenoptera) knows a trick to get to the nectar of this long-tubed California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family. Just drill a hole in the bottom of the tube! Lots of insects seem to know this trick. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2008)
