ScientificName: Xylocopa tabaniformis; Hymenoptera; Epilobium canum; angustifolium; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa tabaniformis; Hymenoptera; Epilobium canum; angustifolium; Onagraceae

File.Name: 8827c_8822-176.jpg
Notes: Beautiful red flower of Scarlet Monkeyflower (Mimulus cardinalis) in the Scrophulariaceae family growing in our little water garden at home. It's a native that is flowering now along the river. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2008)

File.Name: 8828a_8714-055.jpg
Notes:
Here are two different oak galls at once. The star of the photo is a cute gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera). I don't know what the two black "eye spots" are. Behind it is the gall of a Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus chrystallinus) in the same wasp family. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2008)

Like all galls, these are the "tumorlike growths of plant tissue produced by a host plant in responce to chemical and/or mechanical stimuli of invading organisms" - but why are they so beautiful?
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Crystalline Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Andricus chrystallinus
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Andricus chrystallinus

File.Name: 8828b_8814-156.jpg
Notes: Here's a different plant gall on a leaf of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek. I believe these are the galls of the Willow Tube Gall Midge fly in the genus Iteomyia of the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. The galls have a little opening at the apex, and only occur on the undersides of the leaves. They might be unique to this species of willow. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2008)
CommonNames: Arroyo Willow; Willow Tube Gall Midge
ScientificName: Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Iteomyia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Iteomyia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: 8828c_8817-079.jpg
Notes:
Bright red galls of the Saucer Gall Wasp (Andricus gigas) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. These tiny galls are growing on a Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) in the Fagaceae family growing near the river. It's a "white oak" like our common scrub oak, which seen to produce the most interesting galls. (Santa Ynez River, 23 August 2008)

Like all galls, these are the "tumorlike growths of plant tissue produced by a host plant in responce to chemical and/or mechanical stimuli of invading organisms.
ScientificName: Andricus gigas; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus douglasii; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus gigas; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus douglasii; Fagaceae

File.Name: 8829a_8714-058.jpg
Notes:
This is what most people think of as an "oak gall" or an "oak apple". It's the large gall of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. They range in size from ping-pong balls to tennis balls. This one is growing on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae family) in the woods near home. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2008)

What's it good for? Well, one of the most interesting uses of the galls is to produce Iron Gall Ink. The notebooks of Leonardo da Vinci and the scores of Bach were written with this permanent ink made from these oak galls! There's a good web site (with recipes) at:
   http://www.knaw.nl/ecpa/ink/intro.html

The recipe involves tannic acid from the galls, an acid, and iron. Many years ago when I worked at the Outdoor School taking kids on nature hikes, I used to notice that my knife turned black when I sliced open galls to examine them and then sliced apples for lunch. That combination of tannic acid (from the gall), malic acid (from the apple), and my iron knife blade was making ink!
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Iron Gall Ink
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Iron Gall Ink

File.Name: 8829b_8723-111.jpg
Notes:
I believe these are galls of the "Dried Peach Gall Warp" (Disholcaspis simulata growing on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae family) in the woods. I don't make up these names, but maybe the author of my book does. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2008)

The book is Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls of California and Other Western States" (2006), and it's highly recommended.
CommonNames: Dried Peach Gall Warp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: 8829c_8817-076.jpg
Notes:
This is a little gall of the "Striped Volcano Gall Wasp" (Andricus atrimentus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. I kid you not, that is the real name! I found these pretty galls on the underside of Blue Oak leaves (Quercus douglasii in the Fagaceae family) growing near the river. (Santa Ynez River, 23 August 2008)

This is another critter with a complex life cycle. This is the all-female unisexual summer gall of a wasp that also makes a different looking bisexual spring gall in the Blue Oaks. Nature is complicated like that!
CommonNames: Striped Volcano Gall Wasp; Blue Oak
ScientificName: Andricus atrimentus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus douglasii; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus atrimentus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus douglasii; Fagaceae

File.Name: 8830a_8830-041.jpg
Notes:
Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working flowers of well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden today. It was windy, and I was trying to get photos from too far away, but this pretty lady suddenly came right up to me - almost too close! (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2008)

School prep is done, and we start with kids on Tuesday. I've been exhausted at the end of these prep days - but I think I would be anyway. I feel ready for kids, but I'm still a little worried about talking so much. I'm glad I get a holiday - from talking as well as from work - before the onslaught.

File.Name: 8830b_8830-011.jpg
Notes: Beautiful Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) near the creek. It looks fresh, maybe newly emerged. These butterflies don't seem to feed. They seem to spend their days chasing away other butterflies! (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2008)

File.Name: 8830c_8830-013.jpg
Notes: Another Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia) butterfly resting on a dead branch in the canyon. Skippers don't get no respect, but they are officially butterflies with knobs on their antennae, and they have their charm. They are our most common butterflies at the end of summer, but there are still a few "real butterflies" working the flowers. See my [Next] photos! (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2008)

File.Name: 8831a_8831-050.jpg
Notes:
Like a queen combing her golden locks...

This is more like a king - a male dragonfly-size Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera) wasp grooming its amazing antennae. It's resting on a native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Santa Ynez River, 31 August 2008)
ScientificName: Pepsis; Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Pepsis; Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 8831b_8831-058.jpg
Notes:
Enormous Spiny Tachina Fly (Paradejeania rutiloides in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera) near the river today. Well, it SEEMS enormous because it's a fly - it's about 3/4 inch (18 mm) long. I found this one working flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Santa Ynez River, 31 August 2008)

I believe that these bumble bee-size bristly flies lay eggs that become internal parasites of the equally bristly Tiger Moth (order Lepitoptera, family Arctiidae) "woolly bear" caterpillars. I often find the dead caterpillars "hollowed out" in the woods.
CommonNames: Spiny Tachina Fly; Scale Broom; Tiger Moth
ScientificName: Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Lepitoptera; Arctiidae
associatedTaxa: Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Lepitoptera; Arctiidae

File.Name: 8831c_8830-029.jpg
Notes: My [Previous] photo is a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui). This one is an American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working the same Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden yesterday. At first they are similar ladies, but look closer and you'll see that this one has two eyespots on the underside of the hindwing, while yesterday's butterfly has four eyespots. The American Lady is also noticeably smaller. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2008)
CommonNames: Painted Lady; American Lady; Butterfly Bush
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working the same Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii
associatedTaxa: Vanessa cardui; Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working the same Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii

File.Name: 8901a_8901-094.jpg
Notes:
This is our "other" swallowtail butterfly - an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon), working flowers of the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2008)

These butterflies are distinctive because they are a "black swallowtail" - the black band runs along the front wing instead of across the wing like our other swallowtails. I've been expecting these swallowtails for some time as it was a good year for the caterpillars, but the adults have been scarce. Maybe they're waiting for next year?

File.Name: 8901b_8831-057.jpg
Notes:
This wasp is a real tree-hugger!

This is a different Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae in the order Hymenoptera). I didn't find it in the flowers of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum), but rather among the stems of the same shrub. It's not as big as a Tarantula Hawk (what is?), but it's still a good sized wasp, about the size of a paper wasp. I don't know why it's hugging the shrub branch! (Santa Ynez River, 31 August 2008)

File.Name: 8901c_8831-072.jpg
Notes: Looking eye-to-eye with a male dragonfly-size Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera) wasp. It's clasping a flower head of a native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. You can see how it keeps those giant antennae out of its face - by curling them up! (Santa Ynez River, 31 August 2008)
ScientificName: Pepsis; Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Pepsis; Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 8902a_8823-027.jpg
Notes:
A pretty Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti - working flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Santa Ynez River, 31 August 2008)

I just got home from my first day of school teaching after my stroke last summer. We had a full day of classes as well as a potluck dinner. It went ok! But now I'm really exhausted and my pipes are used up from talking so much. But the good news is that day 1 went ok. (I cheated and had these photos all ready to post.)
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 8902b_8823-023.jpg
Notes:
Like a butterfly dream of golden flowers...

Here is a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working the late summer flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Santa Ynez River, 31 August 2008)

File.Name: 8902c_8819-123.jpg
Notes: Beautiful Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia) butterfly working a new summer flower of native California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) flowers in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2008)
ScientificName: Hesperia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: 8903a_8902-182.jpg
Notes:
One of my students captured this large Praying Mantis (order Orthoptera, family Mantidae) by the pool on the first day of school. Strange enough, this same student rescued an injured mantis last November on a field trip to UCSB:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/26986087

Holly, you are the preying mantis lady! (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 September 2008)

I wonder if this is a mature form of the much smaller mantid I've been seeing in the Coyote Bush? See my [Previous] photo.

File.Name: 8903b_8830-023.jpg
Notes:
I've been going back to the same Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) to visit the little Praying Mantis (order Orthoptera, family Mantidae) which seems to be parked there. I first noticed it in the Coyote Bush on August 19, and it's still in about the same place. It's active - though it seems kind of sluggish for a preditor. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2008)

I have previous photos of the same mantid here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/39559071
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/39698390

File.Name: 8903c_8830-020.jpg
Notes: Not all day-flying Lepidoptera are butterflies. This is clearly a moth - note that it lacks knobs on its antennae - but I caught it at midday working new flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae). Like all lepidopetera it has a long proboscis which you can see it you look close. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2008)

File.Name: 8904a_8829-081.jpg
Notes: I'm not sure what this strange critter is! I guess it's some kind of Homoptera, but I don't know which and I'm not going to find out tonight. I'm really tired from a full day of school and a Natural History Society meeting tonight. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2008)

File.Name: 8904b_8901-084.jpg
Notes: Summer dreams - here is a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) working composite fowers of Feverfew (Tanacetum parthenium) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. Feverfew is a non-native weed that comes up around old homesteads, so I think of it as a pioneer weed. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2008)

File.Name: 8904c_8831-056.jpg
Notes: Pretty little "Yellowjacket" type wasp probably in the genus Vespula of the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera working late summer flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. The eyes look painted on! (Santa Ynez River, 31 August 2008)

File.Name: 8905a_8831-052.jpg
Notes: This tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) seems to have a deflated abdomen! It's working native flowers of a Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Santa Ynez River, 31 August 2008)

File.Name: 8905b_8830-002.jpg
Notes: A common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) grooming under a flower of Tomatillo (Physalis ixocarpa) in the garden. The fruit of this plant are the essential ingredient in chile verde and much of my favorite food! (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2008)

File.Name: 8905c_8829-082.jpg
Notes: This is the same "Mystery Bug" as my previous photo. I guess it's some kind of Homoptera, but I don't know which. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2008)

File.Name: 8906a_8906-032.jpg
Notes:
I'd say this tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) looks ferocious, but it actually looks like it's fresh from the beauty parlour, with special attention to its eye lashes!

But it really is ferocious. The tiny spider has an even tinier Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) as its prey. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2008)

File.Name: 8906b_8906-017.jpg
Notes:
I wasn't sure what I was seeing in the upper photo - but I almost knocked my camera over when I saw what it was for sure in the bottom photo with legs akimbo!

These are mating Wild Bees (family Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) doing it face-to-face, missionary style! They are having their brief encounter on foliage of Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family, which is in flower now. I haven't seen this before! (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2008)

File.Name: 8906c_8906-004.jpg
Notes: This critter looks like a yellow jacket wasp, but the buzzy flight and especially the eyes give it away as a fly. I reckon this is a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera), maybe genus Spilomyia. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2008)

File.Name: 8907a_8907-080.jpg
Notes:
I noticed this remarkable little Wasp (order Hymenoptera) on the galls of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. See my photo of the galls at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/39424483

The little wasp has remarkable Popeye-like hind legs! I reckon that is a swollen femur, and it's neat how the tibia fits it so perfectly. I'm pretty sure this critter is a Chalcid Wasp in the family Chalcididae - perhaps genus Conura. It appears to be feeding on the honeydew the gall produces, but I wonder if it is ovipositing its eggs on the larvae inside? (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2008)
CommonNames: Wasp; Clasping Twig Gall Wasp; Chalcid Wasp
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Chalcididae; Conura
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Chalcididae; Conura

File.Name: 8907b_8907-072.jpg
Notes: A butterfly two-fer! This pretty Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly and a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly are working new autumn flowers of the composite Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. It really was a chance encounter. Butterflies are getting scarce if we move into autumn, and these were the only two butterflies working the shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2008)
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Gray Hairstreak; Bush Groundsel; Sunflower
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Strymon melinus; Senecio douglasii; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Strymon melinus; Senecio douglasii; Asteraceae

File.Name: 8907c_8906-024.jpg
Notes:
Do you mind if I keep on eating?

I found this Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) placidly munching the foliage of Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family in the canyon yesterday. It seems a little miffed by the interruption. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2008)

File.Name: 8908a_8907-045.jpg
Notes:
Looking eye-to-eye with a pretty Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera), maybe genus Spilomyia. It has painted eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2008)

See my recent photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/40383737

File.Name: 8908b_8907-069.jpg
Notes:
Interesting galls of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae family) in the woods near home. The galls produce sweet honeydew which attracts ants and little flies. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2008)

This is not the same gall as I show in the [Previous] two photos, though I believe it's the same type. Those galls were older and no longer attracting ants.
CommonNames: Clasping Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: 8908c_8907-084.jpg
Notes:
Another photo of the remarkable wasp I found on a gall of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae family) in the woods near home. I believe the wasp is a Chalcid Wasp in the family Chalcididae - perhaps genus Conura. The gall is made by the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2008)

Whew, that's a lot of latin! But there's nothing else to do with a CRITTER on a GALL on a PLANT - and I know every one!
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Chalcid Wasp; Clasping Twig Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Chalcididae; Conura; Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Chalcididae; Conura; Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 8909a_8906-036.jpg
Notes:
Perfect little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hiding like a signet on a ring in a new flower cluster of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2008)

This is the same spider I show with prey in this photo:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/40384456
As soon as it finished breakfast, it was ready for lunch!

I'm heading off on a school camping trip to Morro Bay and Montana de Oro tomorrow, so no photos for a few days. *Sigh* life goes on!

File.Name: 8909b_8906-011.jpg
Notes:
Beware - the flowers have a fang!

This is a little Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera waiting for prey in a California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2008)

File.Name: 8909c_8906-043.jpg
Notes: This is another Fly with amazing "painted" eyes. It's a large Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens - family Stratiomyidae in the insect order Diptera) in the garden. I caught this fly at rest in the shade of a green leaf in the garden. It's a natural light tent! (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2008)

File.Name: 8913a_8912-431.jpg
Notes: I caught this Brown Pelican (Pelecanus occidentalis) bird in the air! We stopped for lunch at Shell Beach on our way home from our school camping trip to Morro Bay and Montana de Oro. The birds were flying right along the bluffs, and I got lucky with this photo. (Shell Beach, 12 September 2008)

File.Name: 8913b_8911-284.jpg
Notes: Common Willow Herb (Epilobium ciliatum) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family is a common plant along creeks and seeps, but it doesn't look so common in this photo. The buds have intense color which is soon lost when the tiny flowers open. The "stalk" below the flowers is really an inferior ovary that will release a tangle of seed - like a willow tree. (Montana de Oro, 11 September 2008)

File.Name: 8913c_8911-286.jpg
Notes:
Pretty lavender flowers of Douglas Nightshade (Solanum douglasii) in the Nightshade (Solanaceae) family. (Montana de Oro, 11 September 2008)

We took our school camping and exploring at Morro Bay and Montana de Oro. I was busy taking photos os the kids, but I managed to get some photos. It was almost always foggy, but the overcast brings out the color.

File.Name: 8914a_8913-005.jpg
Notes:
Perfectly camouflaged Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with its prey - a little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera). Note that the unfortunate fly's head is turned around backwards - the rictus of its death. The plant is a Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2008)

Today's photos are always better than yesterday's. I have more good photos from our Morro Bay trip, but they can wait.
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Syrphid Flower Fly; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 8914b_8913-032.jpg
Notes: This Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera has skewered its prey with its nasty proboscis. The unfortunate victim seems to be a Wild Bee (family Apoidea, order Hymenoptera). The setting for the action is a humble Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. They are just starting to flower, and they are busy with insects. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2008)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Wild Bee; Coyote Bush; Sunflower
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: 8914c_8913-051.jpg
Notes:
Deadly (to insects) - this little Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera) is hidden in a new flower cluster of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. It's always fun to try to spot these little preditors. Many insects fail the test. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2008)

I reckon this is a female, large with eggs.

File.Name: 8915a_8913-049.jpg
Notes: A different large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey in a cluster of new flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. That's what these spiders do well - they wait! (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2008)

File.Name: 8915b_8914-074.jpg
Notes:
(Continued from [Previous] photo.)

After the large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) finished grooming, it moved over to the little Wild Bee (family Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) which was looking flat again. The spider stood over its prey, and with complete disdain, flipped it off the flower petal. Then it went back to its perch in the Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden, ready for more. (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2008)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Wild Bee; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 8915c_8914-073.jpg
Notes:
This is the same Crab Spider (Thomisidae) as I showed yesterday, but with different prey, a little Wild Bee (family Apoidea, order Hymenoptera).

Then something really interesting happened. After sucking its prey for 20 minutes or so, the spider released it! The spider did some grooming, chewing - and presumably cleaning - its front feet as you can see in this photo. And the little bee got up and staggered around for a few moments before falling over again. Maybe the "skin" of the bee was so hard the spider couldn't get in? (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2008)

See my [Next] photo for the final stage in the story.

File.Name: 8916a_8913-035.jpg
Notes:
This critter looks like a yellow jacket wasp, but the buzzy flight and especially the face give it away as a fly. I reckon this is a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera), maybe genus Spilomyia. It's working a new flower of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2008)

Yesterday I had photos of spiders. Today it's some of the flies they are waiting for.
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Coyote Bush; Sunflower
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Spilomyia; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Spilomyia; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: 8916b_8913-043.jpg
Notes:
This is a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae, order Diptera) despite the absence of bristles. It's perched on a new flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2008)

From looking at the pictures at bugguide.net, I'm think this might be genus Gymnosoma. Adult flies visit flowers, but their larvae are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. They are beneficial - except for the insects they parasitize!
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Gymnosoma
associatedTaxa: Tachinidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Gymnosoma

File.Name: 8916c_8913-053.jpg
Notes: Just another Fly in the order Diptera on a flower of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. The hairs and bristles suggest it is a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae), though I didn't get much of a look at it. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2008)
CommonNames: Fly; Coyote Bush; Sunflower; Tachinid Fly

File.Name: 8917a_8913-030.jpg
Notes: Beautiful Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly on a Zinnia flower in the garden. It looks lost in thought... (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2008)

File.Name: 8917b_8913-021.jpg
Notes: The e roost for the winter on the coast near here, but they are actually uncommon in the garden. I usually only see them in the fall. I caught this one working the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. I had to use my long lens in the breeze so it's not as sharp as I like, but it might be the only one I see this year. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2008)

File.Name: 8917c_8913-055.jpg
Notes: A pretty California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a ripe seed head of a Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) flower in the garden. That's the side of our house in the background. It odd that these butterflies rarely/never visit flowers, but it had its proboscis out for this dry seed head! (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2008)

File.Name: 8918a_8914-080.jpg
Notes:
40943138 This perfect Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly in the garden is working flowers of an unknown Aster in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. This photo looks like I used flash to get such a black background, but it's just the natural morning light and shade. I don't like using flash. (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2008)

Tonight was Back-To-School parents' night at school. I'm exhausted, but I did ok with a lot of speaking!

File.Name: 8918b_8913-042.jpg
Notes: 40942769 This handsome Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia) butterfly is working new flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. It's making a perfect spiral coil with its proboscis! (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2008)

File.Name: 8918c_8913-013.jpg
Notes: 40942579 Another Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly, this time on the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2008)

File.Name: 8919a_8911-294.jpg
Notes:
40983167 A pretty Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae) in the order Neuroptera and a Western Spotted Cucumber Beetle (Diabrotica undecimpunctata) in the order Coleoptera sharing a flower cluster of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. (Montana de Oro, 11 September 2008)

Today's photos are from our school trip to Montana de Oro State Park near Morro Bay last week. I was busy taking pictures of the kids, but I managed to get a few "Treebeard Photos" too.
CommonNames: Green Lacewing; Western Spotted Cucumber Beetle; Coastal Goldenbush; Sunflower
ScientificName: Chrysopidae; Neuroptera; Diabrotica undecimpunctata; Coleoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Chrysopidae; Neuroptera; Diabrotica undecimpunctata; Coleoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: 8919b_8911-251.jpg
Notes:
40982492 Pretty Wild Bee (superfamily Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working flowers of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. These bees drove me nuts because they would stop at a flower and then fly off to another flower many feet away, so it was impossible to frame them in a photo. (Montana de Oro, 11 September 2008)

I think of them as "ghost bees" because they have such a pale white color. The long antennae without a knob seem distinctive.
ScientificName: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: 8919c_8911-253.jpg
Notes: 40981963 I don't know what kind of Wild Bee (superfamily Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) this is. It's "semi-social" meaning that many of the bees were nesting together in the sand on the high edge of the beach. Maybe it's a Burrowing Bee in the family Andrenidae peering out from its burrow? This is a deep crop of my original photo. (Montana de Oro, 11 September 2008)

File.Name: 8920a_8920-070.jpg
Notes:
41028287 Moving in for the coup de gr‚ce...

The Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is about to give the bite that will envenom the unfortunate Honey Bee (Apis mellifera). The bee seems to be bowing its head for the inevitable stroke. The story is almost over. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2008)

This is the photo number 6 in a series of 9.
There's more!

I was lucky today to witness this encounter between a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) and a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) with camera ready. This action took place in less than a minute!
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Honey Bee; Crab Spider; Honey Bee
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Apis mellifera; Thomisidae; Apis mellifera
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Apis mellifera; Thomisidae; Apis mellifera

File.Name: 8920b_8920-065.jpg
Notes:
41027977 The Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is drawing the unfortunate Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) closer to its venom glands in its mouth... (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2008)

This is the photo number 5 in a series of 9.

File.Name: 8920c_8920-062.jpg
Notes:
41027693 It's a struggle between the Crab Spider (Thomisidae) and the Honey Bee (Apis mellifera). It could still go either way. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2008)

This is the photo number 4 in a series of 9.

File.Name: 8920d_8920-060.jpg
Notes:
41027539 The strike!

The Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has attacked the Honey Bee (Apis mellifera). It's incredibly fast. Note that the a Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden is sharp. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2008)

This is the photo number 3 in a series of 9.
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Honey Bee; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 8920e_8920-058.jpg
Notes:
41027306 Oh no, little honey bee - watch out!

This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) is about to meet its fate. The Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is barely visible at the top of the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2008)

This is the photo number 2 in a series of 9.

File.Name: 8920f_8920-054.jpg
Notes:
41027154 Looking into the maw of a Crab Spider (Thomisidae). The leg segments seem to complete a perfect circle with the mouth parts. The plant is a Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. The camouflage is perfect. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2008)

This is the photo number 1 in a series of 9.
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 8921a_8920-080.jpg
Notes:
41069841 The Crab Spider (Thomisidae) dragged her subdued Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) prey away to the underside of the flower to finish it off away from the prying eye of the photographer. The spider was not active the next day. I guess a honey bee is a pretty big meal for a spider! (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2008)

This is the photo number 9 in a series of 9.
All done!

I was lucky enough to witness this encounter between a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) and a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) with camera ready. I managed to catch the entire drama!
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Honey Bee; Crab Spider; Honey Bee
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Apis mellifera; Thomisidae; Apis mellifera
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Apis mellifera; Thomisidae; Apis mellifera

File.Name: 8921b_8920-075.jpg
Notes:
41069148 Her work done, the Crab Spider (Thomisidae) dragged her subdued Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) prey away to the underside of the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2008)

This is the photo number 8 in a series of 9.

File.Name: 8921c_8920-071.jpg
Notes:
41068862 The coup de gr‚ce...

The Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is giving the kiss of death to the unfortunate Honey Bee (Apis mellifera). The bee seems to be bowing its head for the final stroke. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2008)

This is the photo number 7 in a series of 9.

File.Name: 8922a_8920-005.jpg
Notes: 41119279 Looking close at a Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) in the woods - who's looking back at me. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2008)

File.Name: 8922b_8920-016.jpg
Notes:
41119048 I guess this is some kind of Horse Fly in the family Tabanidae of order Diptera, but it is too small to be the real Big Black Horse Fly (Tabanus punctifer). (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2008)

I don't know what it is. Like a fisherman casting his net in the sea, I only know what I catch in a photo - nothing about how it lives.
CommonNames: Horse Fly; Big Black Horse Fly

File.Name: 8922c_8913-036.jpg
Notes: 41118462 Here's a happy Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) doing what the bees do best - gathering pollen from a staminate (male) flower of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family - without a spider! (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2008)

File.Name: 8923a_8921-082.jpg
Notes:
41166314 Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a volunteer flower of Borage (Borago officinalis in the Boraginaceae family) growing in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2008)

It was cool on the day before the autumnal equinox, and the bees were about the only insects a-wing.

File.Name: 8923b_8921-085.jpg
Notes: 41165858 Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a working an unknown Aster in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family growing in our garden. It's an interesting plant that's taller than I am and covered with flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2008)

File.Name: 8923c_8914-079.jpg
Notes:
41165502 I guess this is a Leafcutter Bee (genus Megachile, family Megachilidae, order Hymenoptera) - working an unknown Aster in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family growing in our garden. These interesting bees carry pollen on their bellies rather than their hind legs. They cut those neat circular arcs from leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2008)

Monday was the Autumnal Equinox - the first day of fall - and Sunday was cool and breezy. I mostly found bees to photograph in the garden.

File.Name: 8924a_8920-043.jpg
Notes:
41213708 A white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the woods working the mostly white flowers of a staminate (male) flower of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2008)

Look close at the "pedipalps" - the little arm-like appendages that service its mouth. What do you notice that's interesting?
(Photo tomorrow!)

File.Name: 8924b_8920-035.jpg
Notes:
41213017 Another yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the woods. This one is perched on the flower bud of a Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. This plant is another one of our late-season plants that is just starting to flower in the summer-dry woods. I reckon this is the species growing in our woodland canyon. A more compact variety grows on sandstone outcrops in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2008)

The yellow flowers are surely bait!

File.Name: 8924c_8920-002.jpg
Notes: 41212607 A different little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched in attack mode on a composite flower of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2008)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 8925a_8920-006.jpg
Notes:
41259020 Sometimes a flower is just a flower...

These are a beautiful unknown Aster in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family growing in our garden. It's an interesting plant that's taller than I am and covered with flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2008)

File.Name: 8925b_8921-090.jpg
Notes:
41258722 A pretty Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti - working flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Santa Ynez River, 21 September 2008)

It was windy and cool by the river on Sunday, and I only got a few photos. I had better luck photographing galls on the scrub oaks. Photos tomorrow.
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 8925c_8920-043.jpg
Notes: 41258272 A closer crop of the Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the woods working a composite flower cluster of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in my [Previous] photo. Yes pitquim and garyblau, that is pollen on it's paws. It is completing the illusion that it is part of the flower! (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2008)

File.Name: 8926a_8926-134.jpg
Notes:
41312235 A handful of katydid!

One of my students found this Angular Winged Katydid (Microcentrum rhombifolium in the Tettigoniidae family of the Orthoptera) this morning at school. I wasn't quite ready for a photo shoot and I flubbed the exposure. It's still pretty cool - it looks like a mottled leaf, complete with veins! (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2008)

File.Name: 8926b_8907-094.jpg
Notes:
41311696 Good news for the birds - there's a bumper crop of Holly-Leaved Cherries (Prunus ilicifolia in the Rose (Rosaceae) family) growing in our mountains. I haven't seen such a crop at least since I started this f'log in 2003. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2008)

The ripe fruit are tasty, but the flesh is very thin. The indigenous Chumash people mostly ate the large nuts after leaching. I've tried them. They are tasty, like almonds!

File.Name: 8926c_8907-062.jpg
Notes: 41311071 Ripening acorn of a native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae family) in the woods. It's the staff of life for the chaparral, and this looks like a pretty good year. (The live oaks are not doing so well.) (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2008)

File.Name: 8927a_8927-022.jpg
Notes:
41351847 Pretty West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly working the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in Julie's garden at school. (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 September 2008)

Our local Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society (I'm the president) had a native plant sale at school today, and I was there all day. I took a break in Julie's garden for today's photos. That's not a bad place to be!
Locality: I'm the president) had a native plant sale at school today
CommonNames: West Coast Lady; Butterfly Bush; Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society

File.Name: 8927b_8927-035.jpg
Notes: 41351461 This Banded Argiope (Argiope trifasciata) garden spider has made a bundle of an unfortunate fly. These large garden spiders have been scarce this year - or maybe they're just starting to appear. (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 September 2008)

File.Name: 8927c_8927-017.jpg
Notes:
41351213 It's a dirty job, but someone has to do it!

Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) with full pollen baskets - and pollen everywhere! - working flowers of a staminate (male) Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 September 2008)

File.Name: 8928a_8928-084.jpg
Notes:
41395902 A remarkable Great Purple Hairstreak (Atlides halesus) butterfly working the native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river today. (Santa Ynex River, 28 September 2008)

The name doesn't make sense, but the butterfly is bright iridescent blue above. This butterfly has lost its pretty blue tail (and one of its antennae). They twitch their tails while nectaring to look like another head. I guess the deception works!

I actually went to the river today hoping to get a photo of this butterfly. Sometimes you get what you wish for!

File.Name: 8928b_8928-058.jpg
Notes: 41395341 A perfect California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly getting whatever it gets by "puddling" on the wet ground near the garden where the sprinkler went too far. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2008)

File.Name: 8928c_8928-100.jpg
Notes:
41395043 I guess this is a female Melissa Blue (Lycaeides melissa) butterfly working an unknown composite in the garden - maybe a Chrysanthemum of some sort. The Melissa Blue is our only butterfly with a sub-marginal orange band on both hindwings and forewings, though I would expect it higher in the mountains. It stood out as something different. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2008)

Update 30 Sep. 2008 - I just got word from Nick Lethaby who keeps me honest that "It's a female Acmon Blue with some orange up the FW above." Too bad!

File.Name: 8929a_8928-073.jpg
Notes: 41443532 Beautiful Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti - working flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. They are still common, but their days are numbered as autumn approaches. (Santa Ynez River, 28 September 2008)
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 8929b_8928-050.jpg
Notes: 41443406 Common Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia) butterfly perched on a leaf in the garden catching the morning sun. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2008)

File.Name: 8929c_8928-076.jpg
Notes: 41443277 I can't resist posting another photo of the remarkable Great Purple Hairstreak (Atlides halesus) butterfly I captured yesterday. It has patriotic red white and blue colors, though this one is missing its iridescent blue tail. It's working the native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. I probably won't see another one until about this time next year - if I'm lucky. (Santa Ynex River, 28 September 2008)

File.Name: 8930a_8927-043.jpg
Notes:
41487815 "Hey sailor, looking for a good time?"

I think I'll keep the identity of this critter a secret until tomorrow. Can you guess what it is? (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2008)

File.Name: 8930b_8928-065.jpg
Notes: 41487423 This diurnal (day-flying) Moth is in the same Lepidoptera order as the butterflies, but it is different. Structurally, butterflies have knobs on their antennae that moths lack, and moths have a "frenulum" that locks their wings together. Sure, butterflies are mostly brightly colored and fly by day whereas moth are mostly dull and fly by night. Of course there are always exceptions like this critter that is obviously a moth, working the daytime flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) flowers by the river. (Santa Ynez River, 28 September 2008)

File.Name: 8930c_8930-103.jpg
Notes:
41487088 We had a different kind of "bug" in the air today!

This VERY LOUD helicopter was flying over our house all day today, making a pass about every five minutes. I gave up trying to take a nap and got my camera instead. DEA? Nah, the chopper is too big, and it's making nearly the same run every time. Julie figured it out - its hydro-mulching the burn area from the Gap Fire last summer, spreading a slurry of seed and fertilizer. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2008)

Darn it, I appreciate the concern for mudslides next winter in town, but they're spreading alien seed over an area that needs no help.

Even I have to admit it's a pretty awesome helicopter! Number 745 is an Erickson Air-Crane S-64E, made for heavy lifting. I think I have a photo of the same helicopter working the Gap Fire last summer.

File.Name: 8a01a_8928-070.jpg
Notes:
41533841 Oh those eyes...

This little Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) is perched on a sandstone boulder in the dry wash of the river bed. (Santa Ynez River, 28 September 2008)

The "pupil" is an optical effect that all insects with complicated-enough eyes show. The six-sided highlight is a diffraction pattern of light reflecting from the thousands of six-sided ommatidia or "simple eyes" that make up the damselfly's compound eyes like a retina. They are wonderful, you can gaze into them...

File.Name: 8a01b_8928-066.jpg
Notes: 41533331 I wonder if these Yellow Jacket type wasps in the Vespidae family of the order Hymenoptera remenber the golden days of summer, when everythig was perfect... (Santa Ynez River, 28 September 2008)

File.Name: 8a01c_8927-048.jpg
Notes:
41532903 This is another shot of the "mystery critter" from my [Previous] photo. It doesn't look so inviting in this photo!

Yes Gynandromorph, I reckon it's a vinchuca - at least it's in the same Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family of the order Hemiptera. The true vinchuca carries Chagas' Disease, a form of trypanosomiasis. I'm glad that ours don't carry the parasitic disease organism! (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2008)

File.Name: 8a02a_8928-095.jpg
Notes:
41581521 Margie is holding a large Praying Mantis (order Mantidae; or order Orthoptera, family Mantidae depending which book I use) that we found on the grass at school as we were cleaning up after the Plant Sale last weekend. The mantid made a feeble defence move, but it wasn't feeling well, and we left it in the grass, head down. Maybe it was spawned out? The next day it was gone. (Santa Ynez Valley, 28 September 2008)

What are those pretty black and white structures on its legs?

File.Name: 8a02b_8928-062.jpg
Notes:
41581075 A different Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) perched on a flower cluster of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family. (Santa Ynez River, 28 September 2008)

This might be the same species as the blue critter in my [Previous] photo. I've noticed blue and brown damselflies mating. I think the blue damsel is the man.

File.Name: 8a02c_8928-069.jpg
Notes:
41580917 Another photo of the Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) perched on a sandstone boulder in the dry wash of the river bed. (Santa Ynez River, 28 September 2008)

There's no way to do justice to this critter - or the photo - with just 500 pixels!

File.Name: 8a03a_8928-052.jpg
Notes:
41623130 Golden Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on a stem of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. These plants are not native in California, though the aphids enjoy the native milkweeds too. I think they are as pretty as the flowers, and I leave them be. I always wonder how the pretty aphids find the milkweed every year about this time. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2008)

I like the light and dark backgroud of this photo. Which side are you on?
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 8a03b_8928-060.jpg
Notes: 41622707 Pretty little Yellow Jacket type wasp in the Vespidae family of the order Hymenoptera working a new flower of late-season Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. (Santa Ynez River, 28 September 2008)

File.Name: 8a03c_8927-040.jpg
Notes:
41622484 Here are the first new flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. I reckon these are the the first flowers of the new year - the new "rain year" that is. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2008)

We have a summer-dry and winter-wet Mediterranean climate here in central California. I'm still finding late-season flowers at this dry end of the year, but these pretty flowers mark the beginning of a new year, even before we get a drop of rain.

There's a chance of rain tomorrow - yahoo!

File.Name: 8a04a_8a04-016.jpg
Notes:
41667086 Raindrops on a flower of Tomatillo (Physalis ixocarpa in the Nightshade (Solanaceae) family) growing in the garden. The fruit of this plant are the essential ingredient in chile verde and much of my favorite food! (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2008)

We had real rain today! Just a tenth of an inch or less, but it was steady drizzle most of the day. That's pretty impressive when you consider that we haven't had a drop of rain since last March. We have a summer-dry and winter-wet Mediterranean climate here in central California, and the seasons are just starting to tip to the wet cycle. I hope it clears up tomorrow - without wind - so I can get some photos!

File.Name: 8a04b_8a04-008.jpg
Notes: 41666635 Raindrops on the Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family growing in the garden. Only it's not just raindrops as the drops on the old seed pods were tinted orange like this. Maybe it's the summer accumulation of plant resins washing off the leaves? (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a04c_8a04-018.jpg
Notes: 41666357 Prickley Pear (Opuntia sp.) cactus fruit in the garden during a light rain today. The rain has a way of bringing out the color. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a05a_8a05-048.jpg
Notes:
41706790 The Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) are starting to converge near the creek! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2008)

Usually they converge in mid-winter between December and February, but I have documented this early convergence before. They're early again this year. How do you get invited to this rave?

I have many photos in the archive:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/36077390 (31 May 2008)
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/18527346 (3 Feb 2007)
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/17585542 (16 Dec 2006)
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/16230121 (7 Oct 2006)
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/5623817 (29 Jan 2004)

File.Name: 8a05b_8a05-031.jpg
Notes: 41706023 Pretty Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly on the ground trying to warm up in the morning after a bit of rain yesterday and a cool night. It really feels like autumn now! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a05c_8a05-055.jpg
Notes: 41705656 This mostly-white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the woods has got its prey. It's working the white flowers of a staminate (male) flower of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a06a_8a05-100.jpg
Notes:
41759869 This fearless golden Milkweed Aphid (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) is perched on an aphid killer - a worm-rider for sure!

I believe the worm is the larva of a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera). The aphid actually walked up and down the invader's back at least twice, as though it was taunting the beast. I'll have a photo of the entire beast tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2008)
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Syrphidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Syrphidae; Diptera

File.Name: 8a06b_8a05-090.jpg
Notes: 41759447 This is a Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens - family Stratiomyidae in the insect order Diptera) in the garden. What amazing eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a06c_8a05-084.jpg
Notes: 41759317 Lady Beetles aren't the only critters "converging" right now in the woods after our first rain! I found these reproductive Termites (order Isoptera) emerging from a hole under a leaf a few feet from our porch. I also saw other swarms emerging in the woods. I hope they aren't eating our house! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a07a_8a05-062.jpg
Notes: 41804902 Hah - I was all focused on the little Beetle (order Coleoptera) working the stamens of a late-season flower of native Stephanomeria virgata in the composite Sunflower (Asteraceae) family, when a HUGE Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) flew into the photo. At least it seemed huge in my viewfinder. Nothing to do except take the photo! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2008)
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Stephanomeria virgata; Asteraceae; Apis mellifera
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Stephanomeria virgata; Asteraceae; Apis mellifera

File.Name: 8a07b_8a05-064.jpg
Notes:
41804469 Charcoal-black Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly working the late-season flowers of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. I guess from the color - or lack of it - that it's a male. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2008)

We have two different duskywing butterflies with white trim on the hind wing, and I think I've finally got them right. The Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) is mostly dark brown, and the Funereal Duskywing (E. funeralis) really is black. I'm sure I have a few photos with the wrong name in the archive.
CommonNames: Funereal Duskywing; Sawtooth Goldenbush; Sunflower; Mournful Duskywing; Funereal Duskywing
ScientificName: Erynnis funeralis; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Erynnis funeralis; E. funeralis

File.Name: 8a07c_8a05-099.jpg
Notes: 41804006 Here's another photo of "the beast" - the larva of a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera). It's picking off the golden Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on a seed pod of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. That's the head at the bottom. You can see the worm-rider aphid walking on the back of the beast! See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2008)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Milkweed Aphids; Butterfly Weed
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 8a08a_8a05-104.jpg
Notes: 41848873 Captured in the glare of the autumn sun - an American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. Look close and you'll see that this lady has two eyespots on the underside of her hindwing. Other ladies have four eyespots. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2008)
ScientificName: Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii

File.Name: 8a08b_8814-084.jpg
Notes: 41848701 Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera hanging out in the garden trying to get warm on a zinnia flower. Lots of these wasp nest under the eaves of our porch. I leave them be, they leave me be - we coexist. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a08c_8a05-061.jpg
Notes: 41848482 Here's the same little Beetle (order Coleoptera) working the stamens of a late-season flower of native Stephanomeria virgata in the composite Sunflower (Asteraceae) family - without the huge interloper. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a09a_8928-090.jpg
Notes: 41887579 I guess this is a yellow form of the Banded Argiope (Argiope trifasciata) garden spider in the school garden. It looks like it's flying over a magical fractal landscape, but the background is really a mass of Society Garlic (genus Tulbaghia in the Alliaceae plant family). (Santa Ynez Valley, 28 September 2008)

File.Name: 8a09b_8928-093.jpg
Notes: 41887033 A phalanx of little immature "True Bugs" (order Hemiptera) standing their ground on a leaf in the school garden. It's interesting how they are arranged facing outward to ward off a potential enemy (me). (Santa Ynez Valley, 28 September 2008)

File.Name: 8a09c_8927-020.jpg
Notes:
41886084 Yuck - this is a Green Bottle Fly aka Blow Fly (Phaenicia sericata or kin, family Calliphoridae of the Diptera). These flies are handsome but nasty. They breed in garbage and carrion, but they feed on flowers like this native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. (Santa Ynez Valley, 28 September 2008)

I took this photo at school near the pig pen. (Yes, we raise pigs at school to take care of the dining hall waste - don't you?) I rotated the photo 90 degrees to look more familiar. The real fly was hanging head down.
CommonNames: Green Bottle Fly; Blow Fly; Coyote Bush; Sunflower
ScientificName: Phaenicia sericata; Calliphoridae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Phaenicia sericata; Calliphoridae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: 8a10a_8a05-071.jpg
Notes:
41925956 I know this gall! It's the gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera), and I recognize it as an individual because it's so cute! I have another photo of the same gall at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/39974569

Like a wrinkled apple doll, the gall has shriveled up and acquired character! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a10b_8912-375.jpg
Notes:
41924478 This gall on new female (pistillate) flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family looks like a glob of bread dough that it engulfing the flower! I believe it's the gall of the Coyote Bush Bud Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia californica) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. (Morro Bay, 12 September 2008)

I've been busy with school stuff, and it's the slow time of the year for new photos. I'll slip in a few good photos from my "ready" folder that I should have posted earlier.
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; Sunflower; Coyote Bush Bud Gall Midge
