associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Rhopalomyia californica; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: 8a10c_8a04-015.jpg
Notes: 41923602 This is a fresh-looking Earthstar (Astreus hygrometricus or kin) "mushroom" - at least it's the fruiting body of a fungus - growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods last weekend. What's strange is that last Saturday was our first rain in six months, and this mushroom - at least the "rind" - seems fresh above the leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a11a_8a11-030.jpg
Notes:
41969707 I guess this wasp-mimic fly is a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - maybe genus Copestylum. But it was acting more like a Soldier Fly in the Stratiomyidae family in the way it hovered and faced me. I found it on vegetation by the mostly dry creek in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2008)

It was windy today and it's cold tonight - we're under a frost warning. But we're expected to have Santa Ana winds and heat next week. That's our autumn season in a nutshell! The common factor is wind, wind, wind - the enemy of my photos.

File.Name: 8a11b_8a11-019.jpg
Notes: 41969195 I guess this strange-looking critter is a Treehopper (family Membracidae in the order Homoptera). I found it sitting stationary of a flower of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. If it's trying to look like a thorn, it's on the wrong plant! (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a11c_8a11-007.jpg
Notes: 41968804 Some sort of plain brown "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) looking up at me in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a12a_8a12-067.jpg
Notes: 42008985 A beautiful spent chrysalis of some sort on a fall-colored leaf of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae family). (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a12b_8a12-118.jpg
Notes: 42008558 I guess this strange-looking critter is a Treehopper (family Membracidae in the order Homoptera). It's perched near some buds on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) catching afternoon light. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a12c_8a12-114.jpg
Notes:
42008132 A common Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia) butterfly fully engaged with a hybrid Satureja flower in the Mint Family (Labiatae). It's an interesting plant related to native Yerba Buena (S. douglasii) and non-native Summer savory (S. hortensis). (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2008)

The skippers were the only butterflies on the wing today, and not many of those. It's been cold! We didn't get frost last night, but it was close. I expect it to warm up next week, and the butterflies will probably be back.
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; Mint Family; Yerba Buena; Summer savory
ScientificName: Hesperia; Satureja; Labiatae; S. douglasii; S. hortensis
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Satureja; Labiatae; S. douglasii; S. hortensis

File.Name: 8a13a_8a12-064.jpg
Notes: 42062960 Another photo of a beautiful spent chrysalis of some sort on a leaf of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae family). I'm not sure what it is, or how old it is. I found a half-dozen of these on one particular scrub oak, and none on neighboring plants. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a14a_8a11-037.jpg
Notes:
42064264 A pair of Yellow Jacket wasps in the Vespidae family of the order Hymenoptera working my hummingbird feeder. Yes, these wasps are nasty enough to keep the hummers at bay - they even turn on each other, though I caught these two during a truce. The little arrow pointing down their abdomen is pretty neat! This is perfect late-afternoon light. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2008)

The yellow jackets have only recently become a problem at the hummingbird feeder. They get nasty in autumn. Maybe they know their time is coming?

I gave up trying to post these photos last night. No problem, I was happy for a night off.

File.Name: 8a14b_8a12-051.jpg
Notes:
42063444 Pretty Wild Bee (superfamily Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) - maybe family Halictidae? - working flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2008)

I have a photo of a very similar bee at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/40982492
ScientificName: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Halictidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Halictidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: 8a15a_8a12-089.jpg
Notes:
42113066 The birds haven't yet discovered the bumper crop of Islay aka Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rose (Rosaceae) family growing in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2008)

The ripe fruit are tasty, though the unripe fruit are actually poisonous. The flesh is very thin. The indigenous Chumash people mostly ate the large nuts after leaching. I've tried them. They are tasty, like almonds!

File.Name: 8a15b_8a12-072.jpg
Notes: 42112783 Small but pretty flowers of Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family growing in the mountains. It's a curious plant. Most of its relaties grow in wet places, but this late-summer species flowers along dry trails and roads in the mountains no matter how dry it has been. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a15c_8a11-022.jpg
Notes: 42112604 Another white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the woods on pistillate (female) flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a16a_8a12-048.jpg
Notes: 42156204 Stephanomeria virgata is another one of our late-season natives that has flowers at the end of our dry Mediterranean climate summer. It looks kind of weedy and grows along the mountain roads, but the flowers are elegant and beautiful up close. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a16b_8a12-055.jpg
Notes: 42155881 Late-season flowers of a native composite Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in our mountains. You have to appreciate that it hasn't rained (to speak of) for seven months, yet some flowers wait until now to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a16c_8a12-060.jpg
Notes:
42155635 Cherries aren't the only fruit getting ripe for autumn. Here is a ripe fruit of a California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Laurel (Lauraceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2008)

Have you ever accidentally eaten a "Bay Leaf" in a stew? *Yuck* Our native bay trees are like that only more so, and the fruit are worse! But the large nuts inside the fuit are tasty and were used by the Chumash native Americans. The trees are related to avocados, and they look like it.

File.Name: 8a17a_8a12-101.jpg
Notes:
42200351 Looking out at the city of Santa Barbara over the devastation of the Gap Fire last July. The chaparral has been reduced to burnt twigs, but you can see a bit a green as the Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae family makes new growth.

The Forest Service has been dumping tons of "hydro-mulch" on this burned area to prevent mudslides next winter - no seeds, just the mulch. (See my [Previous] photos.) The mulch contains a dye that only lasts for a day or two to guide the helicopters. I hope it helps, but I live above this wildfire! (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a17b_8a12-099.jpg
Notes: 42199728 Stump-sprouting new growth of Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae family coming up on the Gap Fire burn area from last July. This place has been coated with a "hydro-mulch" compound designed to hold the soil during winter rains. See my [Previous] photo. The word is that there are no seeds in this mix, it's just a mulch. That's good news. It should be a glorious spring with native fire-follower wildflowers! (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a17c_8a12-097.jpg
Notes:
42199293 Good old helicopter number 745 doing a drop on the burn area of the "Gap Fire" which happened last summer just a mile or two from my home. It's not the familiar red fire retardant, but a green "hydro-mulch" that is intended to hold the soil in place. There are no seeds in this hydro-mulch. It's mostly shredded paper and fiber intended to hold the soil in place during the winter rains. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2008)

See my fire photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/37369803
and [Next] from there.

File.Name: 8a18a_8a18-021.jpg
Notes:
42241838 A vinchuca - at least it's in the same Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family of the order Hemiptera as it's notorious South American relative. It's a formidable predator, using it's impressive proboscis to skewer it's prey. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2008)

Insect time is almost done. I mostly found honey bees and wasps amd flies today, nothing very interesting, until I came upon this critter in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) near the creek.

File.Name: 8a18b_8a18-010.jpg
Notes:
42241356 A little Pomace Fly aka Small Fruit Fly in the family Drosophilidae in the order Diptera resting on a milkweed leaf in the garden. I usually find these flies in November when the Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) come out. Maybe this one is a little bit early, or maybe there's an early mushroom that I haven't found yet. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2008)

This fly is in the same Drosophila genus as the critter still studied in genetics classes around the world. No other creature has contributed so much to our understanding of genetics!
CommonNames: Pomace Fly; Small Fruit Fly; Honey Mushrooms

File.Name: 8a18c_8a18-025.jpg
Notes: 42240949 I believe this is a Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera. It's hanging out in the garden trying to get warm. Their antennae don't always coil like this, but it's pretty neat when they do. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a19a_8a19-049.jpg
Notes:
42287946 A perfect Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) perched on a spent flower cluster of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family on the dry wash of the river today. (Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2008)

The eyes are amazing. The "pupil" is an optical effect that all insects with complicated-enough eyes show. The six-sided highlight is a diffraction pattern of light reflecting from the thousands of six-sided ommatidia or "simple eyes" that make up the damselfly's compound eyes like a retina. They are wonderful, you can gaze into them...
ScientificName: Zygoptera; Odonata; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; ommatidia
associatedTaxa: Zygoptera; Odonata; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; ommatidia

File.Name: 8a19b_8a19-052.jpg
Notes: 42287777 A different Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae, order Hemiptera) that I found on the dry river wash today, on a flower of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum). This photo also shows the impressive proboscis that it uses to skewer it's prey, folded up under it's narrow head. (Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a19c_8a18-023.jpg
Notes: 42287606 Another photo of the Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae, order Hemiptera) that I found yesterday in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near the creek. This photo shows the impressive proboscis that it uses to skewer it's prey, folded up under it's narrow head. It's a beautiful turquoise color. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a20a_8a19-061.jpg
Notes: 42329879 This beautiful Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly is showing all its "eyes"! There are still late-season butterflies working the native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry river wash, though they're getting scarce at home. (Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a20b_8a19-035.jpg
Notes: 42329489 This tattered veteran is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly trying to warm up in the sun on a chilly morning. It's perched on an equally tattered leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae family. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a20c_8a19-045.jpg
Notes:
42329088 This is the same perfect Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) as my [Previous] photo. It's perched on a spent flower cluster of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family on the dry wash of the river. (Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2008)

Look close under the rear of the thorax, and you can see several hitchhiking Mites (Acari)! I didn't notice them when I got the photo, I was too distracted by the amazing eyes.
Locality: Acari)! I didn't notice them when I got the photo
ScientificName: Zygoptera; Odonata; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Acari
associatedTaxa: Zygoptera; Odonata; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Acari

File.Name: 8a21a_8a19-054.jpg
Notes:
42376691 A large Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae in the order Hymenoptera) dusted with pollen on the flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. It's a big wasp, but not as big as the fabled Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis) which I haven't see for a while. (Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2008)

I had to fiddle with this photo - it was hard to show the blacker-than-black wasp along with the bright yellow flowers.
CommonNames: Spider Wasp; Scale Broom; Tarantula Hawk

File.Name: 8a21b_8a19-040.jpg
Notes: 42376231 This pretty Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia) butterfly is working a nearly done flower cluster of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family by our mailbox. The flowers are the color of autumn in our mountains... (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a21c_8a19-064.jpg
Notes: 42375834 Tattered Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti - hangin on to a flower head of a native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. A few of these little butterflies are still working the last of the flowers, but their days are numbered as autumn settles in. (Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2008)
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 8a22a_8a19-070.jpg
Notes:
42418783 I should know by now that when I find an insect stationary on a flower, there's a preditor at work! But it took quite a while before I noticed the deadly (for insects) Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera). Do you see it now, on the left side of the flower cluster?

I guess the unfortunate fly is a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae, order Diptera) because it has so many bristles. The flower is native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash of the river. It was very windy. Nothing is quite sharp. (Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2008)
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Tachinid Fly; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Tachinidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Tachinidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 8a22b_8a19-036.jpg
Notes: 42418232 Unknown Wild Bee (superfamily Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working a late-season Aster (Asteraceae) in the garden. The bee is loaded with pollen! It looks like a spider web - and a spider - to the left of the bee, but it's just the detritus of the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a22c_8a18-033.jpg
Notes:
42417989 Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working new winter flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. I reckon these are the the first new flowers of the new year - the new "rain year" that is. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2008)

It's hard to believe it's really autumn - we've had temperatures in the 90's and we're back under a red flag warning for fire danger. But the days are growing short, and the weather will change.

File.Name: 8a23a_8a19-094.jpg
Notes:
42465098 I found this large California Prionus (Prionus californicus) beetle in the Cerambycidae family of the Coleoptera in the garden - dead! It died among the fragrant leaves of native Yerba Buena (Satureja douglasii in the in the Mint Family (Labiatae) and I left it there, though I arranged it to look natural. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2008)

The last photo I have of the living beetle is dated 6 July 2008:
  http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/37594129

I'm tired tonight after taking my after-school Hike Club kids to the swimming hole at Red Rock. One photo will do.
CommonNames: California Prionus; Yerba Buena; Mint Family
ScientificName: Prionus californicus; Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Satureja douglasii; Labiatae
associatedTaxa: Prionus californicus; Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Satureja douglasii; Labiatae

File.Name: 8a24a_8a23-114.jpg
Notes:
42508004 Red Rock is a legendary local swimming hole on the section of the Santa Ynez River known as Paradise. I usually avoid it because it's such a party spot, but I took my after-school Hike Club kids there yesterday for a swim, and we had it to ourselves. If you look close, you can see the kids climbing the rocks at the bottom of the photo for scale! (Santa Ynez River, 23 October 2008)

Yes, some people try to jump from the top of the rock - every year someone dies at Red Rock trying to jump - but it's a fabulous swimming hole!

File.Name: 8a24b_8a18-011.jpg
Notes: 42507577 Just a fly in the order Diptera resting on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) - perhaps it's a Scavenger Fly in the family Sarcophagidae? (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a25a_8a25-033.jpg
Notes: 42550048 I found this perfect Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family by our mailbox today! I'm surprised to find any butterflies at this time of year, let alone one so small and delicate. It's a summer butterfly, transported into autumn! (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a25b_8a25-024.jpg
Notes: 42549674 Pretty little "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) - maybe family Lygaeidae? It's perched on a seed head of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a25c_8a25-017.jpg
Notes: 42549363 Tiny Wild Bee (?) (superfamily Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working little flower of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family. It's a native plant, but it's doing well in our garden - it still has a few flowers!
ScientificName: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Hydrophyllaceae
associatedTaxa: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Hydrophyllaceae

File.Name: 8a26a_8a26-103.jpg
Notes:
42598504 Pretty Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly on the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden today. The underside of the hindwings has beautiful filagree, like the inside cover of an antique book. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2008)

We've had all three of our Ladies visiting the Butterfly Bush recently:
   American Lady: http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/41848873
   West Coast Lady: http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/41351847

File.Name: 8a26b_8a26-076.jpg
Notes:
42597815 This Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly hiding most of its "eyes" - but it's still an effective display. I can almost see half of a fantastic owl with the one eye showing, like a halloween mask! It's feeding on a plant of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash of the river. (Santa Ynez River, 26 October 2008)

I have a recent photo showing all of this creature's "eyes" at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/42329879

File.Name: 8a26c_8a25-037.jpg
Notes: 42597383 I can't resist posting another photo of the perfect Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly I featured yesterday. It's working the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family that still has a few flowers by our mailbox. I guess it's a female because the top side wasn't blue. The iridescent blue flecks surrounding the eyespots on the hindwings give it great beauty. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a27a_8a26-085.jpg
Notes:
42686523 Another tattered Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti.

This one is working new flowers of the native composite shrub Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) growing on the dry flood plain of the river. This is one of the last of our summer flowers that overlaps the new Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrubs starting to flower at home. There is always something flowering in our muntains, but we really need rain to start the new generation! (Santa Ynez River, 26 October 2008)
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Palmer's Goldenbush; Chaparral Currant
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Ericameria palmeri; pachylepis; Ribes malvaceum
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Ericameria palmeri; pachylepis; Ribes malvaceum

File.Name: 8a28a_8a26-071.jpg
Notes:
42687986 This beautiful wasp has desert colors, and it's working near-desert flowers on the dry river wash which is about as dry as we get around here. This is a Vespid Wasp (family Vespidae , order Hymenoptera) tightly wrapped around a native composite flower of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum). (Santa Ynez River, 26 October 2008)

If you compare this flower with the flowers in the [Previous] photos, you'll be struck by how similar they are. There are even more late-season species of yellow composite Asteraceae shrubs in our natural area. It's a very successful plant type!

File.Name: 8a28b_8a26-080.jpg
Notes:
42687129 Bugs suck!

Of course they suck. All "True Bugs" (Hemiptera) have sucking mouth parts, so they can't help but suck their food. I believe this is a "Green Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the order Hemiptera. It might be genus Chlorochroa. It's sucking a new flower head of Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) growing on the dry flood plain of the river. (Santa Ynez River, 26 October 2008)
CommonNames: True Bugs" (Hemiptera) have sucking mouth parts, so they can't help but suck their food. I believe this is a "Green Stink Bug; Chlorochroa; Palmer's Goldenbush
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Ericameria palmeri; pachylepis
associatedTaxa: Hemiptera; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Ericameria palmeri; pachylepis

File.Name: 8a29a_8a26-047.jpg
Notes:
42734457 Now why did the tarantula cross the road?

I suspect he just wanted to get to the other side, at least I can't see any reason behind it. On the way to the river I encountered one tarantula going south to north, but this is is going north to south. Go figure.

It's a hand-size California Tarantula (Aphonopelma reversum or kin) spider walking across a forest service chip seal road. (It's not much of a road.) I tried to pick it up for a in-hand photo, but it just wanted to keep on walking. It feels like a walking feather. They have fangs and can bite, but there's no reason to be afraid of them. (Santa Ynez River, 26 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a29b_8a26-063.jpg
Notes: 42733943 Another pretty little "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) - maybe family Lygaeidae? This one is perched on a seed head of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. (Santa Ynez River, 26 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a29c_8a26-064.jpg
Notes: 42733719 A two-fer of Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterflies - probably Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti. The flowers are composite Hairy Golden Aster (Heterotheca sessiliflora ssp. echoides) on the dry wash along the river. It's another of the golden flowered composites that make up so much of our late-season flowers. (Santa Ynez River, 26 October 2008)
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Heterotheca sessiliflora; echoides
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Heterotheca sessiliflora; echoides

File.Name: 8a30a_8a25-013.jpg
Notes: 42781338 Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) snuggling into a new winter flower of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a30b_8a26-066.jpg
Notes:
42781099 There's love in the flowers!

This is a mating pair of Ambush Bugs (family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera) trying to hide in the flowers of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. It must be hard for such consummate preditors to get close enough to a a member of the same species to mate! (Santa Ynez River, 26 October 2008)

I have another photo of Ambush Bugs "doing it" at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/39511611

File.Name: 8a30c_8a25-042.jpg
Notes: 42780887 Aphid ecology would be a pretty interesting study. Here are golden Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on the tip of a seed pod of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. The is a winged adult at the top of the seed pod. Now look closer. There are a pair of different aphids - one of them parasitized - near the top. I see a few of these different aphids in every photo. Are they really different, or do they carry a parasite which changes their shape and color before it kills them? (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2008)
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 8a31a_8a26-068.jpg
Notes:
42820868 This is halloween terror for many insects! It's an Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera). It's a mighty preditor (for it's size) that hides in the flowers. (Santa Ynez River, 26 October 2008)

This one is pissed off because I interrupted it while mating. See my photo of the same critter posted yesterday:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/42781099

File.Name: 8a31b_8a19-088.jpg
Notes: 42820444 This clutch of insect eggs have almost all been parasitized - you can tell by the neat chewed exit holes where something unintended crawled out of the egg case. Think of the movie "Alien". The preditor is probably a parasitic wasp. There are many kinds. (Santa Ynez River, 26 October 2008)

File.Name: 8a31c_8a25-039.jpg
Notes:
42820042 Perfectly named "Dead Man's Foot" (Pisolithus tinctorius) mushroom coming up in it's favorite habitat - along a road. Sometimes they come up through the road!

I've also heard it called the "Dog Turd Fungus", "Dyemaker's Puffball", and "Bohemian Truffle" - yes it may be edible when fresh, but why bother? (Note that some critter has nibbled on the mushroom "cap" but didn't care for it.) You can see some bluish color if you look close. The species name "tinctorus" refers to its use as a fabric dye for colors ranging from gold to dark blue depending on the mordant. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2008)

File.Name: 8b01a_8b01-090.jpg
Notes:
42865337 This Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae, order Hemiptera) is stalking the golden Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on a Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2008)

We had 1/4" (less than 1 cm) of welcome rain today. It's a start, and more is expected tonight!
Locality: less than 1 cm) of welcome rain today.  It's a start

Notes:
42865040 Tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) ready to take off on a rainy day in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2008)

I like the little hairs of the leaf which show the depth of field - or lack of it!

File.Name: 8b01c_8b01-060.jpg
Notes: 42864871 I found Termites (order Isoptera) in the rain today on leaves of some sort of Lavender (Lavendula) in the garden. They would land and then try to rub their wings off on the wet foliage. Strange behavior. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b02a_8b02-099.jpg
Notes: 42907727 Ladybugs aren't the only critters in the woods exhibiting mass behavior after the rain this weekend. These are winged Termites (order Isoptera) about to fly away. It's interesting how they are all lined up in the same direction. I don't think these termites invade houses. They live in the woods and don't bother us. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b02b_8b02-106.jpg
Notes: 42907150 A rave of Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) in the woods today. Mating is definately occurring! (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b02c_8b01-088.jpg
Notes:
42906566 Slowly the monster predator walks across the leaf, little knowing the prey are right below its feet...

This is another shot of the Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae, order Hemiptera) in my [Previous] photo, stalking the golden Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on a Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2008)

42957987 This Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae, order Hemiptera) seems to be stalking the golden Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on a Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. I never actually saw it take one, though I waited and rooted for it! (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2008)

I think my son Ryan actually took this photo, but he used my camera so I claim it! As I recall, he took it hand-held with a monopod. *Sigh* I used to be that steady!

I'm jittery with the election tomorrow. It's important that the right person wins!

File.Name: 8b03b_8b01-071.jpg
Notes: 42957670 Free of its wings at last, this once-winged Termite (order Isoptera) looks forward to... what future? I expect a mating swarm of males to follow a winged female, but I'm really confused what these now-wingless termite males are up to. It's perched on some kind of Lavender (Lavendula) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b03c_8b01-064.jpg
Notes: 42957338 I'm intrigued by the winged Termites (order Isoptera) that are so common in the woods after the rain on Saturday. I watched a number of them land on the leaves of some kind of Lavender (Lavendula) in the garden. They would land and then squirm and try to rub their wings off on the wet foliage. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b04a_8b02-116.jpg
Notes:
42999421 Very dark little Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) perched on the fence railing of our garden. It's not really "lizard weather" but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2008)

He's so dark, I had to overexpose the railing to capture the lizard.

Now for the election returns...

File.Name: 8b04b_8b01-047.jpg
Notes:
42999024 There's some gender confusion among early vistors to the ladybug rave which is happening in the canyon!

These are Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) in the woods today. Mating is definately occurring! (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b04c_8b01-038.jpg
Notes:
42998659 One more photo of an Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae, order Hemiptera). This one is resting on a native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis var. redivivus - now given species rank as Styrax redivivus) - in the Storax (Styracaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2008)

D'oh - my camera was still set on ISO 3200 for the school festival on Friday in the dark barn, and I forgot to change it back. This is a problem with shooting JPEG rather than RAW.

File.Name: 8b05a_8b01-058.jpg
Notes: 43052379 Pretty Yellow Jacket wasp in the Vespidae family of the order Hymenoptera grounded by cool weather in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b05b_8a25-020.jpg
Notes:
43052322 I believe this is an unfortunate California Mountain Kingsnake (Lampropeltis zonata) that I found dead along the road in our canyon. Our neighbors must have killed it, but I'll try not to hold it against them. The snakes are considered harmless to humans, and they are uncommon. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2008)

It seems to mimic a venomous coral snake, but there are no coral snakes now overlapping it's range. The evolution of color pattern mimicry might have occurred in the past when its ancestors' distribution overlapped the distribution of coral snakes.

File.Name: 8b06a_8b02-094.jpg
Notes: 43102067 These flower buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) family look fully-formed with holiday colors (and rain drops), but they will wait a few more months before opening for business next spring. It's an interesting strategy. Several plants form their buds early, but many don't. I wonder if there's an advantage, or is this just how it's always been done? (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b07a_8b06-165.jpg
Notes:
43135233 Treebeard and entwife Julie - and a whole bunch of happy Hike Club kids! - at the mouth of the cave I've been featuring in my [Previous] two photos. You can probably guess who I am! (Santa Ynez River, 6 November 2008)

We take our after-school kids exploring in the local mountains every Thursday when the busy school schedule allows. I captured this photo using my camera's self-timer mode. Push the button and run like hell to get in the photo!

File.Name: 8b07b_8b06-150.jpg
Notes: 43134868 There are real pipe-stem stalactites in the cave near the the second crossing of Paradise! I used flash for this photo. See my [Previous] photo for more info. (Santa Ynez River, 6 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b07c_8b06-155.jpg
Notes:
43134699 I took my after-school Hike Club kids to a secret cave in the woods near the second crossing of Paradise. That sounds like a bit of final days/rapture doggerel, but I only mean it's near the second river crossing in the area of the Santa Ynez River known as Paradise.

It's a real "live" cave, with water dripping through the massive sandstone rock above and making real pipe-stem stalactites inside. You can see a few little stalactites in this long-exposure natural light photo, on a tripod of course. You can crawl through the cave through the opening in the distance. (Santa Ynez River, 6 November 2008)

The walls of the cave look water-scoured. Is it maybe an ancient fossilized waterway that's been washed clean of debris?

File.Name: 8b08a_8b08-019.jpg
Notes:
43177777 The ladybug rave in the canyon continues!

These are Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2008)

It's not really a solid ball of ladybugs. The ladybugs are covering a seed head of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family.

Marcoptero asked what camera and lenses I use. It's really not very interesting. I use my Nikon D300 and Nikkor 105 mm micro lens (a few years old, not VR) for almost all the photos that I post here. I usually shoot at ISO 400 for insects, manual focus, RAW or JPEG depending on my free time. I prefer natural light, and I use the +/- exposure buttons a lot! I use an old version of Paint Shop Pro (v7) to do some darkroom stuff with curves and contrast and cropping. That`s it.
CommonNames: Convergent Lady Beetles; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Mint

File.Name: 8b08b_8b08-068.jpg
Notes:
43177018 A different little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on a leaf of a Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. It was interested in the golden Milkweed Aphids but I couldn't catch it in the act of ovipositing. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2008)

This syrphid fly is very similar to the fly in my [Previous] photo, but the bright yellow face is distinctive.
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Butterfly Weed; Milkweed Aphids

File.Name: 8b08c_8b08-015.jpg
Notes: 43176843 Beautiful little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) visiting a new winter flower of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b09a_8b08-009.jpg
Notes:
43210462 Translucent Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) with full pollen baskets working the glandular new winter flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. I love backlight! (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2008)

No chance for photos today - we had a bit of rain last night and gale-force winds all day today. Too windy for me!

File.Name: 8b09b_8b08-002.jpg
Notes: 43209998 I believe this is a Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera. It's hanging out in the garden on a petal of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) trying to get warm after a cold night. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b09c_8b08-042.jpg
Notes:
43209709 The ladybug rave in the canyon continues!

Here is a ring of Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) in the woods yesterday. They are encircling a seed head of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2008)
CommonNames: Convergent Lady Beetles; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Mint

File.Name: 8b10a_8b08-059.jpg
Notes:
43270635 It's the wasps vs. the ants at our hummingbird feeder!

This Yellow Jacket wasp in the Vespidae family of the order Hymenoptera looks ferocious, but the ants usually hold them away. They hold off the humminbirds too. I keep moving the feeder around in the shrub, which usually slows the ants down. The hummers find the feeder right away, but it takes the ants the better part of a day to find it again. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b10b_8b08-030.jpg
Notes: 43270408 I'm proud that I captured this butterfly with my camera, but I'm sorry about the grass stalk right across it - I had no choice! It's a new California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly in the canyon. These winter butterflies are hard to capture. They fly around and rarely land, except occasionally to "puddle" moisture from the soil. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b10c_8b08-012.jpg
Notes:
43270276 Another Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) with full pollen baskets working the glandular new winter flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2008)

Did you know they have such hairy eyes?

File.Name: 8b11a_8b08-039.jpg
Notes:
43316148 This looks like some exotic sushi!

I leave it as a mystery photo until tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b11b_8b08-006.jpg
Notes:
43316089 My photo for Veteran's Day - I reckon this tattered Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly trying to warm up on an oak leaf is a verteran that has been through a lot in its time. We should respect that. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2008)

And we should respect the veterans of our wars, no matter what we feel about those wars!

File.Name: 8b11c_8b08-055.jpg
Notes: 43315959 I think this is the winner in the battle between the wasps vs. the ants at our hummingbird feeder - the ants! Nasty as the wasps are, these Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) are nastier! (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b12a_8b08-049.jpg
Notes: 43361753 Ripe fruit of a native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Honeysuckle (Caprifoliaceae) family catching a beam of sunlight in the shady north-slope woods of our canyon. The berries certainly look edible, but they taste bitter to me. The birds eat them, but they don't really savor them. The connate upper leaves are distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b12b_8b08-076.jpg
Notes: 43361689 Even the skippers are getting scarce as winter approaches, but I found this pretty Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia) butterfly working a beautiful unknown Sage (genus Salvia in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family) in the garden last Saturday. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b12c_8b08-039.jpg
Notes: 43361608 The "exotic sushi" in my [Previous] photo is just the stamens and interesting pollen grains of this California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family. Most of my kids at school guessed correctly right away - they are well trained by now! It's a beautiful late-season hummingbird flower in our mountains that is still going strong. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b13a_8b01-004.jpg
Notes:
43406534 Strange new Oak Gall on a leaf bud of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae family. Is that a new gall starting on bud below? This interesting gall doesn't match any of the desciptions in Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls of California". (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2008)

I got this photo after a recent rain, but the weather has shifted again to the hot and dry side. The sundowner winds are howling tonight. There's another wildfire in Santa Barbara tonight, and our friends' homes are in peril. I wish them well.

File.Name: 8b13b_8b01-013.jpg
Notes: 43406324 Just a week ago we had some rain, and I finally caught one of these Earthstar (Astreus hygrometricus or kin) "mushrooms" emerging from the ground with it's spore mass intact. It pushing up through the leaves like a spike. It will soon lose the spore case and leave its strange starfish-like rind in the woods for me to find and wonder about. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b13c_8b02-091.jpg
Notes:
43406168 It's not as beautiful as the [Previous] photo - but the plant is a cousin, and the berries are more interesting. This is Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) which only grows on the south-facing coastal side of our mountains.

Botanist/explorer David Douglas collected the type specimen in 1831. The inland variety (L. subspicata var. denudata) is more common, but his first named collection is official. This species is rare - the variety is common. It's interesting that the species has these salmon-colored berries, while the variety is red. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2008)
ScientificName: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; L. subspicata; denudata
associatedTaxa: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; L. subspicata; denudata

File.Name: 8b14a_8b13-188.jpg
Notes: 43447014 I took my after-school Hike Club kids to Surf Beach out past Lompoc for the super low -1.4 foot tide on Thursday. It was perfect for skimboarding - and shooting silhouettes against the setting sun! The color grew deeper as the sun got lower. Ryan's skimboard is catching the light just right to shine! (Surf Beach, 13 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b14b_8b13-152.jpg
Notes: 43446928 I took my after-school Hike Club kids to Surf Beach out past Lompoc for the super low -1.4 foot tide on Thursday. It was perfect for skimboarding - and shooting silhouettes against the setting sun! (Surf Beach, 13 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b14c_8b13-118.jpg
Notes: 43446854 I took my after-school Hike Club kids to Surf Beach out past Lompoc for the super low -1.4 foot tide on Thursday. It was perfect for skimboarding - and shooting into the setting sun! (Surf Beach, 13 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b15a_8b15-032.jpg
Notes:
43488464 I guess this "Hover Fly" is hovering!

Hover flies are aka Syrphid Flower Flies (family Syrphidae, order Diptera). I caught this one hovering in a sunbeam in the shady woods. The sun is disappearing below the trees in our north-facing canyon - it's only a month or so until the winter solstice. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b15b_8b15-038.jpg
Notes: 43488185 Some sort of Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, family Gryllidae, order Orthoptera) hiding in a California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family. I'm surprised to find it. I haven't heard any crickets for weeks. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2008)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: 8b15c_8b15-028.jpg
Notes: 43487999 Some sort of plain brown "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) looking at me in the woods today - though the 4-cell antennae are pretty. The plant is a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Laurel (Lauraceae) plant family. It has a very pungent taste - I don't expect to find any predators on it. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b16a_8b16-076.jpg
Notes:
43531550 Beautiful little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working flowers of some kind of Radish (?) (genus Raphanus in the family Brassicaceae) in the garden. The plant is not a weed, though I can't remember where it came from. The little fly seems so delicate! (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2008)

Darn, the afternoon light was fading (at 1:30!) when I got this photo. This is the dark time of the year.
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Raphanus in the family Brassicaceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Raphanus in the family Brassicaceae

Notes:
43531104 Look close!

Some kind of Wild Bee (superfamily Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) is working this pretty flower of late-season native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2008)

This may be a Potter Bee (family Megachilidae). I may have photos of their nests:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/23078194

File.Name: 8b16c_8b15-030.jpg
Notes:
43530360 This looks like an old galley ship, but it's really just a fly in flight, flashing its wings with every beat!

It took a few shots to get the exposure right on my [Previous] photo of a hovering "Hover fly" aka Syrphid Flower Flies (family Syrphidae, order Diptera). This photo is one of the goofs, but look!

From the EXIF data, the exposure is 1/25 second at F/8. In 1/25" second, the fly beat its wings 11 times. Therefore it is beating its wings at 11*25 = 275 beats per second.

It's a goof, but I learned something I could not see! (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b17a_8b15-008.jpg
Notes: 43577697 For some reason, I really like this photo of two very different flies (order Diptera) resting on a dead oak leaf in the woods. The larger fly is probably a Scavenger Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. The smaller fly is something different. I wonder what they think of each other in their tiny fly minds? (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b17b_8b15-039.jpg
Notes:
43577363 Another photo of the Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, family Gryllidae, order Orthoptera) hiding in a California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family. I disturbed it trying to get it to pose, and it moved to a more open branch. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2008)

My original photo of this cricket is here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/43488185

associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: 8b17c_8b16-058.jpg
Notes:
43577128 Another Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working the morning flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. The face is a little too dark, but the right wing is perfect! (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2008)

This time of year, I usually begin my day of leisure photographing the bees on the Chaparral Currant. They are available just out the door, and they catch the morning sun just right!

File.Name: 8b18a_8b16-071.jpg
Notes:
43622327 (Milkweed series 3 of 3)

Shazam! The seed head of the Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden has exploded open. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b18b_8b15-048.jpg
Notes:
43622124 (Milkweed series 2 of 3)

The first seed is almost free of this Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed starting to split apart in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b18c_8b15-047.jpg
Notes:
43621968 (Milkweed series 1 of 3)

A seedpod of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed getting ready to split apart in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b19a_8b15-017.jpg
Notes: 43666800 Almost ripe fruit of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii in the Heath (Ericaceae) family) tree growing in the canyon. The textured fruit are edible, but kind of mealy. The birds like them. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b19b_8b15-012.jpg
Notes: 43666708 This is a seed head of a composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family growing in the canyon. The plant gets its name from from its way of following roads and trails. Though often considered a weed, it is a native species that competes very well with naturalized weeds in disturbed areas. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b19c_8b16-066.jpg
Notes: 43666501 Ripe Thanksgiving nuts of a deciduous native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis var. redivivus - now given species rank as Styrax redivivus) - in the Storax (Styracaceae) family. I haven't actually tasted these fine looking nuts, but the birds and other critters of the woods eat them all pretty fast. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2008)
ScientificName: Styrax officinalis; redivivus; Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae
associatedTaxa: Styrax officinalis; redivivus; Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae

File.Name: 8b20a_8b16-075.jpg
Notes: 43712487 I believe these are new flowers of Salvia holwayi in the Mint Family (Labiatae). It's native to the highlands of Guatamala and Chiapas, but it does well in our garden - unless it gets cut back by heavy frost, below about 15 degrees fahrenheit. Our resident female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) perches in the oaks overhead and works these flowers every day. I'll try to get a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b20b_8b15-050.jpg
Notes: 43712382 New flowers of true native Yerba Buena (Satureja douglasii in the Mint Family (Labiatae)). This fragrant plant is a native in the canyon, though this one is taken from a cutting, and it's now doing well in our garden. It seems to be saying thank you for the bird dropping! (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b20c_8b15-015.jpg
Notes: 43712257 There are still a few flowers of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family growing in the woods as we move into winter. The flowers may have outlasted the pollinators this year - insects are scarce. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b21a_8b20-534.jpg
Notes:
43755959 This is interesting. The photo shows a Banded Argiope (Argiope trifasciata) garden spider in the school garden wrapping a "package" - probably an unfortunate Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) that got tangled in its web. But sitting on the package are two different kinds of Fly in the order Diptera, one just above the spider's leg and one just below.

I guess this preditor has preditors, and that the flies are trying to lay eggs that will consume the spider's prey before the spider's own young can. But maybe the prey is wrapped so tight that the flies can't get in? Will the different fly larvae attack each other? (Santa Ynez Valley, 20 November 2008)

I've had three *l*o*n*g* days of teacher-parent-student conferences at school, but I had a few short breaks to take photos in Julie's school garden. Conferences went OK, though I could barely mouth my words by the end of the day. This is NOT recommended for recovering stroke victims!

Yahoo, it's the start of a week of Thanksgiving vacation!

File.Name: 8b21b_8b19-518.jpg
Notes:
43755343 Western Spotted Cucumber Beetle (Diabrotica undecimpunctata in the family Chrysomelidae of the order Coleoptera) exploring a Winter Squash (Cucurbita) flower in the school garden. The large flower is a vast landscape for this little beetle to explore! (Santa Ynez Valley, 19 November 2008)

By sheer coincidence, my friend Gary across the country features the same (or similar) insect in his photo today:
   http://www.fotolog.com/garyblau/28942038

File.Name: 8b21c_8b19-513.jpg
Notes:
43755069 "My what a long proboscis you have!"

This is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) with full pollen baskets working a composite flower of Common Sow Thistle (Sonchus oleraceus) in the school garden. (Santa Ynez Valley, 19 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b22a_8b22-026.jpg
Notes: 43796818 I found this cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on an oak leaf today. It's tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b22b_8b22-031.jpg
Notes:
43796488 Flies eyes...

... are amazing! This photo shows some of the thousands of six-sided ommatidia or "simple eyes" that make up the fly's compound eyes like a retina. The fly is a Scavenger Fly in the family Sarcophagidae of the order Diptera. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b22c_8b22-002.jpg
Notes: 43796352 A dandelion-like seed of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family caught on early morning flower buds of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. The seeds are everywhere. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2008)
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: 8b23a_8b23-056.jpg
Notes:
43831408 This is a tiny immature spider-like Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae, order Hemiptera). I gently herded it back and forth along the oak twig until I got this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2008)

This started as a nice day, but it's ending nasty. I hope this is our next rain storm settling in. We need it!

File.Name: 8b23b_8b23-050.jpg
Notes: 43830847 It was windy today, so I went looking for something that wasn't blowing in the wind. I found these strange galls on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae family. This interesting gall doesn't match any of the desciptions in Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls of California", but it looks like the gall of a Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus chrystallinus) without the hairs. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b23c_8b23-057.jpg
Notes: 43830090 Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) for Thanksgiving - yum! I guess I shouldn't be surprised - they seem to appear every year for Thanksgiving, but it has seemed hopeless this year, with record heat and sundowner winds for the past two weeks. Somehow they know that it's time to fruit. They are growing from an old stump of a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Laurel (Lauraceae) plant family. The mushrooms get their name from the color, not the taste. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b24a_8b24-112.jpg
Notes:
43884569 This interesting plant has no chlorophyll - it grows as an obligate root parasite and has a fungal connection with another flowering plant nearby. The "plant" is nothing but a flower stalk. I had to get out my serious books for this one! I believe this is California Broomrape (Orobanche californica ssp. feudgei). (Santa Ynez River, 24 November 2008)

I did a serious hike today beyond Red Rock to Gibraltor Dam and back on the high road. Tim and I were scouting the trail for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society hike we're leading in two weeks. I found the Broomrape growing right on the dirt road trail near the last (and best) swimming hole before Gibraltor Dam.

I'll post more photos from our hike tomorrow. I'm tired!

File.Name: 8b24b_8b22-025.jpg
Notes: 43884245 This is another photo of the cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) I found on an oak leaf two days ago. They have amazing eyes that can see all around. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b24c_8b23-053.jpg
Notes: 43884177 Another photo of the immature Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae, order Hemiptera) that I found yesterday on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). You can see its long proboscis folded under its head. It has long antennae, which give it a spider-like look. Maybe that's camouflage? (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b25a_8b25-168.jpg
Notes:
43922646 A different Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae, order Hemiptera) with a winged abdomen in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree today. You can see ite long proboscis folded under its head. (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2008)

Yesterday I thought a storm was approaching - nope. Today, it's definately moving in. It's starting to rain - blessed rain - as I type!

File.Name: 8b25b_8b24-093.jpg
Notes:
43921782 From my hike yesterday - brilliant trunks of a Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree, with leaves starting to turn. (Santa Ynez River, 24 November 2008)

I did a serious hike yesterday beyond Red Rock to Gibraltor Dam and back on the high road. Tim and I were scouting the trail for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society hike we're leading in two weeks. I'm still sore today!

File.Name: 8b25c_8b24-071.jpg
Notes:
43921405 From my hike yesterday - beautiful contrast between the bright Fremont Cottenwood (Populus fremontii) trees showing some fall color and the charcoal-dark face of Camuesa Peak in the background above the Santa Ynez River. (Santa Ynez River, 24 November 2008)

I did a serious hike yesterday beyond Red Rock to Gibraltor Dam and back on the high road. Tim and I were scouting the trail for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society hike we're leading in two weeks. I'm still sore today!

File.Name: 8b26a_8b26-173.jpg
Notes:
43973545 D'oh!

This unfortunate Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera seems to have landed too hard on a cactus spine. It's still alive in the photo, but struggling. I went back to check on it after dark, and it's dead. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2008)

Note the raindrops! We've had about two inches (5 cm) of rain so far. It's been a good gentle soak. I don't believe these have been any problems with mud slides in the burn areas.

File.Name: 8b26b_8b25-156.jpg
Notes: 43973049 This tiny white-spored Mycena mushroom was determined to fruit even before the rain started yesterday afternoon! It's growing from a patch of yarrow in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b26c_8b25-171.jpg
Notes:
43972913 Another photo of the Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae, order Hemiptera) with a winged abdomen in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2008)

I don't know what insects do in the rain - I guess they just curl up under a leaf and wait. That does look like a pretty good leaf to curl up in!

File.Name: 8b27a_8b26-198.jpg
Notes:
44016434 The birds are thankful for the good crop of Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood) (Heteromeles arbutifolia) in the Rose (Rosaceae) family berries in the woods this year! The smaller shrubs in the woods are doing especially well. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2008)

I'm thankful for the rain - not too hard, just right.

I'm also thankful because I'm still able to take these photos every day after my stroke last summer. And I am still a teacher!

We're off to my Dad's tomorrow for our usual "day after" Thanksgiving dinner. Most of all, I'm thankful that we can be together again!

File.Name: 8b27b_8b27-240.jpg
Notes:
44015584 Author J.G. Ballard once wrote a series of sci-fi books describing the end of the world in different ways. My favorite was "The Crystal World" - the world ends by crystalline growths that won't stop growing until they take over everything!

This looks right out of the book, but the "crystals" are rubbery rather than hard. I reckon this is a "Jelly Fungus" (Tremellales). It's growing on a small Peach tree (Prunus persica) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2008)
ScientificName: Tremellales). It's growing on a small Peach tree (Prunus persica
associatedTaxa: Tremellales). It's growing on a small Peach tree (Prunus persica

File.Name: 8b27c_8b27-201.jpg
Notes:
44014396 "Clutching at the dagger in my heart..."

I don't mean to make fun. This unfortunate Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera landed too hard on a cactus spine just off our porch. It's skewered by the cactus spine just like the raindrop in the foreground. I thought it was dead last night, but it was still kicking this morning. I removed it from the spine that did it in, and it's still kicking in a potted plant nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2008)

It's a terrible fate to be "stuck like a bug" in the rain, but I have to chuckle. I can just imagine the wasp going "D'oh - fucked up again!

File.Name: 8b28a_8b27-223.jpg
Notes:
44036894 I believe this pretty gall with raindrops is the gall of a Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera. It's growing on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae family. Most gall-forming wasps are quite picky about the species of oak they inhabit, especially whether it is a Black Oak (like this) or a White Oak (like Scrub Oak). White Oak galls are usually more interesting, but this one is an exception. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2008)

I'm posting early today. We're soon off to my Dad's for our usual "day after" Thanksgiving dinner. Happy turkey day!
CommonNames: Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) in the family Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) in the family Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
Families: Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) in the family Cynipidae; Fagaceae

File.Name: 8b28b_8b27-229.jpg
Notes: 44036037 A Fly in the order Diptera trying to dry out after two days of rain on a leaf of Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the canyon. I reckon its a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae) because it is so bristly. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b28c_8b27-218.jpg
Notes: 44035734 I found this soggy Agaricus mushroom as the rain was clearing out yesterday. The first real rain and this mushroom is already old! (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b29a_8b29-044.jpg
Notes: 44100505 A troop of little white-spored Mycena mushrooms growing under a canopy of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae family in the canyon. The mushrooms are so tiny it really doesn't matter if they are edible or not. But they sure are beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 29 November 2008) The exposure was 1/10 second @ f/22 @ ISO 200. Sometimes you have to say to hell with diffraction effects and go with depth of field!

File.Name: 8b29b_8b29-026.jpg
Notes: 44100191 Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working new flowers of native Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) along East Camino Cielo road. The shrub is a busy ROAR of insects - and a few hummingbirds - that have been waiting through the long dry summer. New manzanita flowers is a big deal in our woods! (San Marcos Pass, 29 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b29c_8b29-039.jpg
Notes:
44099724 This Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae, order Hemiptera) is under the same cup-shaped oak leaf where I last found it before the good rain this week. That settles one stumper: where insects go when it rains! (San Marcos Pass, 29 November 2008)

See my "before the rain" photo of this critter at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/43972913

File.Name: 8b30a_8b30-073.jpg
Notes:
44147050 The Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) are "converging" again in the canyon. I reckon it's a ladybug rave! (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2008)

Why do the ladybugs keep coming back to the same place in the woods year after year?

File.Name: 8b30b_8b30-057.jpg
Notes: 44146729 Beautiful Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushrooms coming up in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2008)

File.Name: 8b30c_8b30-069.jpg
Notes: 44146474 I know, I've shown this particular Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae, order Hemiptera) several times before, but here it is one more time. It hasn't budged since before the last rain, but it looks so cool perched on this Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae family with its mighty probiscis! (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2008)

File.Name: 8c01a_8b19-507.jpg
Notes:
44195858 Pretty dandelion-like seeds of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family taking wing. This is in the wild area of Julie's garden at school. (Santa Ynez Valley, 19 November 2008)

I'm really tired tonight after doing my grades and comments - the down side of being a teacher! One photo will do, and an old one at that - before the rain. I'm going to eat something and go to bed!

File.Name: 8c02a_8b30-071.jpg
Notes:
44239096 It looks like a ladybug lollipop!

The Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) are "converging" again in the canyon. This gathering is on an old seed head of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. Mating is definately occurring! (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2008)
CommonNames: Convergent Lady Beetles; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Mint

File.Name: 8c02b_8b29-031.jpg
Notes:
44238806 This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) is working new flowers of native Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) along East Camino Cielo road.

This little bee is multitasking! It's sucking new white pollen from the flower while it passes old pollen down the line to its pollen baskets on its hind legs. I don't multitask very well myself, so I'm impressed! (San Marcos Pass, 29 November 2008)

File.Name: 8c03a_8b27-231.jpg
Notes: 44289293 This grass looks like a pretty native bunchgrass, but it's really invasive Smilo Grass (Piptatherum miliaceum, family Poaceae) - introduced from the Mediterranean region, and doing very well this season in our canyon along the road. It's hard not to overexpose grass plumes, but I think I got this one right. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2008)

File.Name: 8c03b_8b27-238.jpg
Notes: 44289026 Here are different plant galls on a leaf of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek. I believe these are the galls of the Willow Tube Gall Midge fly in the genus Iteomyia of the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. The galls have a little opening at the apex, and only occur on the undersides of the leaves. They'll soon fall with the leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2008)
CommonNames: Arroyo Willow; Willow Tube Gall Midge
ScientificName: Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Iteomyia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Iteomyia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: 8c03c_8b29-036.jpg
Notes:
44288766 One of those tiny Flies (order Diptera) that dance in the sunbeams after a rain just happened to land in front of my camera, and I took the photo. The spine of the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf is only about 1 mm long. You can see the stellate (star-like) hairs on the leaf! This is a tight crop of a near 1:1 macro photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 November 2008)

What amazes me is that the tiny flies are just like the bigger flies - only tinier!

File.Name: 8c04a_8b29-015.jpg
Notes:
44333135 Some kind of pretty Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working new flowers of native Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family along West Camino Cielo road in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 29 November 2008)

Some kind of pretty Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working new flowers of native Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family along West Camino Cielo road in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 29 November 2008)

I'm a little rusty on my wing venation recognition, but this syrphid doesn't seem to have a "spurious vein"??
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Big-berry Manzanita; Heath; Syrphid Flower Fly; Big-berry Manzanita; Heath
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Syrphidae; Diptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Syrphidae; Diptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: 8c04b_8b15-005.jpg
Notes:
44332920 These are the little galls of the Pumpkin Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus minusculus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera. They are only about 1 mm across, so the wasps must be tiny! Most galls drop to the ground in the fall. Adult wasps emerge in spring, and the cycle starts again. What a life! (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2008)

I'm working on my school science fair project on local plant galls. I'm the teacher, but I always do my own project. These tiny critters that induce genetic changes in their hosts for their own ends are fascinating!

File.Name: 8c04c_8b25-162.jpg
Notes: 44332721 These strange growths on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf are "Erineum Galls" caused by tiny Live Oak Erineum Mites (Eriophyes mackiei). The undersides of the bumps on the leaf are filled with rusty-brown hairs - modified plant hairs - where the mites live. (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2008)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Live Oak Erineum Mites

File.Name: 8c05a_8c04-112.jpg
Notes: 44374449 My after-school Hike Club kids in silhouette on the top of the Lizard's Mouth in the Santa Ynez Mountains. It's a glorious place! (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c05b_8c04-103.jpg
Notes: 44374378 I took my after-school Hike Club kids to Lizard Mouth in the Santa Ynez Mountains - there are great rocks to climb! - and we found this memorial carved in the sandstone rock for Nicholas Samuel Markowitz who was murdered here in the mountains on August 8, 2000. It's a sad story, told in the movie "Alpha Dog". I don't know who carved the memorial. They did a good job. The red line adds poignancy, like a tear. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c05c_8b24-131.jpg
Notes: 44373642 I'm leading a nature walk for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society up the trail past Red Rock about 5 miles to Gibraltor Dam. I got this photo of the dam about two weeks ago when I scouted the trail with my partner Tim Matthews. I'm posting this photo now because I'll be too tired tomorrow night! (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2008)

File.Name: 8c06a_8c06-040.jpg
Notes: 44427302 Beautiful Fremont Cottenwood (Populus fremontii) trees showing fall colors - with a perfect reflection. I led a hike for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society today up the trail past Red Rock along the Santa Ynez River. The river is not flowing, but there is still water in the pools. Note the hiker - one of our group exporing by himself - in the center of the photo for scale. (Santa Ynez River, 6 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c06b_8c06-059.jpg
Notes: 44415623 I wasn't really set up for my usual macro shots on my hike for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society today up the trail past Red Rock, but I couldn't resist trying to get a photo of this Common Spreadwing (Lestes disjunctus) dragonfly in the family Lestdae of the order Odonata. True dragonflies hold their wings horizontal at rest and true damselflies hold their wings vertical. But these critters are in between. (Santa Ynez River, 6 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c06c_8c06-073.jpg
Notes: 44415425 I led a hike for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society today up the trail past Red Rock about 3 miles to Gibraltor Dam and back on the "high road". I got this photo of the dam with a perfect reflecting pool. We saw Western Pond Turtles basking on the rocks with binoculars - maybe they were mating? (Santa Ynez River, 6 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c07a_8c07-134.jpg
Notes: 44454306 Looking very close at a beautiful Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly parked in an oak tree on the woods - just because I can! It's a Skimmer - family Libellulidae, in the order Odonata. It must be grounded by the cold weather. It let me get very close without budging. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2008)
CommonNames: Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly parked in an oak tree on the woods - just because I can! It's a Skimmer

File.Name: 8c07b_8c07-128.jpg
Notes: 44454244 Beautiful Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly parked in an oak tree on the woods. It's a Skimmer - family Libellulidae, in the order Odonata. It must be grounded by the cold weather. It let me get very close without budging. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2008)
CommonNames: Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly parked in an oak tree on the woods. It's a Skimmer

File.Name: 8c07c_8c07-139.jpg
Notes:
44454197 I believe this is a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly parked on an oak tree on the woods. It's a Skimmer - family Libellulidae, in the order Odonata. It must be grounded by the cold weather. It let me get very close without budging. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2008)

Hah - this motionless dragonfly was hard enough to notice, and I almost overlooked the second dragonfly in the background. Can you see it now?
CommonNames: Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly parked on an oak tree on the woods. It's a Skimmer

File.Name: 8c08a_8c07-117.jpg
Notes:
44496754 Most insects were grounded by the cold weather on Sunday, but there are always ladybugs to photograph!

The Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) are still "converging" in the canyon. I like this photo with the dull cloudy day light and background. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c08b_8c07-119.jpg
Notes: 44496457 The only insects I found on the wing yesterday were these pretty little Syrphid Flower Flies (family Syrphidae, order Diptera). There were quite a few of them buzzing around trying to get warm on the fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2008)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Flies; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 8c08c_8c07-155.jpg
Notes:
44496155 Looking very close at the back of the head of a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly (family Libellulidae, order Odonata) parked in an oak tree on the woods yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2008)

This photo shows the mysterious neck joint of the dragonfly. It also shows some of the many thousands of six-sided ommatidia or "simple eyes" that make up the dragonfly's compound eyes like a retina. The little eyes remind me of the shadow mask of a TV set or the even-smaller etched-in-silicon mask of a digital camera sensor. Maybe that's an appropriate comparison.

File.Name: 8c09a_8c07-104.jpg
Notes: 44547292 I don't need to get out my books to know what this beautiful mushroom is! It's a perfect Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom in the woods. Yum! It's one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings! (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2008) This specimen is unusually pale - but it was tasty! This specimen is unusually pale - but it was tasty! It has smooth folds under the cap instead of sharp-edged gills like most mushrooms. It's easy to recognize once you've seen it.

File.Name: 8c09b_8c07-095.jpg
Notes:
44547186 Pretty little dark-spored mushooms coming up with the new herbs along our road. I don't know what it is - or much care. It's another of the LBMs - "little brown mushrooms" - in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2008)

Give me some time to get the names right... I've got to get back in mushroom-mode after many months in insect-mode!

File.Name: 8c09c_8c07-089.jpg
Notes:
44546958 Our beautiful Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) have already turned into a river of maggot infested slime. I guess that's why they call them MUSHrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2008)

You can see what they used to look like here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/43830090

File.Name: 8c10a_8c07-145.jpg
Notes:
44594881 I'm pretty sure this elegant mushroom is a Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) in the Amanita (Amanitaceae) family. The amanitas are our most elegant mushrooms - and the most complex. This mushroom is probably poisonous with the same interesting toxins as Amanita muscaria and A. pantherina. It's growing from a patch of native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c10b_8c07-110.jpg
Notes: 44594782 At first I thought these little mushrooms in the woods were just more little Mycena 'srooms, but looking closer at the photo I believe they are Chocolate Marasmius (Marasmius fuscopurpureus or kin) mushrooms. *Sigh* Every year I have to learn the mushrooms all over again! (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c10c_8c07-108.jpg
Notes: 44594618 Perfect Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom pushing up a "mushrump" in the woods. Yum! It has smooth folds under the cap instead of sharp-edged gills like most mushrooms. It's easy to recognize once you've seen it. It was delicious! (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c11a_8c06-052.jpg
Notes: 44639405 They're hardly showing fall colors yet, but these leaves of a Fremont Cottenwood (Populus fremontii) tree - and the shadows - along the river are beautiful. (Santa Ynez River, 6 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c11b_8c06-051.jpg
Notes: 44639024 Tim and I led a hike for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society up the river trail past Red Rock along the Santa Ynez River. The river is mostly dry at the dry end of the year, but the fall colors were outstanding. The golden trees are mostly native Fremont Cottenwood (Populus fremontii) trees. (Santa Ynez River, 6 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c11c_8c07-148.jpg
Notes:
44638732 I believe this elegant mushroom is a Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) in the Amanita (Amanitaceae) family, though it's pallid rather than yellow in color. I show the same mushroom "in situ" in my [Previous] photo. It has proper "volva" or "death cup" at the base, but it disintegrated when I picked the mushroom, and only the rim remains. (I found pieces of the universal veil in the ground.)

This mushroom is probably poisonous with the same interesting toxins as Amanita muscaria and A. pantherina. It's growing in the woodland under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2008)
CommonNames: Jonquil Amanita; Amanita; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Amanita gemmata; Amanitaceae; Amanita muscaria; A. pantherina; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Amanita gemmata; Amanitaceae; Amanita muscaria; A. pantherina; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 8c12a_8c06-045.jpg
Notes: 44684325 A perfect reflection-mandala of two little Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) trees growing on the rocks above a pool in the Santa Ynez River. Try turning your head 90 degree sideways to the left! (Santa Ynez River, 6 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c12b_8c06-080.jpg
Notes:
44684097 The most unexpected plant I found on our hike with the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society up the river trail past Red Rock, was no doubt this native Southern California Black Walnut (Juglans californica) tree with walnuts. We found it on our way back along the "high trail" growing in a side-canyon that crossed our trail. And darned if there wasn't another one growing further along the trail. Clouds had moved in by then. (Santa Ynez River, 6 December 2008)
