I usually associate these trees with Chumash Native American sites, but here it is in the wild. The walnuts are edible - and tasty - if you can get them before the worms do!

File.Name: 8c12c_8c06-033.jpg
Notes:
44683272 These are autumn leaves (or rather leaflets) of deciduous False Indigo (Amorpha californica) in the Pea Family (Fabaceae). It's an uncommon shrub in our mountains that's growing on a sandy flat along the river. We found it on our Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society hike up the river past Redrock last Saturday. (Santa Ynez River, 6 December 2008)

False indigo is said in every book to be the host plant for the California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterflies, but I don't believe it. The butterflies are common, but the plants are uncommon. I have many photos of the butterflies, start here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/39016879
CommonNames: False Indigo; Pea Family; California Dogface

File.Name: 8c13a_8c13-015.jpg
Notes:
44722594 Don't believe everything you read on mushrooms in the woods - or in the newspapers or TV or the Web!

I scratched this message with a twig on the blue-staining cap of this Boletus satanas mushroom in the woods growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the canyon. This is Satan's Bolete mushroom that is NOT EDIBLE. There are other interesting mushrooms that turn blue in the woods, but this one is definately poisonous. Pretty though! (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2008)

We had a bit of rain this morning, but then it turned very windy and cold. Mushrooms were about the only critters in the woods not blowing around!

File.Name: 8c13b_8c13-027.jpg
Notes: 44722145 The woods are coming alive after recent rains! These are tiny spore-bearing Moss (Bryophyta) sporophytes growing on a rock in the canyon. They look like little microphones or... well, use your imagination! (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c13c_8c13-004.jpg
Notes: 44721704 A new clump of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in the family Tricholomataceae growing with Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae family in the canyon today. These are growing from the stump of a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree. The mushrooms are edible if you get them before the worms do! They get their name from the color, not the taste. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2008)
CommonNames: Honey Mushrooms; Wild Blackberry; California Bay
ScientificName: Armillariella mellea; Tricholomataceae growing with Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Umbellularia californica
associatedTaxa: Armillariella mellea; Tricholomataceae growing with Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Umbellularia californica

File.Name: 8c14a_8c1-056.jpg
Notes: 44767496 Interesting "Milky Cap" - I guess it is the Golden Milky Cap () mushroom. The cap is zoned concentrically with yellow and orange bands. These mushrooms contain white latex that appears when you break the gills, as you can see in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c14b_8c13-021.jpg
Notes: 44766877 Pretty little Puffball fungus in the woods yesterday - maybe Bovista plumbea - with raindrops. The coarse sandy soil is really broken down sandstone. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c14c_8c14-090.jpg
Notes: 44766408 I guess these are the Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus or kin) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the canyon. Like all boletes - and like my [Previous] photo - they have sponge-like pores underneath instead of gills. The mushrooms are edible if you find fresh specimens, but they are quickly parasitized by the Bolete Eater (Hypomyces chrysospermum) fungus that seems to prefer this species among all boletes, and turns them into bright yellow mush. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2008)
CommonNames: Oak-Loving Bolete; Coast Live Oak; Bolete Eater
ScientificName: Boletus dryophilus; Quercus agrifolia; Hypomyces chrysospermum
associatedTaxa: Boletus dryophilus; Quercus agrifolia; Hypomyces chrysospermum

File.Name: 8c15a_8c14-063.jpg
Notes:
44813785 Pretty wasp gall - like a piece of candy stuck to this leaf in the woods!

I believe this is the summer gall of a Disk Gall Wasp (Andricus parmula in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera) on a twig of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae family. To quote my book - Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls of California" (2006) - "Little is known about this species' biology." Here's a science fair project ready to roll! (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2008)

I understand that galls are "tumorlike growths of plant tissue produced by a host plant in responce to chemical and/or mechanical stimuli of invading organisms." But why are they so darned pretty?

We had over 3 inches (8 cm) of rain last night! There will be mushrooms for Christmas!
ScientificName: Andricus parmula; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus parmula; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: 8c15b_8c14-069.jpg
Notes: 44812944 This is an insect gall masquerading as an acorn. I believe it's the gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera) on a twig of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae family. It's old. The hole is either where the wasp came out or some other preditor went in. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2008)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: 8c15c_8c13-003.jpg
Notes:
44812352 The Grateful Dead had the "wall of sound", but we have a wall of mushrooms!

This is the new clump of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in the family Tricholomataceae growing with Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae family in the canyon today. These are growing from the stump of a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree. The mushrooms are edible if you get them before the worms do! They get their name from the color, not the taste. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2008)
CommonNames: Honey Mushrooms; Wild Blackberry; California Bay
ScientificName: Armillariella mellea; Tricholomataceae growing with Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Umbellularia californica
associatedTaxa: Armillariella mellea; Tricholomataceae growing with Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Umbellularia californica

File.Name: 8c16a_8b29-004.jpg
Notes:
44858224 These flowers look like they're wearing mittens on their anthers!

There are new flowers in the winter woods, but this is one of the old flowers that is still hanging on despite the cold weather. This is a late-season native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. There are still a few of these flowers growing in rocky places along the road. There were no insects visiting them that I could see. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2008)

We have a 60% chance of snow tonight. I'm hoping for a snow day tomorrow!
CommonNames: Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Sunflower

File.Name: 8c16b_8c14-040.jpg
Notes: 44857452 This is exciting! Here are the first new flowers of native BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family are already appearing! I don't expect the mass flowering until February. Some years they cover the mountains like snow that you can see from the beach. Maybe they'll be early this year. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c16c_8c07-151.jpg
Notes:
44857054 Like little baby birds demanding to be fed, or voices in a choir...

These are new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family. They are an important hummingbird flower at a time when nothing else is available - though the flowers are not long, red tubes like most hummingbird flowers. My hummingbird feeders are staying full for days at a time. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c17a_8c14-049.jpg
Notes:
44905734 I didn't know what I had!

I found this remarkable white-capped bolete mushroom last weekend, and didn't realize it's the White King Bolete (Boletus barrowsii) in the family Boletaceae, so I didn't collect it for dinner. It's said to be one of the finest tasting mushrooms. It was growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri) near home - it's impossible to say which tree it was associated with. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2008)

The combination of white cap, reticulate stem, and sponge-like yellowish "gills" is distinctive. I'll have a close shot of the reticulate stem tomorrow.

We never got our snow last night, but maybe tonight!
CommonNames: White King Bolete; Coast Live Oak; Coulter Pines
ScientificName: Boletus barrowsii; Boletaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Pinus coulteri
associatedTaxa: Boletus barrowsii; Boletaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Pinus coulteri

File.Name: 8c17b_8c14-074.jpg
Notes:
44904896 New flower buds of a California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Laurel (Lauraceae) plant family growing in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2008)

Have you ever accidentally eaten a "Bay Leaf" in a stew? *Yuck* Our native bay trees are like that only more so. The trees are related to avocados, and they look like it.

File.Name: 8c17c_8c14-080.jpg
Notes: 44904554 Interesting (not beautiful) new flower buds of Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Silk-Tassel (Garryaceae) family. They'll soon be open. This native shrub is "dioecious" with separate male and female plants, and the males usually appear first. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c18a_8c13-036.jpg
Notes: 44949719 Pretty mushrooms on the edge of the garden. I guess these are the Rufous Candy Cap (Lactarius rufulus). They are said to be edible and good, but I've never tried them. They have a mild odor instead of the strong maple syrup odor of the true Candy Cap (L. fragilis). (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c18b_8c13-006.jpg
Notes: 44949501 I guess this is a pretty little Lepiota mushroom coming up through fallen leaves of Bay and Live Oak in the woods. I don't know anything about it. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c18c_8c14-053.jpg
Notes: 44949252 Close detail of the "finely reticulate stem" of the White King Bolete (Boletus barrowsii) in the family Boletaceae that I show in my [Previous] photo. This refers to the pattern of fine net-like ridges on the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c19a_8c18-155.jpg
Notes: 44988885 Two of my after-school Hike Club boys on a stone chimney at "the castle" with the beautiful sunset behind them. I think that's Hunter on top. "Knapp's Castle" is the ruin of a mountain mansion that was destroyed by a wildfire in the 1930s. It's also the site of some fabulous solstice drum circles! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c19b_8c18-141.jpg
Notes: 44988529 These are a few of my after-school Hike Club girls standing on a stone wall at Knapps's Castle - the ruins of a mountain mansion that was destroyed by a wildfire in the 1930s. I live a little to the left of the first ridge that you can see on San Marcos pass. Lake Cachuma is in the distance. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c19c_8c18-177.jpg
Notes: 44988228 "The Castle" is Knapps's Castle - the ruins of a mountain mansion that was destroyed by a wildfire in the 1930s. Only stone walls and chimneys remain. Here it is catching the last glow from the setting sun, with snow in the San Rafael Mountains mountains beyond. I brought my after-school hike club kids here yesterday. It's great fun to play in the ruins - and the best view of the backcountry! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c20a_8c20-012.jpg
Notes:
45021038 Pretty little "Fairy Fingers" (Clavaria vermicularis or kin) mushrooms growing in a natural compost pile of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2008)

I'm on vacation, yahoo! It's been a busy time getting here, including performing a wedding ceremony for Dawn and Eric - fellow DMS teachers - yesterday! Two weeks to take photos in the most interesting time of year, with a (very) slight time-out for Christmas.

File.Name: 8c20b_8c20-007.jpg
Notes: 45020384 Tiny immature white-spored Mycena mushrooms growing on an overturned "mushrump" in the woods. They work in the woods as decomposers of the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c20c_8c18-164.jpg
Notes: 45020102 My after-school hike club kids jumping in the frigid air at the castle! See my [Previous] photos for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c21a_29AUG98-pic00010.jpg
Notes:
45063134 The Salamander Cave - aka Arrowhead Springs - is a remarkable native American Chumash pictograph site half-way down the Santa Ynez Mountains towards Goleta. These are prehistoric paintings on rock, not petroglyphs. The images probably have something to do with the winter solstice, but I don't have a clue what they mean. It's an appropriate photo for the winter solstice day. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 1998)

This panel has always seemed remarkable to me because it makes artistic sense. Campbell Grant's reconstrictuction of the painting still hangs in the Goleta library (I think). The paintings overhang a waterfall, but they are are out of reach of the water. Alas, the site was in the middle of the "Gap Fire" last summer, and I don't know if it survived. The area is still closed to entry after the fire.

I dug in the archives today for a winter solstice photo. I took this photo many years ago with my very first digital camera - an Olympus 320L.
Locality: I think).  The paintings overhang a waterfall

File.Name: 8c21b_29AUG98-pic00011.jpg
Notes:
45061904 I guided Drew Richard ("Coastal Eddie") to the Salamander Cave - aka Arrowhead Springs - 10+ years ago to photograph the rock art. It's a remarkable native American Chumash pictograph site. These are prehistoric paintings, not chippings. Here is Drew getting a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 1998)

I took this photo many years ago with my very first digital camera - an Olympus 320L.

File.Name: 8c21c_8c21-069.jpg
Notes:
45060828 I almost forgot - today is a special day for the Earth - the solstice day, the shortest day of the year. Even the local shooting range in the mountains is closed! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2008)

It's the dark day of the year, the shortest day here in the northern hemisphere. It's the day when priests and shamans do their stuff to bring back the Sun. There will be drum circles in the mountains tonight!

I'll celebrate the solstice with two 10+ year old digital photos that I (finally) found in the archives.

File.Name: 8c22a_8c21-073.jpg
Notes: 45103364 Interesting charcoal-black Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) on the charcoal-black burned soil after the "Gap Fire" last summer. It's wonderful camouflage. I only noticed them when they moved. These are the first insects I've seen after several days of very cold weather. Maybe they warm up faster with the dark colors? (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c22b_8c21-067.jpg
Notes: 45102980 New life in the woods after the "Gap Fire" along West Camino Cielo road in the mountains. I guess this is a stump-sprouting Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) family. It should be a glorious spring with new fire-follower flowers. Nothing has sprouted yet, but I'm sure it will. It's a appropriate winter solstice photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c22c_8c21-041.jpg
Notes: 45102496 A fist-sized Bolete mushroom coming up from Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the woods. It has no red on the stem or in the cracks on the cap, so I guess this is the "Boring Brown Bolete" (Boletus subtomentosus), as David Arora calls it in his excellent book "Mushrooms Demystified" - my mushroom bible. It may be edible, but it's bland at best, so why bother? Note the yellow mycelium at the nibbled-on base. They are pretty in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2008)
CommonNames: Bolete; Coast Live Oak; Boring Brown Bolete

File.Name: 8c23a_8c23-122.jpg
Notes:
45147769 Fantastic - but true - color of this Cortinarius mushroom in the woods today. I guess it's part of the Cortinarius sodagnitus group? It's definately in the in the Bulbopodium subgenus of the genus Cortinarius with a viscid cap and rimmed basal bulb. Look close and you can see a remnant of the "cortina" veil of hairs that once covered and protected the immature gills. Be careful! It's easy to confuse this with the edible Blewitt (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom. They grow together here in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2008)

There was rain yesterday, and there's a new storm growing tonight. Rain for Christmas is the perfect holiday gift!
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Cortinarius sodagnitus; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius; Clitocybe nuda
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Cortinarius sodagnitus; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius; Clitocybe nuda

File.Name: 8c23b_8c23-091.jpg
Notes:
45147382 I don't know what this little mushroom is - maybe a Lepiota? Fortunately I don't have to know all the LBMs - little brown mushrooms. It's good enough to enjoy and appreciate them! This LBM is growing with native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) and a seedling of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula). (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2008)

Can you see another little mushroom in this photo?

File.Name: 8c23c_8c23-092.jpg
Notes: 45147242 Perfect little Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushroom coming up in the canyon today, along with exfoliated bark from Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) and perennial California Bedstraw (Galium californicum) foliage. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2008)
CommonNames: Oak-Loving Collybia; Big-berry Manzanita; California Bedstraw
ScientificName: Collybia dryophila; Arctostaphylos glauca; Galium californicum
associatedTaxa: Collybia dryophila; Arctostaphylos glauca; Galium californicum

File.Name: 8c24a_8c23-104.jpg
Notes:
45185993 Perfect little white-spored Mycena mushrooms growing from the oak twigs and leaves on the forest floor. New life from old is a good message for Christmas Eve. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2008)

There's a new storm settling in for Christmas Eve here in central California. What could be better? I wish you all well!

File.Name: 8c24b_8c23-096.jpg
Notes: 45185430 Really tiny Horsehair Fungi (Marasmius androsaceus or kin) mushrooms sprouting from the fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the soggy woods. The are small but tough. These little mushrooms with wiry stems have a connection with lichens - they have a remarkable ability to dry out and then revive after new rain. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c24c_8c23-113.jpg
Notes: 45185042 The Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) on the edge of the garden are a little the worse for the rain we've been having, but they are still quite beautiful in an archectural sort of way. (Think Antoni GaudÌ!) You can see some of the white spore deposits on the caps below the gills. These mushrooms are probably too old for the table, but they are edible. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c25a_8c25-134.jpg
Notes:
45217003 For Christmas this year, I'm only giving what the Earth bring's forth. This year it's lots of little white-spored Mycena mushrooms growing from the oak twigs and leaves on the forest floor - with raindrops. New life from old is a good message for Christmas Day. That's a fine Christmas present! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2008)

Our big storm crapped out, but we've had 3/4 inch (about 2 cm) of showery rain today. That will do until we get real rain.

File.Name: 8c25b_8c25-148.jpg
Notes: 45216583 This year for Christmas, I'm only giving what the Earth bring's forth. This looks like a hot toasted Christmas bun, but it's really a Boletus subtomentosus mushroom growing under Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) and Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c25c_8c23-076.jpg
Notes:
45216048 This year for Christmas, I'm only giving what the Earth bring's. Sometimes it comes from above, like this huge native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) pinecone with the native grass-like sedge. The fresh cones can weigh up to 10 pounds ( 5 kg). I won't camp under these pines! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2008)

I went looking in the pines for red Amanita muscaria before Christmas as Dimilinchen suggested, but I had no luck. Other mushrooms will have to do.

File.Name: 8c26a_8c26-186.jpg
Notes:
45260403 I guess this is the yellow-form of Amanita muscaria var. formosa growing under a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii in the Heath (Ericaceae) family) tree. "I guess" because I'm really not sure of the I.D. - the volva doesn't look quite right for A. muscaria, but everything else matches. I'll show a photo of the undersides tomorrow. I assume it's poisonous and hallucinogenic - probabaly more the former than the latter. Anyway, here is this beautiful and interesting mushroom in the woods today. It's large - as big as a tea plate! (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2008)

It's been very cold - the ice never melted from the back of my truck today!
ScientificName: Amanita muscaria; formosa; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Amanita muscaria; formosa; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

File.Name: 8c26b_8c26-201.jpg
Notes: 45259500 Beautiful Amanita mushroom coming up in the woods today. I believe this is the Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) coming up under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. These are quite toxic. They contain the same toxins as Amanita muscaria, though in higher concentrations - large doses can be fatal. It's safe to handle and admire though - all mushrooms are! (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2008)
ScientificName: Amanita; Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita; Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita muscaria

File.Name: 8c26c_8c26-196.jpg
Notes: 45258834 These are different little white-spored Mycena mushrooms growing from fallen branches in the woods. You can see some of the white spores fallen on the caps. You also can see part of one of the ordinary Mycena mushrooms in the lower right corner of the photo, and these are quite different. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c27a_8c27-013.jpg
Notes:
45300951 I hiked out to the Kinevan Ranch apple orchard on the pass today to look for more Amanita muscaria growing under Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) - I've found them before! No luck with amanitas, but I got this photo of the native American Chumash bedrock mortar holes filled with rainwater and reflecting the oak above and the sky. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2008)

This is a place where native American Chumash people once ground acorns under a huge Live Oak tree that still stands. These deep bowls are ground into the sandstone bedrock 1-2 feet across. It's remarkable how the holes merge together from so much use.

File.Name: 8c27b_8c27-053.jpg
Notes: 45300542 Close shot of the exquisite "cortina" these mushrooms have. (See my [Previous] photo.) It's a partial veil of hairs that cover and protect the immature gills, and it turns rusty-brown from the falling spores. The cortina is soon torn away from the cap, but you can find the remnants of rusty-brown stained hairs on the stems of many mushrooms. This is probably a <i>Cortinarius fulmineus<i> or kin mushroom - that's my generic name for all these yellow cortinarii with a basal bulb. I don't collect then for the table because I can't tell the many species apart. Feel like experimenting? (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c27c_8c27-049.jpg
Notes: 45299872 Beautiful yellow Cortinarius mushrooms in the woods today. I guess it is Cortinarius fulmineus or kin - it's definately in the Bulbopodium subgenus of the genus with a viscid cap and rimmed basal bulb. They are "probably" edible, but I don't collect them for the table because I can't tell the many species apart. The ones I've nibbled don't taste very good either. I'd rather collect them with photos! (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2008)
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Cortinarius fulmineus or kin - it's definately in the Bulbopodium
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Cortinarius fulmineus or kin - it's definately in the Bulbopodium

File.Name: 8c28a_8c28-074.jpg
Notes:
45334895 Here's looking (and looking) at you kid!

I found this cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on an oak leaf in the woods today. They have the most remarkable eyes - with eyelashes! And blue booties! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2008)

It warmed up a notch today. I went to the sunny side of the pass for photos, but it's still frigid at home where the sun never shines in winter. The ground is still frozen after several days!

File.Name: 8c28b_8c28-056.jpg
Notes: 45334057 Ripe berries of native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii in the Heath (Ericaceae) family). I found these on the ground, probably knocked down by birds. They have the texture of lizard skin! They are edible, but kind of mealy. I didn't enhance the colors one bit for this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c28c_8c28-060.jpg
Notes:
45333622 Beautiful little white-spored Mycena mushrooms growing under a non-native two-needled pine at an old homestead site of the pass. They have pretty violet colors. Maybe it's Mycena pura? The caps do seem kind of large for mycena - about an inch (2.5 cm). They are edible by some accounts, but really too small to matter. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2008)

I'm getting a backlog of great mushroom photos while on vacation. But today's photos are always the best!

File.Name: 8c29a_8c29-121.jpg
Notes:
45381813 Remarkable gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera) on a twig of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae family today. It's amazing to find such a thing on a scrub oak - remember that oaks make acorns. This one seems kind of late, but here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2008)

Plant galls are the tumor-like growths caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) and other organisms which lay eggs that are nutured by the abnormal growth they initiate. Today was a perfect day to go looking for plant galls - there wasn't a bit of wind in the winter mountains.
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae

File.Name: 8c29b_8c29-116.jpg
Notes:
45381046 Today was a perfect day to go looking for plant galls - there wasn't a bit of wind, and there are few flowers in the winter mountains. Plant galls are the tumor-like growths caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) and other organisms which lay eggs that are nutured by the abnormal growth they initiate.

This gall is remarkable because it's growing on Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Fagaceae chryolepis) in the Fagaceae family, which doesn't usually have many galls in our mountains. Heh - it looks like a little avocado, or a big tit, complete with the nipple! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2008)
CommonNames: Gall Wasps; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak

File.Name: 8c29c_8c29-080.jpg
Notes: 45380254 I saw these mushrooms last week and figured the were the Clustered Psathyella (Psathyrella hydrophilia), but looking at them now I see no trace of dark spores. Shucks, I guess they are another Mycena growing on wood? The caps seem to be falling over, like seedlings with "damping off" fungus. Maybe this fungus has a fungus? (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c30a_8c30-162.jpg
Notes:
45424044 The real challenge in photographing all the Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) is to find just a few of them. Here are "a few" monarchs today at the Ellwood Main Monarch Grove, part of the Coronado Butterfly Preserve. (Ellwood Mesa, 30 December 2008)

This Eucalyptus grove in town is now protected. The butterflies come to the same trees every winter. I wanted to get closer, but I stayed behind the rope barrier and used my long lens. ;=)

There's more info here:
   http://www.sblandtrust.org/coronado.html

File.Name: 8c30b_8c30-158.jpg
Notes: 45423521 Huge cluster of Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) waking up at the Ellwood Main Monarch Grove as part of the Coronado Butterfly Preserve. The butterflies congregate here every year in the same trees. It's one of the world's great migrations. (Ellwood Mesa, 30 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c30c_8c30-182.jpg
Notes:
45423161 Massive cluster of Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) hanging like grapes on the vine in the Ellwood Main Monarch Grove as part of the Coronado Butterfly Preserve. No, they're more like a planet waking up as the sun moves over the edge!

There are strange fruit hanging from the Blue Gum (Eucalyptus globulus or kin) trees - here are the masses of Monarch butterflies that gather here (and other butterflly trees along the west coast) from all the western states on this side of the Rocky Mountains. Eastern Monarchs head to Mexico. It's hard to make sense of this many butterflies at once, and a challenge to photograph. (Ellwood Mesa, 30 December 2008)

File.Name: 8c31a_8c31-236.jpg
Notes:
45467345 I found these Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from buried wood in the woods today. The "grass" is a native Sedge (Cyperaceae). I can't imagine why anyone would confuse them with chanterelles, but I suppose anything is possible. DON'T confuse them with chanterelles! These are quite poisonous, causing intense sweating and stomach issues. (Literally!) The stems are tough and have a greenish tinge. But I've been fooled before - just for a moment! (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2008)

I ignore most holidays - New Year's Eve included - but the start of the new year means something. I figure tomorrow I'll count my blessings rather than make resolutions I won't keep.

File.Name: 8c31b_8c31-219.jpg
Notes:
45467158 I guess this pretty mushroom in the woods today is the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) in the Amanita (Amanitaceae) family - though I don't rule out A. pantherina. Either way it is poisonous with the same interesting toxins as Amanita muscaria - only more so. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2008)

I found an "egg" of this mushroom today, but I'll wait a few days to post it so I can feature it by itself.
ScientificName: Amanita gemmata; Amanitaceae; A. pantherina; Amanita muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita gemmata; Amanitaceae; A. pantherina; Amanita muscaria

File.Name: 8c31c_8c31-199.jpg
Notes:
45466888 I believe this is the Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus or kin) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the canyon. This one seems to be growing from a stump. Like all boletes, they have sponge-like pores underneath instead of gills. Did you notice the tiny Mycena mushroom growing under it? (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2008)

The mushrooms are said to be edible if you find fresh specimens, but they are quickly parasitized by the Bolete Eater (Hypomyces chrysospermum) fungus that seems to prefer this species among all boletes, and turns them into bright yellow mush!
Locality: Hypomyces chrysospermum) fungus that seems to prefer this species among all boletes
CommonNames: Oak-Loving Bolete; Coast Live Oak; Bolete Eater
ScientificName: Boletus dryophilus; Quercus agrifolia; Mycena; Hypomyces chrysospermum
associatedTaxa: Boletus dryophilus; Quercus agrifolia; Mycena; Hypomyces chrysospermum

File.Name: 7101a_7101-056.jpg
Notes: Female Nuttall's Woodpecker (Picoides nuttallii) bird on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods today. I hear these shy birds more than I see them, let alone get a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2007)

File.Name: 7101b_7101-055.jpg
Notes: Female Nuttall's Woodpecker (Picoides nuttallii) bird in the woods today. I hear these shy birds more than I see them, let alone get a photo. This is like a "boudoir" shot! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2007)

File.Name: 7101c_7101-044.jpg
Notes: Beautiful male Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) bird in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree close to our upstairs porch. A flock of these beautiful birds was busy in the trees this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2007)

File.Name: 7101d_7101-045.jpg
Notes: This female Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) bird in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree close to our upstairs porch has caught some critter for breakfast. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2007)

File.Name: 7101e_6930-001.jpg
Notes: Little Ruby-crowned Kinglet (Regulus calendula) bird looking for prey in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees near our upstairs porch. Morning coffee and a photo is aleays a great way to start the day! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2007)

File.Name: 7101f_7101-060.jpg
Notes:
Yipes! I think this is a Western Bloodsucking Conenose Bug (Triatoma protracta) in the Reduviidae family of the insect order Hemiptera. It's resting on the cold irom handle of our fire water valve. I poked it and it moved. Dark shade - I used flash. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2007)

If you can identify this for sure, please let me know!

I hope it stays outside. These bugs mostly live in wood rat (Neotoma) nests, but they sometimes invade homes and act like a "Big Bed Bug" (one of it's common names). Dusky-Footed Wood Rats (Neotoma fuscipes) also invade our home in the woods, so I'm not surprised to find this critter. Apparently the bug can carry a Trypanosome parasite that is non-virulent (?) in California, but the bites are still nasty. There was an outbreak of bites in the nearby Painted Cave community a few years ago in our mountains. Some species in South America can pass Chagas' Disease. I hope this is the only one we see!
ScientificName: Triatoma protracta; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Neotoma; Neotoma fuscipes; Trypanosome; Chagas' Disease
associatedTaxa: Triatoma protracta; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Neotoma; Neotoma fuscipes; Trypanosome; Chagas' Disease

File.Name: 7102a_6c31-028.jpg
Notes:
Tiny LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - growing in the fallen oak leaves in the woods. It's probably a little white-spored Mycena 'shroom. I added the pencil to give a sense of scale. Hah! (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2006)

*Sigh* I never got outside today. School starts tomorrow after a wonderful holiday vacation.

File.Name: 7103a_6c31-030.jpg
Notes:
The strangest things are just outside the front door!

This is a rooting stalk of native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rose (Rosaceae) family trying to invade our garden. This is how they make brambles. The branches grow up and out and root where they touch down, ready to leapfrog out and do it again and again. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2006)

We fight the blackerries in the garden. If you want to propagate them, the secret is to make cuttings and plant them upside down!

File.Name: 7104a_6c30-005.jpg
Notes: Leaves of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family with the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2006)

File.Name: 7105a_6c30-010.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful grape-sized wasp gall on a branch of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the woods. I believe this is the gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella, family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera). The adult is a tiny gnat-sized wasp - at least it's in the wasp family. The wasps re-program the oak to induce these tumor-like growths that become a distinctive home for their young. That's advanced genetic engineering! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2006)

Most of these oak galls have lost their color by now, but this one is still bright. I don't understand why galls are so colorful. Wouldn't that just attract preditors? This gall is a "twig gall" growing below the base of the leaf. There's a bud growing right above the leaf. I think it's so interesting that the bud and the gall have the same shape. I wonder if the wasp is tapping into the same genetics?
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Beaked Twig Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 7105b_6c30-009.jpg
Notes: Most acorns are long gone, but I still find a few on the native Scrub Oaks (Quercus berberidifolia) in the woods. The dent at the top is a sign that some critter has comsumed this acorn from the inside, and is probably using it as a snug winter home. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2006)

File.Name: 7106a_7106-027.jpg
Notes:
First new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family in our canyon. I took this photo with the sun behind. The urn-like flowers look like beautiful blushing lanterns. The white lines in the upper-right are awns of a dandelion-like composite seed stuck in the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2007)

We're missing the rain this year. We had another sprinkle this week, but the woods are still crunchy dry. I hope we get real rain soon.

File.Name: 7106b_7106-016.jpg
Notes: I tripped over a branch in the woods and fell down today - protect the camera! Face down in the fallen oak leaves, I noticed these tiny LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms". I would have walked right past these little mushrooms if I didn't fall. I believe these mushrooms are growing from buried wood. They are not the usual saprophytes I've been finding. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2007)

File.Name: 7106c_7106-019.jpg
Notes: I tripped over a branch in the woods and fell down today - protect the camera! Face down in the fallen oak leaves, I noticed these tiny LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms". I would have walked right past these little mushrooms if I didn't fall! (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2007)

File.Name: 7106d_7106-008.jpg
Notes: Tattered little housefly-type fly (order Diptera) perched on the edge of an LBM -"Little Brown Mushrooms" - in the woods. These flies make it through winter as adults rather than passing winter as eggs or larvae or pupae like most insects. I find them when the winter sun shines. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2007)

File.Name: 7106e_7106-010.jpg
Notes: Old leaf of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Honeysuckle (Caprifoliaceae) family getting ready to fall. This shrub is evergreen, but old leaves must make way for new leaves. The plant is absorbing nutrients as the fungi move in. It looks like a map! (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2007)

File.Name: 7106f_7106-037.jpg
Notes: Leaflets of native Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta) in the woods with the sun behind, showing the secret spore-bearing sporangia under their skirts. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2007)

File.Name: 7107a_7107-047.jpg
Notes: I had my camera set on my tripod with my macro lens focused as close as possible trying to get photos of those tiny black and white insects, when this HUGE Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) landed in my picture. I shifted focus as fast as I could to get a photo. This is almost full-frame! The bee gives a good scale for the tiny Thrips (Thysanoptera) shown closer in my [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2007)

File.Name: 7107b_7107-051.jpg
Notes:
Tiny insects on new urn-shaped flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family. At first I thought they were flies because of the mottled wings. Then I thought they might be wasps because they were social and kept touching antennae like ants. But I also noticed they are aggressive. I watched one critter sit near the mouth of the flower and chase the others away!

I believe these tiny critters are Thrips in the family Phlaeothripidae in the insect order Thysanoptera. They don't have wings fringed with long hairs like typical thrips, but here they are. It's fascinating that these tiny thrips have real social behavior! (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2007)

See my [Previous] photo for a closer crop, and my [Next] photo for scale.

File.Name: 7107c_7107-051.jpg
Notes:
This is a very close crop of the mystery insects I found on the flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family in the woods. This critter is pinhead size. At first I thought fly because of the mottled wings. Then I thought wasp because they were social and kept touching antennae like ants. Then I got home and looked up close (like this) on my computer screen - YIPES, they're aliens! There's the head, then two legs on the second body part, and more legs behind? (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2007)

I figured it out. See my [Next] two photos!

File.Name: 7107d_7107-053.jpg
Notes:
Not quite a flower, but here are the colored bracts that will hold the new flowers of this Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2007)

This is our winter-rainy season, but we've had very little rain since summer. Most wildflowers haven't even started yet, but the shrubs and a few perennials like this one are trying to flower. The plants are confused by the weather, and so am I.

File.Name: 7107e_7107-064.jpg
Notes: Branch of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family showing new flower buds and those strange persistent "corky stipules" at the base of the leaves. This makes me think of a slice through a Grecian urn, with goodies. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2007)

File.Name: 7107f_7107-066.jpg
Notes: New flower buds of native Islay aka Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rose (Rosaceae) family. This is our winter-rainy season, but we've had very little rain since summer. Most wildflowers haven't even sprouted yet, but the shrubs are trying to flower. The plants are confused by the weather, and so am I. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2007)

File.Name: 7108a_7107-060.jpg
Notes: I was searching for mushrooms, not a heart of gold - but here it is. This is a new flower of a native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) shrub in the Poppy (Papaveraceae) family. The woods are dry. We're still waiting for real rain to break our summer-dry season. New winter-flowering shrubs are forming buds, but these late-season summer poppies never stopped flowering! (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2007)

File.Name: 7109a_6c26-065.jpg
Notes: There are still a few late-season flowers of the native composite shrub Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa var. Grindelioides) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. This is the more congested variety that grows in the rocks rather than the woods. We've had little rain this year, and the late summer flowers just keep going. At this point, the flowers have outlived the pollinators. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2007)

File.Name: 7109b_7107-058.jpg
Notes: Looking into the "heart of gold" of a new flower of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Poppy (Papaveraceae) family. See my [Previous] photo. These are male stamens crowding the female stigmas in the center, like a tribal rave. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2007)

File.Name: 7110a_7106-003.jpg
Notes: This Christmas flower was a gift from one of my students. The tag on the plant says "Amaryllis" - but I'm sure it's genus Hippeastrum in the Amaryllidaceae family. It's happy in our bathroom and catches perfect light through the skylight every morning like this. It grows native from Argentina north to Mexico. Have you seen it in the wild? (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2007)

File.Name: 7111a_6c30-025.jpg
Notes:
This uncommon native shrub is Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata in the Berberidaceae family). It only grows in dark canyons where the sun never shines. (The exposure is 3/4 sec @ f/16 @ ISO 200.) This is a kind of "Oregon Grape" that really belongs further north - in Oregon. I guess these are new flower buds for next spring. Interesting how the tips are black. I hope they are all right. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2007)

I have a photo of the yellow flowers at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=12752896

File.Name: 7112a_7112-121.jpg
Notes: Looking up into the canopy of the "Carpe Diem Tree" at school. This is the tree of life for many birds and other critters. It also looks like a river of life in this photo - an estuary flow pattern. This mighty tree is a living network of branches supporting leaves. It's a good testament that even on a cold day in winter, life goes on if the network is there. See my [Previous] photos. (Santa Ynez Valley, 12 January 2007)

File.Name: 7112b_7112-126.jpg
Notes: Looking up into the canopy of the "Carpe Diem" Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) at school. This tree - like most - is built like an umbrella, with a shell of leaves supported by a network of branches. The tree is hollow - inside leaves would get no light because it's blocked by the outside leaves. This tree is deciduous, but it still has old leaves - confused by our very dry winter. I'm confused too. (Santa Ynez Valley, 12 January 2007)

File.Name: 7112c_7112-134.jpg
Notes: We call this the "Carpe Diem" tree at school - "Seize the day!" This grand Valley Oak (Quercus lobata in the Fagaceae family) tree has seized many days. I guess it's 150-200 years old. I can almost see the (now extinct) California Grizzly Bears ((Ursus californicus)) sharpening their claws on the trunk; and Native American Chumash hunters, and Californio vaqueros, and pioneer farmers, and hopeful new settlers and commuters all resting in the shade.... and I see middle school girls dancing! This mighty tree of life is a good testament that even on a cold day in winter, life goes on. (Santa Ynez Valley, 12 January 2007)

File.Name: 7113a_7113-028.jpg
Notes:
It's very cold today. I noticed these icicles this afternoon in our outdoor water garden (an old bathtub) in a place where the sun never shines. This exposure is 2.5 seconds @ f/22 @ ISO 200. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2007)

We had a hard frost last night that damaged many garden plants we've had for years, and tonight might be colder. I think the frost is worse because it's so dry. Many of our wonderful Salvia sages are gone. I hope they come back. This will be a hard winter for the hummingbirds.

File.Name: 7113b_7113-023.jpg
Notes:
Looking close at the exquisite "cortina" of cobwebby hairs that cover and protect the gills of this Cortinarius mushroom and catch the rusty-red spores. Maybe this is Cortinarius fulmineus or kin in the "Bulbopodium" group. Store-bought Agaricus mushrooms have a membranous skin to protect the gills, but these mushrooms have a different design. My photo shows the "partial veil" - the petticoats that protect the gills. It also has a "universal veil" that covers the cap and bulbous base. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2007)

This mushroom was still underground, but I noticed the "mushrump" - the raised leaves above - and dug it up. This is our rainy season, but it's still very dry. We need real soon to keep the woods alive.

File.Name: 7113c_7113-003.jpg
Notes: Convergent Lady Beetle - aka "ladybug" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) on an oak branch by the creek. Most of the Lady Beetles are gone, though there are still a few on the plants and many more hiding under the fallen leaves on the ground. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2007)

File.Name: 7113d_7113-007.jpg
Notes: A flock of birds and a school of fish... the proper collective noun for these flowers must be a chorus of Manzanita! These are new urn-shaped flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2007)

File.Name: 7114a_7114-058.jpg
Notes:
This is as close as I can get - a tight crop in a photo taken at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. It's pinhead size.

This is a tiny gnat - a little fly - (order Diptera) perched on the margin of a leaf of native Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) in the Sumac Anacardiaceae) family with flower buds behind. I reckon this is one of those tiny flies that dance in the winter sunbeams. Maybe it's resting because it's so darn cold. I don't usually even try to photograph any critter this small, but I noticed it when I was looking at the flowers in the background. I pre-focused to 1:1 and moved in. Nice whiskers! (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2007)

File.Name: 7114b_7113-004.jpg
Notes: It's been very cold - into the 'teens at night and barely above freezing during the day. This little housefly-type fly (order Diptera) is huddled in an inverted oak leaf trying to get warm - like a natural solar collector! (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2007)

File.Name: 7114c_7114-036.jpg
Notes: New male staminate catkins of Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Silk-Tassel (Garryaceae) family. The strange flowers hang like pearls on a thread. They must be wind-pollinated. My lens was dusted with pollen after I got this photo. This native shrub is "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. The males mature first, but I also found a receptive female plant today. More photos tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2007)

File.Name: 7114d_7114-060.jpg
Notes:
Seed head of our uncommon native summer-flowering Green Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata

- or maybe P. michaelii?) in the Orchidaceae family. Orchid seeds are like fairy dust. They must combine with a fungus to get started. They are more like spores than normal plant seeds that at least contain a meal to get their seedling started. Note the twisted ovary that turns the summer flowers upside down. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2007)
ScientificName:
Piperia elongata

- or maybe P. michaelii; Orchidaceae
associatedTaxa:
Piperia elongata

- or maybe P. michaelii; Orchidaceae

File.Name: 7114e_7114-032.jpg
Notes: Like a bird's eye view of a medieval friar's head, complete with curls! This is really the healed scar of a lost branch on a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family. It feels so good to rub - it must be lucky! (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2007)

File.Name: 7114f_7114-033.jpg
Notes:
New flowers of California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) in the mountains. The tree is related to avocado and cinnamon and sassafras - all in the Laurel (Lauraceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2007)

It's disturbing to see that many of the new Bay flowers are drying up before they open. This feels like a drought year despite El Nino. It's still very dry in what should be our rainy season.

File.Name: 7115a_7115-079.jpg
Notes:
Monarch Butterfly
Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera

I visited the Ellwood Butterfly Preserve today. There are masses of Monarch butterflies in the Eucalyptus trees - but it's a challenge to get a photo of just one. This Eucalyptus grove is now protected, and I stayed behind the rope barrier. This butterfly was on the other side of the rope, so I used my big 500 mm telephoto lens to get close. It's been very cold, so this butterfly is fluttering its wings to warm up for take off. The exposure is just slow enough to show the motion - 1/30 sec @ f/9 @ ISO 400. (Ellwood Mesa, 15 January 2007)

All Monarch butterflies west of the Rocky Mountains fly to California for winter and this tree is a favorite stop. I first saw the butterflies almost 40 years ago in the same trees. This butterfly grove is now more than half surrounded by houses just a few hundred meters away. These winter butterflies are so vulnerable to habitat loss. This grove is now protected as a preserve.

The irony is that the butterflies roost in the introduced Blue Gum (Eucalyptus globulus or kin) trees - not native, but doing well here after 100+ years. It's an eco-quandry - remove the alien trees or disturb the native butterflies? I think the message is that we can't put the egg back together again - we have to make do with what we have, and do our best to not lose more.
Locality: Eucalyptus globulus or kin) trees - not native

File.Name: 7115b_7115-086.jpg
Notes:
Monarch Butterfly
Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera

I visited the Ellwood Butterfly Preserve today. There are masses of Monarch butterflies in the Eucalyptus trees - see my [Previous] photos. This Eucalyptus grove is now protected, and I stayed behind the rope barrier, but this butterfly crossed the rope from the other side, and I managed to get close with my macro lens. It was fluttering its wings and moving forward trying to warm up for take off. A moment later it was gone. Look close at the speckled head to see eyes and proboscis. (Ellwood Mesa, 15 January 2007)

File.Name: 7115c_7115-082.jpg
Notes:
Monarch Butterflies
Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera

There are masses of Monarch butterflies in the Eucalyptus trees at the Ellwood Butterfly Preserve. The challenge is to photograph just a few. See my [[Previous] photos. This Eucalyptus grove is now protected, and I stayed behind the rope barrier, though I wanted to get closer for photos! (Ellwood Mesa, 15 January 2007)

File.Name: 7115d_7115-063.jpg
Notes:
Monarch Butterflies
Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera

Masses of Monarch butterflies in the Eucalyptus trees at the Ellwood Butterfly Preserve where western monarch butterflies converge for winter. Despite the cold weather, many butterflies were active today. (Ellwood Mesa, 15 January 2007)

File.Name: 7115e_7115-070.jpg
Notes:
Monarch Butterflies
Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera

Masses of Monarch butterflies - strange fruit in the Eucalyptus trees - at the Ellwood Butterfly Grove today. You notice the butterflies with open wings in the sun, but look in the upper-left of this photo. With wings closed, the butterflies look like dull winter leaves. (Ellwood Mesa, 15 January 2007)

File.Name: 7115f_7115-078.jpg
Notes:
Monarch Butterflies
Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera

I had errands in town today, so I stopped by the Ellwood Butterfly Grove where western Monarch butterflies converge for winter. All Monarch butterflies west of the Rocky Mountains fly to California for winter and this tree is a favorite stop. Eastern Monarchs head for Cuba or Mexico. I first saw the butterflies almost 40 years ago in the same trees. This Eucalyptus grove is now protected, and I stayed behind the rope barrier, though I wanted to get closer for photos. I used my big 500 mm telephoto lens for all but one of today's photos. This many butterflies at one time - the whole idea of butterflies migrating across a continent - is hard to comprehend. (Ellwood Mesa, 15 January 2007)
ScientificName: Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera; Ellwood Butterfly Grove
associatedTaxa: Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera; Ellwood Butterfly Grove

File.Name: 7116a_7114-057.jpg
Notes:
Tree leaves with the sun behind and shadows.

This beautiful tree is native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family. It's a big brother of the Manzanita (genus Arctostaphylos) shrubs I've been showing, with similar bark and flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2007)

File.Name: 7117a_7111-114.jpg
Notes:
Just another tumble-down cabin in the woods - but for me this will always be "The Cabin." We lived here for 10 years with no electricity or phone back in the 70s and into the 80s. We had artesian spring water, propane refigerator and hot water, Aladdin kerosene lamps, and a dangerous - but wonderful - wood-fire sauna. We had a large garden, along with goats, pigs, rabbits, ducks, chickens, and geese. I started learning local natural history here, we played music into the night, I built my first computer using wire-wrap and a propane torch to solder, and I started teaching. Our two kids started life here. So many memories in this tumble-down cabin that make it a glow for me now... Maybe every tumble-down cabin in the woods has this glow in memory for someone.

We moved a few miles away to our real house in 1983. The cabin has been abandoned for many years except for the deer mice and wood rats. Now it's a "Hanta Hotel" - with a threat of Hanta Virus from rodent droppings just breathing the air inside. Life goes on. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2007)

File.Name: 7118a_7118-115.jpg
Notes: I took my after-school Hike Club kids to remote Surf Beach at the mouth of the Santa Ynez River for a very low -1.5 foot tide today. There are interesting plants up in the dunes, but it was more fun to chase seagulls on the mirror-like low tide beach! (Surf Beach, 18 January 2007)

File.Name: 7119a_7114-048.jpg
Notes:
New male staminate catkins of Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Silk-Tassel (Garryaceae) family. This native shrub is "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. The flowers of this particular shrub will only produce pollen. It depends on the wind to carry pollen to the female shrub 50 feet away that has pistils and ovaries ready to make fertile seeds. See my [Previous] photo.

The male flowers are confusing. They hang like a string of pearls and have a pretty cream color. There are no obvious petals, but fused bracts make an "involucre" cup with several stalked clusters of stamens (and another involucre) growing from each one. The hairy flower parts around the stamens of each flower are joined at the tip like a little cage - maybe this helps shake the pollen loose in the wind? My camera lens was dusted with pollen after I got this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2007)

File.Name: 7119b_7114-050.jpg
Notes: New female pistillate catkins of Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Silk-Tassel (Garryaceae) family. This native shrub is "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. Look close and you can see the demure receptive pistils exposed from under the petticoat-like bracts of the catkin flowers waiting for pollen carried by the wind from nearby male plants. Compare my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2007)

File.Name: 7120a_7120-025.jpg
Notes: Leaves of native Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus) in the woods. Notice how each new grass leaf comes out at a different angle from the leaf above and below. Try a web search on the word "phyllotaxis" to understand this interesting geometry. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2007)

File.Name: 7120b_7120-024.jpg
Notes: Leaves of native California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) in the Laurel (Lauraceae) plant family in the woods, with the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2007)

File.Name: 7120c_7120-001.jpg
Notes: White leaves of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family growing on the dry side of our garden. It's been cold. Today is the first day in a week that the ground has thawed above freezing. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2007)

File.Name: 7120d_7120-008.jpg
Notes:
Pretty new violet Cortinarius mushroom in the Bulbopodiumsubgenus. This mushroom has a sticky viscid cap, dry stem, and a rimmed bulb at the base. Look how the hairs on the stem - and the gills themselves - are stained cinnamon-brown from falling spores. I've confused these with edible Blewitt (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms, but the rusty-red spores mark the difference. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2007)

There are many different kinds of cortinarius mushrooms in the wood. Some are edible and some are poisonous. I can't tell them apart - and I'm not starving - so I just enjoy finding them and getting photos. I never collect them for the table.

File.Name: 7120e_7120-012.jpg
Notes: Pretty yellow Cortinarius mushroom pushing up the leaves into a "mushrump" in the woods. I guess this is subgenus Phlegmacium with viscid cap but no enlarged bulb at the base. You can see the rusty-red spores on the hairs on the stem. There are new mushrooms in the woods. The only way to find them in this dry year is to look for the "mushrumps" as they push up the leaves from below. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2007)

File.Name: 7120f_7120-026.jpg
Notes:
Native Land Snail (Gastropoda) shell in the woods. This snail has a flat shell that turns clockwise. It is NOT one of the nasty introduced Garden Snails with a vertical shell that are so common in town and at school. I hope it's too harsh in the mountains for them to get established here. I like how they leave a spiral record of their lives. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2007)

I rarely see the live native snails in the woods. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=9497119

File.Name: 7121a_7120-053.jpg
Notes:
I was trying to get close photos of the native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family. I heard a buzz and swung my camera to get this photo of a male Hummingbird working the Manzanita flowers three feet away! No time to fiddle with exposure. This will probably be my only hummingbird photo ever at f/22! (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2007)

I first thought Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) - they are with us all winter. Then I thought Black-chinned Hummingbird (Archilochus alexandria) because of the violet florets. Then I thought maybe a Costa's Hummingbird (Calypte costae) with long florets? I don't really care. I'm just glad the hummers survived the cold weather last week!
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Heath; Anna's Hummingbird; Black-chinned Hummingbird; Costa's Hummingbird
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Calypte anna; Archilochus alexandria; Calypte costae
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Calypte anna; Archilochus alexandria; Calypte costae

File.Name: 7121b_7120-042.jpg
Notes: Brave new seedling of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the woods near home. The ground has been frozen all week, but there's very little visible frost because there's no moisture in the air in this drought year. It was a pleasant surprise to find water drops on the lupine seedlings this morning! (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2007)

File.Name: 7121c_7120-051.jpg
Notes:
Strange insect stem gall on a native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) by our house in the woods. I believe this gall is made by the Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth (Periploca ceanothiella in the order Lepidoptera).

Here's some info from:
http://oregonstate.edu/Dept/nurspest/Ceanothus_stem_gall_moth.htm:

The moth lays eggs on the buds and flowers. The young larvae bore into the stem and feed within causing the ue to gall. The larvae overwinter inside these galls which are very noticeable in the early spring. They then pupate and emerge as adult moths in summer. I can't recall seeing these galls before at home, and I didn't see any more on my hike today as I passed many Ceanothus shrubs in the canyon. I hope this isn't a new pest. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2007)

This is the same Ceanothus shrub that had colonies of ants and aphids last year. I haven't seen any ant colonies this year. See my photo at: http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=12345615
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Periploca ceanothiella in the order Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Periploca ceanothiella in the order Lepidoptera

File.Name: 7121d_7121-043.jpg
Notes:
I spent the morning stacking firewood after we finally broke down and bought a cord. It's been that kind of year - very cold and dry. We still need rain, but at least the firewood supply is good. Is this what "global warming" looks like? (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2007)

Here's my "Hard Freeze" stumper for the week:

It's been very cold this week! DMS students have many stories of frozen pipes and ponds and garden plants. California citrus and avocado crops have been damaged by the hard freeze. Farmers try to protect their tender crops by turning on sprinklers and using wind machines to keep the frigid air moving. (Are "smudge pots" still used?) The stumper is that this combination of cold, wet, and wind is the worst condition for humans. It's a recipe for hypothermia. How can the combination of water and wind protect plants but harm people?
Locality: Are "smudge pots" still used?)  The stumper is that this combination of cold

File.Name: 7122a_7121-075.jpg
Notes: Here's a mystery stumper photo I got yesterday afternoon just before the sun went down behind the trees. Like usual, I cropped and bumped up brightness and contrast and color saturation, but otherwise there are no "photoshop" tricks. The exposure is 1/15 sec @ f/14 @ ISO 200. This is something you've see before! (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2007)

File.Name: 7123a_7121-073.jpg
Notes:
Of course my [Previous] mystery photo was a close shot of the web of a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae). Here's a sharp photo of the same web on our porch without the extra vibrations (and colors) from the wind. The CD-sized web has CD-like colors. It's easy to say "diffraction and refraction" - but I really don't understand the not-quite-rainbow colors. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2007)

There's been a change-of-shift of spiders in the woods. In the Fall, I was finding big spders going after big prey. Now I find these tiny spiders going after tiny prey - the fungus flies and gnats that dance in the sunbeams. We're having a very dry (and cold) winter season. We've only had 1/10th of the rain we should have by now. The woods are confused, but life goes on.
Locality: and cold) winter season.  We've only had 1/10th of the rain we should have by now.  The woods are confused

File.Name: 7124a_7121-047.jpg
Notes: Every year is different, but this is a strange one in California. It's been very cold and dry in what should be our rainy season. We lost many garden flowers to the hard frost, though I still hope they will come back with Spring. This opening seed head of non-native Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) in the Asclepiadaceae family is a different stumper. It should be done by now. The leaves were frosted by the cold weather, but I noticed this late seedpod opening to send new dandelion-like milkweed seeds into the world. Life goes on. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2007)

File.Name: 7125a_7125-181.jpg
Notes:
You have to look close to understand this photo. It's a plain brown Moth (order Lepidoptera) resting on the sliding glass door to the porch - moth on the right and reflection on the left. The afternoon sun has already set below the trees in the back-right, though there is still some glow from the far south-facing side of the canyon. I wedged my camera and tripod as close to the glass as I could. The exposure is 3 seconds @ f/16 @ ISO 200. I brightened it up. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2007)

This new moth reminds me that there have been very few moths in this dry cold winter. It's hard to remember all the critters I don't see.

File.Name: 7126a_7114-056.jpg
Notes: These flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family are done flowering. Now it's gestation time. The hanging lantern-like flowers drop away, revealing the long female pistil and red ovary within. The ovary will swell into a berry with a single large nut (in this species) within. I believe the green tissue at the base of the ovary is the remant of the "nectary disk" that produces sweet nectar to attract insects and hummingbirds to the flowers. Manzanita is an important nectar source in winter when flowers are scarce in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2007)

File.Name: 7127a_7127-002.jpg
Notes:
Today was a rain day - just what we need to break our wet season drought. We've only had an inch or so of rain from the storm so far by my coffee can rain gauge, but it never stopped all day, so it's a good soaker. I went out for a rain photo with camera and umbrella. This is an unfolding seedling leaf of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) with raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2007)

Today was a good day for computer backups and new music. I'll be happy if tomorrow is a good day for photos. I'll be even happier if it keeps raining!

File.Name: 7128a_7128-043.jpg
Notes:
Banana Slug weather! We had 3 1/4 inches (8+ cm) of rain so far this weekend. No big deal in a wet year, but just what we need in this very dry winter season. We had real rain yesterday and constant drizzle today. I went for a short hike with my camera and plastic bags for protection.

I found several of these hand-long Pacific Banana Slugs (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) in the woods. This is our largest terrestrial mollusk (Gastropoda). You can see its dark eyespots on the tips of the upper stalks. The hole (only on their right side) is the "pneumostone" opening for breathing. Where do they hide when it's not raining?

It was dark and drizzly today. This exposure is 1/8 second @ f/20 @ ISO 800. The so-slow slugs were still moving too fast to get sharp. I took many photos to get a few keepers like this. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2007)

File.Name: 7128b_7128-014.jpg
Notes: Little mushroom in the woods - I guess Lepiota with free white gills and cap with a dark center that breaks into scales. I'm sure this mushroom was here before the rain this weekend, but I first saw it today. It's dark and drizzly. The exposure is 1.3 seconds @ f/32 @ ISO 400. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2007)

File.Name: 7128c_7128-010.jpg
Notes:
Another lupine leaf with raindrops - how can I resist?

This is seedling leaf of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) with raindrops. It was gloomy and drizzly all day. It's not as bright as it looks. This exposure is 1/3 second @ f/32 @ ISO 200. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2007)

File.Name: 7129a_7128-013.jpg
Notes:
New mushrooms in the woods on a rainy weekend. I believe these are Entoloma rhodopolium or kin in the Rhodophyllaceae family. They have a chlorine smell like bleach or a swimming pool, and they are definately NOT EDIBLE. You wouldn't want to try one. Other species have dark caps. They all have unusual "pink" flesh-colored spores that stain the gills. You can see some color in the top mushroom. Pretty though. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2007)

I have to brag. I got this photo yesterday on a dark drizzly day in the woods using an umbrella to protect my camera. The exposure is 5 seconds @ f/32 @ ISO 400. The reward is great Depth of Field. Of course I used a tripod and the camera self-timer. And I held my breath even after I moved away from the camera! I can't lock-up the mirror on my Nikon D-70. I want that feature in my next camera!

File.Name: 7129b_7128-030.jpg
Notes: Hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) in the woods on a rainy day. This is our largest terrestrial mollusk (Gastropoda). Wikipedia says it's the second-largest species of slug in the world. Metalmarianne is right - they are also quite fast for their size, and it has hard to get sharp photos in the dim light on this drizzly day because it kept moving. And they are wonderfully delicate. This Banana Slug is moving through the new grass leaves without disturbing the raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2007)

File.Name: 7130a_7130-055.jpg
Notes: Winter-bare Poplar trees (genus Populus) in the Willow (Salicaceae) family by a faculty home at school today. There's a storm a' coming! This is that last quiet moment of sunshine glow before the storm dumps, with dark clouds building in the background. A few minutes later, we had heavy rain and hail. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 January 2007)

File.Name: 7131a_7128-026.jpg
Notes: Shy Blewit (Clitocybe nuda aka Lepista nuda) mushroom pushing up through the oak leaves in the woods. The caps have a deep blue-violet color when fresh, but they quickly fade in the rain. Heh - I took this photo in the drizzle last weekend with an umbrella to protect my camera. The exposure is 1 second @ f/32 @ ISO 800. These mushrooms are edible and tasty, but don't confuse them with Cortinarius mushrooms with similar colors. Compare my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2007)

File.Name: 7131b_7128-024.jpg
Notes: Pretty Blewit (Clitocybe nuda aka Lepista nuda) mushroom along our road in the woods. I picked this mushroom for the photo. Note all the leaf-humus attached to the base by the abundent mycelium. The caps have that same blue-violet color when fresh, but they quickly fade in the rain. These mushrooms are edible and tasty, but we also have Cortinarius species mushrooms with similar colors. My book says that the name "blewit" is derived from "blue hat". The mushrooms are really more violet than blue. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2007)

File.Name: 7201a_7201-075.jpg
Notes: The dark side of nature... This is a strange Louse Fly (family Hippoboscidae in the order Diptera). It is a real fly, but it has special adaptations to live as an external parasite among the feathers of birds, like a tick. I found this fly on a perfect - but recently dead - roadkill Great Horned Owl (Bubo virginianus) that a parent brought to school this morning. The fly is flattened with a sack-like abdomen and claws adapted to cling to the feathers of the host. I've seen similar parasitic flies on deer that lose their wings and really look like ticks. Unpleasant, but fascinating. (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 February 2007)

File.Name: 7202a_7201-088.jpg
Notes:
Reflections of my students in the lifeless eye of a fresh roadkill Great Horned Owl (Bubo virginianus) that a parent brought to school yesterday. I'm in the reflection too, with my camera on tripod with self-timer. I had second-thoughts about showing this image of the dead, but these are the living kids who will protect our wildlife in years to come, and they must understand that it's worth protecting. You too. (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 February 2007)

I know - don't think about Sauron and Lord of the Rings.

----

This is real.

Metalmarianne asked about Groundhog Day. We don't have groundhogs here in California, but the gopher did see his shadow on this beautiful sunny day, which portends 6 more weeks of winter. Good, we need rain! I don't have a photo, but I do have a stumper:

Today was Groundhog Day. Traditions don't have to make good sense, but they often do. What's so special about February 2? We don't have groundhogs in California, but if any gopher stuck his head out of the ground this week, I'm sure he saw his shadow because it's been sunny and clear. By tradition, this means we'll have six more weeks of winter. But isn't this backwards? Is there any reasonable way to explain why sunshine means more winter, while clouds mean that spring is here? Shouldn't it be just the opposite?

See my answer at: http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/05feb99a.html

File.Name: 7202b_7201-068.jpg
Notes: This is where macro meets micro. These are Biting Lice (order Mallophaga) on the feathers of a a fresh roadkill Great Horned Owl (Bubo virginianus). Like beetles vs. bugs, these lice have chewing mouthparts as opposed to the Suckiing Lice in the order Anplura. This is an almost 500x500 pixel crop into a 3008x2000 pixel original photo taken at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. The critters are only 1 mm long or less, but they still have remarkable structure! (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 February 2007)
CommonNames: Biting Lice; Great Horned Owl; Suckiing Lice

File.Name: 7202c_7201-065.jpg
Notes: Strange Louse Fly (family Hippoboscidae in the order Diptera) on a fresh roadkill Great Horned Owl (Bubo virginianus) that a parent brought to school yesterday. It's a real fly, but it has tick-like adaptations for living as an external parasite on birds. I don't know if this species is specific to owls or maybe they "fly around". If you know fly anatomy, you will notice that the radial veins are crowded along the costal margin, with very thin veins extending across the wing. These flies don't fly very well, and cling when they land. See my [Previous] photo for more info. (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 February 2007)

File.Name: 7203a_7203-024.jpg
Notes: A rave of Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) in the woods today. I haven't seen many Lady Beetles for a few weeks. I'm not sure if they left and came back, or maybe they've been here all the time hiding in the leaves during the cold weather. Here they are in the canyon - lots of them - reproduction is happening! (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2007)

File.Name: 7203b_7203-027.jpg
Notes: Lots of Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) converging in the woods. It's a ladybug rave! I haven't seen many Lady Beetles for a few weeks. I'm not sure if they left and came back, or have been here all the time. But here they are in the canyon - lots of them! (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2007)

File.Name: 7203c_7203-010.jpg
Notes: Two realms of life - a new green grass seedling and a leaf-colored Entoloma rhodopolium or kin mushroom in the Rhodophyllaceae family. The one makes and the other takes. Both are essential for the life of the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2007)

File.Name: 7203d_7203-032.jpg
Notes: This Cortinarius mushroom (there are many species) is dull on top, but it has pretty violet colors underneath. The tasty Blewit (Clitocybe nuda aka Lepista nuda) mushrooms (see my [Previous] photo) have similar colors, but the rusty-brown hairs on the stem give this (probably) non-edible Cortinarius away. It's impossible to give simple "up or down" rules for edible mushrooms. You just have to learn them all. What fun! (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2007)

File.Name: 7203e_7203-035.jpg
Notes: New Blewit (Clitocybe nuda aka Lepista nuda) mushroom pushing up through the oak leaves in the woods today. These white-spored mushrooms are edible and tasty, but don't confuse them with Cortinarius mushrooms with similar violet colors. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2007)

File.Name: 7203f_7203-014.jpg
Notes: New leaflets of Coffee Fern (Pellea andromedifolia) growing on a sandstone outcrop in our canyon, with the sun behind. The leaflets are not "fern-like", but there are many different kinds of ferns in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2007)

File.Name: 7204a_7204-069.jpg
Notes: Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) snuggling into an urn-like flower of a native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Heath (Ericaceae) family flowering in the woods. Insects and flowers are both scarce in the winter woods in the very dry year. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2007)

File.Name: 7204b_7204-062.jpg
Notes: Snuggling dark-spored mushrooms in the woods - maybe Psathyrella longipes or kin. The mushrooms are fragile. They broke when I tried to pick them. This is my favorite photo of the weekend. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2007)

File.Name: 7204c_7204-053.jpg
Notes:
Short-Stemmed Russula (Russula brevipes or kin) mushroom pushing up through Madrone (Arbutus menziesii in the Heath (Ericaceae) family) leaves in the woods. This is a large hand-sized mushroom. I gave it the "Russula test" - the stem snaps clean like chalk. It has a spicy taste, maybe edible, but not worth it. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2007)

I was hoping to find many mushrooms this weekend after rain last week, but I only found a few. This is one of our driest years on record. The woods are still resting.

File.Name: 7205a_7203-038.jpg
Notes: Little spider resting in the shade on a leaf of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) by our house in the woods. I overexposed the photo to show the spider, and I love how the leaves look like neon! I guess this is a Cobweb Weaver aka Combfooted Spider (Theridiidae) because it doesn't have an organized web like an Orb-weaver (family Araneidae). But I'm just guessing.
