(San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2007)
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Cobweb Weaver; Combfooted Spider; Orb-weaver

File.Name: 7205b_7205-070.jpg
Notes: Native Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri) on "the castle" sandstone rock catching some alpenglow light as the sun goes down behind me. This is the view from our porch at home just after I got home from school today, using my big lens. The beautiful light only lasted a few minutes. I fiddled with my white balance control. Corrected for "incandescent light" the trees look normal, how boring. This is "daylight setting". Better, but it still doesn't glow like the real thing! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2007)

File.Name: 7206a_7204-066.jpg
Notes: The first sweet-smelling white flowers of native BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the mountains. The individual flowers are tiny, but in a wet year, gazillions of these little flowers cover the south-facing slopes of the mountains like snow. We've had about 1/3 normal rainfall this year, so I'm happy to see any new flowers. There's a chance of rain tomorrow. The frogs are croaking as I write this. That must be a good sign! (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2007)

File.Name: 7206b_7204-050.jpg
Notes: Pretty Cortinarius mushroom (there are many species) pushing up a large "mushrump" of oak leaves in the woods. This mushroom has violet colors on cap and stem, and rusty-brown hairs on the stem stained by falling spores. It also has the remnants of a white universal veil on the cap that once enclosed the mushroom "egg" from cap to base. When I dug this mushroom up, it had a very long hand-sized stalk with a small bulbous base. I guess it's in the Cortinarius sodagnitus group of the subgenus Bulbopodium. It doesn't really matter what it is. The mushroom is hard to recognize, and probably bitter. *Sigh* Pretty though! (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2007)

File.Name: 7207a_7202-132.jpg
Notes: Tiny LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - and an even tinier sibling - growing from dead wood and fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the woods. The little white scales arlong the rim of the mushroom cap are a sign of a "universal veil" of fibers that once covered cap and stem. Compare the small mushroom cap below. Part of the art of learning mushrooms is spotting these clues of what the mushroom once was. The sun was going down. This is a 2 second exposure in the shadows. (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 February 2007)

File.Name: 7208a_7208-103.jpg
Notes:
A pair of Bolete mushrooms in the woods near Lake Cachuma. I guess they are Oak-Loving Boletes (Boletus dryophilus) because of the brilliant colors. The mushroom on the left has been taken over with an infection from another mold-like fungus. The mushroom on the right looks better, but it may have caught the same infection.

The infection is probably caused by the Bolete Eater (Hypomyces chrysospermum fungus that consumes boletes and turns them into unrecognizable bright yellow masses of spores. Bolete mushrooms have pores instead of gills, but the pores of this mushroom are obscure and hard to see. I guess it's "stuffed up" from the infection. The mushrooms are edible when fresh, but don't eat spoiled food. Note how the stem has blue stains where I touched it. There are other mushrooms that stain blue, but this isn't one of those. (Santa Ynez Valley, 8 February 2007)

File.Name: 7208b_7208-109.jpg
Notes: A new cluster of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from buried wood near Lake Cachuma today. They are poisonous, but the greenish color tones and tough texture are not inviting. These mushrooms are orange like the delicious Chanterelles (Cantharellus cibarius). Go on a mushroom walk with someone who knows. You won't confuse them after you've seen them both. (Santa Ynez Valley, 8 February 2007)

File.Name: 7209a_7208-106.jpg
Notes: New leaflets of native California Maiden-Hair (Adiantum jordanii) fern in the woodland near Lake Cachuma. The black stems are so smooth you can wear them if you have pierced ears! (Santa Ynez Valley, 8 February 2007)

File.Name: 7209b_7208-099.jpg
Notes: Pretty new leaves of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae family. This interesting perennial is usually flowering by now, but we're having a very dry year in our part of California. I'm happy to find the Peonys showing signs of life, and there's a good chance of rain this weekend to keep them going! (Santa Ynez Valley, 8 February 2007)

File.Name: 7210a_7210-018.jpg
Notes:
Despite their reputation, flies are quite fastidious about personal hygene. I guess they have to be.

I believe this is a Pomace Fly aka Small Fruit Fly in the family Drosophilidae (order Diptera). It's kin to the "fruit flies" I remember studying in high school biology. They breed in rotting mushrooms (among other things). This fly is carefully grooming its dark wings with its hind legs while resting on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf near the creek. Flies have special bristles on their legs to do this. Compare my [Previous] photo of the same fly on the same leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2007)

File.Name: 7210c_7210-021.jpg
Notes: Pretty little golden Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) resting on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf near the creek. It's interesting that these flower flies are some of the first and last flies I find every year. We have many different species. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2007)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 7210d_7203-019.jpg
Notes: Little Leaf Hopper (family Cicadellidae) insect on leaves of a young Bush Lupine (Lupinus longifolius in the Fabaceae family) in the canyon. The leaves are silvery-white, so the blue colors must be a white balance thing. Insects are still scarce in the winter woods, so every one is interesting. It is warm today with a tropical storm moving in, and I found several new insects in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2007)

File.Name: 7210e_7209-135.jpg
Notes: We've had this kind of cloudy weather for several days now. It sure looks like rain, but we've only had drizzle so far. Maybe we'll get the rain we need tonight. The frogs are croaking. That's gotta be a good sign! (Santa Ynez Valley, 9 February 2007)

File.Name: 7211a_7211-041.jpg
Notes:
Raindrops and Rosemary...

We got more than an inch of much-needed rain last night. I was hoping for more, but this helps. Busy day, but I got a few photos of raindrops hanging from the stamens and style of the Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis) flowers in the garden. You can see the woods upside down in the raindrop. The 14-pointed starburst has something to do with the 7-sided diaphram of my lens. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2007)

I was busy with school stuff today, and music. We went to see Prince Toumani DiabatÈ play kora (an African harp) at a local church. What a treat to hear such a virtuoso from 10 feet away in a small venue. I guess that's even worth missing some new photos after the rain!

File.Name: 7211b_7211-040.jpg
Notes:
Raindrops and Rosemary...

We did get an inch+ of much-needed rain last night. I was hoping for more, but this helps. Busy day, but I got a few photos of raindrops hanging from the stamens and style of the Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis) flowers in the garden. The starbursts and prisms have something to do with the seven-sided diaphram of my lens. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2007)

File.Name: 7212a_7210-015.jpg
Notes:
Spring is making moves in our canyon. These are the swelling male staminate catkins of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek. You can see that the catkins are originally enclosed by a hairy brown bract. The swelling stalk bursts out of the bract and exposes two green leafy bracts at the base as well as the scaled catkin that will open to expose the pollen-bearing stamens within. Yes, it's all very sexual. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2007)

This is my favorite photo today because it's the sort of ordinary event - buds on the willows - that I would walk right past if I wasn't always looking for a new photo to post here on my f'log. Thank you Fotolog. I see things and moments - and sometimes capture them - that I would never otherwise notice.

File.Name: 7212b_7210-025.jpg
Notes: I found our first new flowers of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) in the woods last weekend. The flowers always remind me of a dancer lifting her red skirt in the air to show her pink petticoats. It's beautiful how the pale red male stamens (the dancer's "red shoes") clasp the green-tipped female style. The red sepals catch our eye, but the white "petticoats" are the real petals. This gooseberry has glandular flowers and leaves. Look close and you can see little hairs with a pinhead-like gland on the tip. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2007)

File.Name: 7212c_7210-028.jpg
Notes:
New flowers of California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) in the canyon. The tree is related to avocado and cinnamon and sassafras - all in the Laurel (Lauraceae) plant family, and all with interesting tastes and smells. We use the leaves for cooking. but don't use too much! (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2007)

The flowers are interesting. They come in little buds covered with brown bracts. You can see a single flower inside the opening bud on the right covered with three sepal-like green bracts. These open to expose the six yellow petals, though my book says they are really sepals. There are 9 stamens - one for each petal and three extra inner stamens with a pair of glands at the base.

File.Name: 7213a_7210-009.jpg
Notes: Looking eye to eye to eye to eye (and I and I) with a little pencil-erasor size Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. It's strange to make personal eye contact with any critter as small as this, but the spider looked right at ne and moved back under the oak leaf aware of my presence. See my [Previous] photo. I love the punk hair and white beard! (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2007)

File.Name: 7213b_7210-007.jpg
Notes: Tiny pencil-erasor size Jumping Spider (Salticidae) hiding in the shade between two Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves after I startled it getting my [Next] photo. This was a difficult exposure with spider, leaves, and shadows. I like the symmetry between leaf and shadow. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2007)

File.Name: 7214a_7210-013.jpg
Notes:
I only have eyes for you...
Spider Valentine with a heart...

The spider is a little pencil-erasor size Jumping Spider (Salticidae). The heart is a fungus infection on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. See my [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2007)

File.Name: 7215a_7209-145.jpg
Notes:
Pretty flower head of native Blue Dicks (Dichelostemma capitatum), aka Brodiaea for the squeamish, in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family. The afternoon sun is setting through the clouds of an approaching storm, but the low light and long exposure brings out the delicate colors like a painting. This exposure is 1/10 second @ f/16 @ ISO 400. See my [Previous] photos today. (Santa Ynez Valley, 9 February 2007)

I had some time after school last Friday before an evening event. I was surprised to find real spring flowers along Figueroa Mountain Road not far from school. I remember thunderstorms in October that dropped lots of rain here that missed us at home. Woody shrubs have been flowering on San Marcos Pass, but it's been so dry this winter that most of our annuals and perennials haven't even started. We finally had rain over the past two weeks, so I hope we catch up soon.

See my photo of the clouds on the same day at
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/18668418
to appreciate what a dark day this was. It was a challenge to get these photos.

File.Name: 7215b_7209-144.jpg
Notes: New whorled flowers of native Sky Lupine (Lupinus nanus) in the woods on Midland School property at the base of Figueroa Mountain. The afternoon sun is setting through the clouds of an approaching storm, but the low light and long exposure brings out the delicate colors like a painting. This exposure is 1/10 second @ f/16 @ ISO 400. See my other photos today. (Santa Ynez Valley, 9 February 2007)

File.Name: 7215c_7209-136.jpg
Notes: New California Buttercup (Ranunculus californicus) flower in the woods on Midland School property at the base of Figueroa Mountain. The sun is setting through clouds of an approaching storm, but the low light and long exposure brings out delicate colors. This exposure is 1/20 second @ f/18 @ ISO 400. See my [Next] two photos. (Santa Ynez Valley, 9 February 2007)

File.Name: 7216a_7215-227.jpg
Notes: This sculpted sandstone rock wall is maybe 15 feet (5 meters) tall. It's a shallow cave protected by the overhanging rock above. There are ancient Native American Chumash rock art pictographs painted on this rock and other rocks nearby. See my [Previous] photos. The old Chumash rock art is disappearing as the elements take their toll, like a faded newspaper clipping photo of a former lover we try to remember. What was she dreaming of way back then? Magic places... (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2007)

File.Name: 7216b_7215-232.jpg
Notes:
I call this kind of sculpted sandstone outcrop "wind rock" - I'm not sure where I got the name, though I'm sure wind and rain are responsible for the amazing shapes. There must be something else special about the rock itself. These wind rock formations are not common. Is this some kind of fossil structure?

There is ancient Native American Chumash rock art in the smooth kidney bean-shaped area in the lower left, and more on rocks nearby. Look close, and you can see bits of red pigment and the rude initials "MP" scratched over them by some gringo visitor. Seclusion is good protection, but maybe not good enough. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2007)

The sandstone shapes remind me of the "Alien" movies.

File.Name: 7216c_7215-213.jpg
Notes: Our mountains are haunted by these ancient Native American Chumash rock art images that I find here and there in secluded places in the mountains. I don't know what they mean, but they speak to me, like a whisper from the past I don't quite understand. The ancient pictographs are slowly disappearing due to wind and rain and people and time. It's important not to touch the rock or even breath on it if you find one of these sites. Even the moss and lichens on the sandstone take a toll. There are at least a dozen of these "painted caves" in our mountains, mostly hidden in remote places like this one. See my [Next] and [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2007)

File.Name: 7216d_7215-255.jpg
Notes: There are many sandstone rock outcrops in our mountains surrounded by oaks, but this particular rock is special. I took my after-school Hike Club kids to this secret place yesterday. This is just a few miles from home. See my [Next] few photos. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2007)

File.Name: 7216e_7209-133.jpg
Notes: Delicate flowers of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the woods on Midland School property at the base of Figueroa Mountain. I'm still waiting to find them at home in this dry year. It's a woodland native in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family related to the weedy yellow mustards that cover the California hills. The afternoon sun is setting through the clouds of an approaching storm, but the low light and long exposure is perfect to bring out the delicate colors. This exposure is 1/10 second @ f/16 @ ISO 400. (Santa Ynez Valley, 9 February 2007).

File.Name: 7216f_7209-134.jpg
Notes:
The delicate flowers of Canyon Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) remind me of ballet dancers. The leaves and flowers lack the gandular hairs of the Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) that is more common near home. The sun is setting behind clouds - the exposure is 1/4 second @ f/18 @ ISO 400. (Santa Ynez Valley, 9 February 2007)

Compare my photo of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/18712242
CommonNames: Canyon Gooseberry; Bitter Gooseberry; Bitter Gooseberry
ScientificName: Ribes californicum; Ribes amarum; hoffmannii; Ribes amarum; hoffmannii
associatedTaxa: Ribes californicum; Ribes amarum; hoffmannii; Ribes amarum; hoffmannii

File.Name: 7217a_7217-044.jpg
Notes: Mating Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) in the woods today. There is a slight problem here, maybe they didn't read the manual! I wonder if the larvae make jokes about choo-choo trains. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2007)

File.Name: 7217b_7217-034.jpg
Notes: This hairy fly in the order Diptera seems like an unlikely pollinator working the male staminate catkins of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek, but here it is dusted with pollen. Willow catkins look like they should be wind-pollinated - and maybe they are - but they also have sweet nectar glands at the base of the stamens to attract insects like this. This fly was the only pollinator I saw today. The flowers may be emerging ahead of the insects in this unusual dry winter season. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2007)

File.Name: 7217c_7217-029.jpg
Notes: Unlikely pollinator working new male staminate catkins of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek. This is some kind of hairy fly in the order Diptera. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2007)

File.Name: 7217d_7217-012.jpg
Notes: I saw several of these tattered California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterflies in the woods near the creek today. These butterflies overwinter as adults - through rain, snow, wind, and cold - and then emerge in spring - today - to face hungry birds and start the circle of life again. These butterflies are heroes! (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2007)

File.Name: 7217e_7217-056.jpg
Notes: Lizard weather! Today was a perfect day - warm but breezy - feels like spring. This is a little Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) resting in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2007)

File.Name: 7218a_7218-117.jpg
Notes: Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working new flowers of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) - the other manzanita. If the bee was a cat, it would be purring. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2007)

File.Name: 7218b_7218-130.jpg
Notes:
Here is the first Shooting Star (Dodecatheon clevelandii) wildflower in the Primrose Primulaceae family) that I've seen in the woods this year. It's been a very dry winter. The annuals haven't even sprouted, but the shrubs and perennials like this are starting to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2007)

The good news is that we're getting some rain right now as I type. Let it rain!

File.Name: 7218c_7218-125.jpg
Notes: This new mushroom-like plant of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora)in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family is growing under a Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub. This is a root-parasite plant that really is part fungus. It sucks life from its host plant via a fungus in a plant-fungus-shrub triad. These interesting plants are scarce in this dry year. I've only seen a few. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2007)

File.Name: 7218d_7218-083.jpg
Notes: Here's what a machine-cleared chaparral hillside looks like after it's been shredded by a TimbCo T425 "Hydro-shredder" machine. I'm glad the operator left the Bigberry Manzanitas (Arctostaphylos glauca) and the Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the front. I have mixed feelings about this project. See my [Previous] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2007)

File.Name: 7218e_7218-087.jpg
Notes: Native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family about to be shredded by a TimbCo T425 "Hydro-shredder" machine clearing a firebreak a mile or two from home. See my [Previous] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2007)

File.Name: 7218f_7218-078.jpg
Notes:
There's a new bug in the woods. This is a TimbCo T425 "Hydro-shredder" machine shredding the chaparral to clear a firebreak a mile or two from home. I talked with the operator when he walked over to tell me to back away with my camera. These amazing machines were created in Wisconsin to pull trees, but this one is modified with a horizontal drum shredder to digest chaparral. This is independent contract work for the Forest Service. There are three of these monster machines in the mountains near home, cutting the chaparral down to bare ground to make firebreaks. The operators have been lucky that it's such a dry winter. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2007)

This is something new, and I can't find any Forest Service info about the project. I have conflicting thoughts about this. The Lorax and that other Treebeard wouldn't like it, but it sure is an effective way to make a firebreak to protect local residents like me.

There have been five ways tried to clear the local chapparal:
   * Let it burn wild
   * Controlled burn
   * Let the summer hotshot crew cut - and burn the slash
   * Let the summer hotshot crew cut - and leave the slash
   * Use the monster machines

Natural wildfires do the job in a catastrophic kind of way. Controlled burns are tricky near homes. My favorite as a naturalist is to "cut and burn" - the fire-follower wildflowers thrive. "Cut and leave" isn't so good for the flowers, and it sure is hard to walk around. The problem with both "cut" methods is that the stump-sprouting chaparral shrubs come back in just a few years. I've been photographing the fire-follower wildflowers on new firebreaks in previous summers. I reckon the shrubs take over in three years.

I watched this monster machine shred chaparral today. It not only shreds the chaparral, but it also shreds the soil to mulch the plant material and probably destroy the rootstalks of the shrubs. I worry that all these machine-cleared areas will come back as invasive weeds like Star Thistle. What about mushrooms? Time will tell.

File.Name: 7219a_7219-163.jpg
Notes:
Fruiting Moss (Bryophyta) sporophytes in the canyon, caught in a raindrop, and reflecting the world. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2007)

I am curious why the woods in this raindrop are right-side up rather than upside-down. Compare my recent photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/18689590

We had 3/4 inch (2 cm) of rain last night - not a lot, but these little rains are keeping the woods alive in our dry winter. I'm on vacation from school this week. What a pleasure finding new photos every day when the woods are fresh!
Locality: 2 cm) of rain last night - not a lot

File.Name: 7219b_7219-178.jpg
Notes: Tiny Mycena acicula mushroom growing from the fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. The spines on the oak leaf in the photo are 1 mm or less. This is the smallest mushroom I know by name. I only noticed it because I've seen it before. Why is anything so small also so beautiful - especially a mushroom whose only job is to drop spores. Nature is like that. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2007)

File.Name: 7219c_7219-136.jpg
Notes: I caught this pretty spider in a sunbeam in the woods. I guess it's a Cobweb Weaver aka Combfooted Spider (Theridiidae) because it doesn't have an organized web. There are conflicting geometries here! I love the contrast between the web and the seven-sided polygon flare that is an internal reflection of the seven-sided diaphram of my favorite Nikon 105mm micro lens. The flare is a refraction thing, but doesn't it look like the spider is grasping the heptagonal wafer of light between its legs? (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2007)

File.Name: 7219d_7219-151.jpg
Notes: Green "Stink Bug" (order Hemiptera, maybe family Pentatomidae) in the Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) tree that is starting to flower by the creek. The bug has a mighty proboscis folded under its body, but I don't understand that sucking mouth part - it seems to be made of two different parts. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2007)

File.Name: 7219e_7219-134.jpg
Notes: Tiny Bee or Wasp (order Hymenoptera) resting on a native flower of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family in the woods. Insects are starting to emerge. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2007)

File.Name: 7219f_7219-159.jpg
Notes: After I noticed one, I saw dozens more of these caterpillars on a Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Diplacus longiflorus) shrub near the creek. I guess all the bristly hairs are to fend off egg-laying parasites like `Syrphid Flower Flies (family Syrphidae, order Diptera). Maybe they are checkerspots? (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2007)
CommonNames: Bush Monkey Flower; Syrphid Flower Flies
ScientificName: Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus; Syrphidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus; Syrphidae; Diptera

File.Name: 7220a_7220-223.jpg
Notes:
New Shooting Star (Dodecatheon clevelandii) wildflower in the Primrose Primulaceae family) opening in the woods. It's still working to spread its petals back into shooting-star position. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2007)

These pretty native flowers are just starting to flower in our mountains. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/18834693

It's interesting that the opening flower has red stamens, but they become blue when mature. How does this work?

File.Name: 7220b_7220-190.jpg
Notes:
You never know what you'll find in the woods! That's why I keep at it every day, and usually find something new and surprising.

This is the streaming plasmodium of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes) on a decaying branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It (or they) are a remarkable critter that's part fungus and part giant amoeba. They (or it) live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in a substrate like decaying wood. Sometimes they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" that can flow and congregate. It will finally dry and form little mushroom-like structures to release tiny spores that will disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one decaying branch to another. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2007)

Some slime molds develop exquisite structures as they mature. See my photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/13257621
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/9511218
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/9094716

File.Name: 7220c_7220-213.jpg
Notes:
Pretty LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - growing from the green Moss (Bryophyta) on a rocky bank in the woods. They appear to be white-spored with no veils, but I have no idea what they are. I know there are some mushrooms that only grow on moss. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2007)

We were in the clouds today between winter storms, but low light and long exposures are good to bring out the colors. This exposure is 3 seconds @ f/22 @ ISO 200. Of course I used a tripod - I almost always do. No one is that steady!

File.Name: 7220d_7220-181.jpg
Notes: Little black-spored Inky Cap (Coprinus sp.) mushroom in the woods, with new leaves of a native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum Xanti) shrub. We were in a cloud today between winter storms. The low light and long exposures are good to bring out the colors. This photo is 1/2 second @ f/22 @ ISO 200. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2007)

File.Name: 7220e_7220-240.jpg
Notes:
New swelling flower buds of a native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the Maple (Aceraceae) family. Once the engine of Spring revs up, it's hard to stop! (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2007)

It's still a very dry winter rain season for us. Trees and shrubs and some perennials are trying to keep to the usual schedule, but there are few annuals seedlings. There's a good chance of more rain tomorrow night. Every little bit helps.

File.Name: 7221a_7219-149.jpg
Notes: Male stamens emerging from the scales on a new catkin of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek - commonplace, but elegant. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2007)

File.Name: 7221b_7219-132.jpg
Notes: Ripe berries of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. Look close and you can see the tiny pin-like stalked glands on the berries, like little cloves. The berries are edible, but bland. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2007)

File.Name: 7221c_7218-071.jpg
Notes: New flowers of native Canyon Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae family. You can see the ovary at the base of each flower starting to swell into a berry. The ripe fruit are edible and tasty if you can get past the spines. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2007)

File.Name: 7221d_7220-220.jpg
Notes: Beautiful inside-out Shooting Star (Dodecatheon clevelandii) flower in the Primrose Primulaceae family) opening in the woods. Compare my [Previous] photo of another flower just starting to fold back its petals back into shooting-star position. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2007).

File.Name: 7222a_7222-248.jpg
Notes:
New flowers of Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the canyon near home, with raindrops. This is a woodland native perennial in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family related to the weedy introduced yellow mustards that cover the California hills. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2007)

We had a good rain today, enough to get the creek flowing for the first time this year. We finally had a break in the rain in the late afternoon. I did a rain-walk with camera, tripods, and many plastic bags for cover.

File.Name: 7222b_7222-261.jpg
Notes:
Not a good photo day - it rained all day until we finally had a break laye this afternoon when the sun was low. Nothing to lose, so I pointed my camera up at a raindrop hanging from this native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) over my head that is just starting to flower. That's a new flower inside the raindrop, with gray sky behind. I bumped contrast (a lot) and fiddled with the histogram (like usual), but this is not "photoshopped" in any other way. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2007)

What a strange image of something real - I want to come back and work with this.

File.Name: 7222c_7222-246.jpg
Notes:
Like a strange sci-fi ocean monster walking through the woods blasting slime instead of fire!

Nah - but this strange Earthstar (Astreus hygrometricus or kin) fungus under the oaks does seem to have legs, like it crept in on starfish-like feet from somewhere else. It's a double-skinned puffball mushroom. The thick outer skin opens like a star and curves down to lift the inner thin-skined spore sack to expose the spores to the elements. After the good rain today, the spore mass is flowing like slime. The thin inner membrane and spores will soon disappear, but the thick rinds persist in the woods for months, like washed-up shells on the beach. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2007)

File.Name: 7222d_7222-255.jpg
Notes:
I guess insects do the same thing as we do when it rains - find shelter and get out of the %&#@ rain!

These are Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) huddling under a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the rainy woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2007)
CommonNames: Convergent Lady Beetles; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 7222e_7222-249.jpg
Notes: Raindrops on a staminate catkin of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek. Each raindrop reflects the woods in its own way, like so many parallel universes that are not quite the same. I guess that's how we all see the world. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2007)

File.Name: 7222f_7222-250.jpg
Notes:
Raindrops on a staminate catkin of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek, reflecting the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2007)

We had a good rain today, enough to get the creek flowing for the first time this year. We finally had a break in the rain in the late afternoon. Not much sun, but it was fun trying to shoot the raindrops.

File.Name: 7223a_7219-143.jpg
Notes:
Tasty Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom pushing up through the fallen oak leaves in the woods. I found several groups of these wonderful mushrooms in the woods this week, but I didn't pick them before the rain yesterday. No problem. I'll wait another day or two to let them dry out, and they'll be just fine. Yum! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2007)

These mushrooms are edible and delicious, but be sure you know what you're picking! Don't confuse them with Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms that grow from buried wood:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/18631171

No time for photos today, I'm catching up with school work after a good vacation week. Just as well. It was very windy today as our last storm moved through after dropping 1-1/4 inch (3 cm) of much needed rain. We still only have about 1/3 normal rainfall. Isn't this supposed to be an El NiÒo year?
Locality:
Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms that grow from buried wood:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/18631171

No time for photos today

File.Name: 7223b_7204-056.jpg
Notes: Pretty little LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - growing from the oak leaves. I'm not sure what they are, but they look confortable snuggling together, and I see remnants of a veil. These caps are "Hydrophanous" - they change color and texture as they dry out. I got this photo before the rain yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2007)

File.Name: 7223c_7217-021.jpg
Notes: Strange Black Elfin Saddle (Helvella lacunosa) mushroom in the woods, the first I've seen this year. I call it the "Brain Mushroom" because that`s what it looks like. Said to be edible, but I'm not tempted. Sometimes when you touch the cap, it shoots off a little cloud of spores! (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2007)

File.Name: 7224a_7224-008.jpg
Notes:
This strange little mosquito-size fly (order Diptera) is dusted with pollen from the male staminate catkins of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek. I guess this is a Dance Fly in the family Empididae. My books say that most species in the family are predacious, but some visit flowers. I hope it still dances! (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2007)

Interesting - I captured the same kind of fly on the same Willow tree almost exactly a year ago:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/12640730

I still have school work to finish, but I couldn't resist taking a walk with my camera this morning. I'll have to catch up with work tomorrow, so I saved some photos to post. Life is busy, but good!

File.Name: 7224b_7224-009.jpg
Notes:
Bristly fly in the order Diptera working the male staminate catkins of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek. It seems like an unlikely pollinator, but here it is dusted with pollen. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2007)

It's hard to photograph something as dark as this fly along with something as bright as this flower!

File.Name: 7224c_7224-005.jpg
Notes:
Green "Stink Bug" (order Hemiptera, maybe family Pentatomidae) in the Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) tree that is starting to flower by the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2007)

This bug is very slow moving. I think I got a photo of the same bug on the same willow branch a few days ago:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/18854907

File.Name: 7224d_7224-028.jpg
Notes: I guess this is a little Cobweb Weaver aka Combfooted Spider (Theridiidae) spider. It's perched under a leaf of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus). I like the curves of the leaves and angles of the legs. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2007)
CommonNames: Cobweb Weaver; Combfooted Spider; Greenbark Ceanothus

File.Name: 7225a_7224-026.jpg
Notes: New flowers of a native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the Maple (Aceraceae) family exploding from the buds. Sometimes that's what you have to do! (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2007)

File.Name: 7225b_7224-014.jpg
Notes: Interesting flowers of California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) in the canyon. This tree is related to avocado and cinnamon and sassafras - all in the Laurel (Lauraceae) plant family, and all with interesting tastes and smells. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2007)

File.Name: 7225c_7224-004.jpg
Notes:
I guess this is a Dance Fly in the family Empididae of the insect order Diptera working a male staminate catkin of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2007)

I've been inside all day reading student lab books and figuring grades. This looks like a pretty good way to spend the day!

File.Name: 7226a_7219-139.jpg
Notes:
New sprouting leaves of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Honeysuckle (Caprifoliaceae) family after a rain, with the sun and a galaxy of sunspots reflected and refracted from raindrops on the oak leaves behind. I guess the seven-sided polygons are an internal reflection of the seven-sided diaphram in my favorite Nikon 105mm micro lens. I'd rather call them magic! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2007)

I'm back in school today, but I have lots more photos from "winter break" vacation last week. It feels like more rain tonight to keep the woods alive in this dry year.

File.Name: 7226b_7217-019.jpg
Notes: Tiny Moss (Bryophyta) sporophytes in the canyon, growing into the sun from a still-shaded rock. I follow the light beams for winter photos, but these "moss fruit" look like they're lit from within. Mosses are commonplace, but they have a strange lifecycle with alternating haploid and diploid forms. I guess we do too, but we don't usually think of it that way! (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2007)

File.Name: 7226c_7220-207.jpg
Notes: New fronds of California Polypody (Polypodium californicum) ferns on a mossy rock bank in the woods where the sun never shines. These delicate ferns completely disappear in our dry summer and reappear like magic with the winter rains. This exposure is 2 seconds @ f/22 @ ISO 400. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2007)

File.Name: 7227a_7220-183.jpg
Notes: I guess this pretty, solitary mushroom is a Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silicola) or kin. It has a cap that bruises yellow on the margin (look close) and a long tapered stem and a single ring that also bruise yellow, and a bulbous base that does not turn bright yellow when cut. This mushroom had a pleasant anise-like smell and taste. It's said to be edible, but I find the local Agaricus group really confusing, so I usually stick to a nibble. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2007)

File.Name: 7227b_7218-105.jpg
Notes: Brown "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) in the hairy branches of a native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the woods. It's the same size and shape as the green "Stink Bugs" (order Hemiptera, maybe family Pentatomidae) that I've been finding in the willows at home. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2007)
CommonNames: True Bug; Eastwood Manzanita; Stink Bugs
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Hemiptera; Pentatomidae
associatedTaxa: Hemiptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Hemiptera; Pentatomidae

File.Name: 7227c_7217-042.jpg
Notes:
Strange critter in the woods. At first I thought it was a Crustacean (Crustacea) - related to a shrimp or sowbug - because of the many body segments and lobster-like look. Then I thought maybe it's a Silverfish (order Thysanura). It does have silvery scales that match the quartz fragments in the sandstone.

Now I'm pretty sure this is a "Jumping Bristletail" insect in the order Archaeognatha. It looks like a silverfish with three tail-like filaments, but it has a humped thorax and eyes that touch each other on top of the head. It's a primative insect. There are fossil forms of this critter dating back to the Devonian period. Here it is in our canyon. It may be old-school, but it's keeping up! (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2007)

File.Name: 7228a_7217-067.jpg
Notes: New Crocus (Iridaceae) flower in the garden. Spring is coming even though we had snowflakes as I left for school this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2007)

File.Name: 7228b_7220-182.jpg
Notes: Solitary Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silicola or kin) mushroom in the woods. See my [Previous] photo for another view of the same mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2007)

File.Name: 7301a_7301-126.jpg
Notes: Small cascade along San Jose Creek this afternoon with my after-school Hike Club kids. It's dark shade - the exposure is 1.5 second @ f/16 @ ISO 400. The blue color is a white balance thing, though I did have my camera set for shade. I tried changing the color balance with my computer, but I like this better. Notice the little streams of bubbles - like electric sparks - radiating from the base of the falls. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2007)

File.Name: 7301b_7301-108.jpg
Notes: Reflections of Sycamore and Alder trees in a pool along San Jose Creek this afternoon. The sky is bright blue, but the canyon is shady and dark. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2007)

File.Name: 7302a_7302-199.jpg
Notes:
First new walnut-sized flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. The flowers always face the earth. I had to lay down on the ground and prop the flower up a bit to get this photo in the afternoon light today. This is about as open as they get until they drop their petals.

Peonies used to be grouped in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family that all have many separate stamens and pistils. They are considered primative flowers. They usually hide under the chaparral, but this area near Lake Cachuma burned a few years ago to give them light. The perennial roots used to be collected for export to China as a medicinal herb. I'd rather find the flowers. (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 March 2007)

File.Name: 7302b_7302-193.jpg
Notes: New flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae family just starting to open to the world. See my [Next] and [Previous] photos. (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 March 2007)

File.Name: 7302c_7302-190.jpg
Notes: New grape-sized flower bud of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae family. The buds are like tight little cabbages wrapped with bits of red wrapping paper by a child. See my [Next] two photos. (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 March 2007)

File.Name: 7303a_7303-071.jpg
Notes: Spider love! I guess these are mating Cobweb Weavers aka Combfooted Spiders (Theridiidae) spiders in the native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) shrub in front of our house. I'm sure that's the male on top with the more slender abdomen. Mating is difficult for spiders because they are such consummate solitary hunters. It's hard for us too, but it happens. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2007)
CommonNames: Cobweb Weavers; Combfooted Spiders; Greenbark Ceanothus

File.Name: 7303b_7303-030.jpg
Notes: Look close to see this long-legged translucent Spider (Arachnida) under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) near the creek with the sun behind. The spider is huddled next to a pair of tiny galls made by Gall Wasps (family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera). The split stem in the upper left is another gall. There's a community of life here, and great camouflage. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2007)
CommonNames: Spider; Coast Live Oak; Gall Wasps

File.Name: 7303c_7303-016.jpg
Notes: I'm not sure what this interesting long-legged Spider (Arachnida) is. It's perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) near the creek. I find similar spiders sitting on their orb-webs over flowing water in the creek. Maybe it's a Long-Jawed Orb-Weaver (Tetragnathidae) wondering where the creek water is in this dry year? (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2007)
CommonNames: Spider; Coast Live Oak; Long-Jawed Orb-Weaver

File.Name: 7303d_7303-032.jpg
Notes: Looking eye to eye to eye to eye (and I and I) with a little pencil-erasor size Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. It's looking at me, and raising its whiskered chelicerae "jaws" to scare me off. It didn't work. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2007)

File.Name: 7303e_7303-005.jpg
Notes: Unknown fly (order Diptera) working male staminate catkins of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek. This fly is gathering pollen from the stamens. Look under its head to see how delicately it is holding the stamen to get the pollen. Some flies like pollen, and some prefer nectar. That's how nature works. Compare my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2007)

File.Name: 7303f_7303-021.jpg
Notes: Tiny mosquito-size fly (order Diptera) working male staminate catkins of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek. I guess this is a Dance Fly in the family Empididae. It needs that long proboscis to get to the nectar glands hidden under the scales between the stamens. I sometimes saw this fly butts-up slipping its proboscis down under the scales. Compare my [Next] photo of a different fly on the same flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2007)

File.Name: 7304a_7303-059.jpg
Notes: A group of Deer Mushrooms (Pluteus cervinus) growing from a fallen log of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. The log was quite rotten. I could break it apart with my hands. Note that the gills are turning flesh colored from the pink spores. The mushrooms name is interesting. They do have deer-colored brown caps, but they also have microscopic cystidia cells on the gills that have little horns like antlers. They are said to be edible, but I haven't tried them. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2007)

File.Name: 7305a_7304-098.jpg
Notes: Aphid-herding Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) tending her cattle-like Aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) on an almost flowering branch of a native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) shrub. The ants tend the aphids for their sweet honeydew exudations. The ants are helpers, not preditors. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2007) Last year, these ants maintained visible colonies of aphids all winter long in the Ceanothus, but this is the first time I've seen them this year. I guess every year is different. These associations between very different insects are fascinating, the stuff of science fiction.
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus

File.Name: 7305b_7304-096.jpg
Notes:
Aphid-herding Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) tending their cattle-like Aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) on an almost flowering branch of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus).

Look very close and you might spot a preditor that the tending ants have missed! Start at the lower ant and follow its middle leg up to the flower buds. Right above that is a green caterpillar-like larva going after the next aphid. It's caterpillar-like, but I guess it's the larva of a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera). It has a good trick to evade those watchful ants. I only noticed it on my computer screen. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2007)
CommonNames: Ants; Aphids; Greenbark Ceanothus; Syrphid Flower Fly
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Syrphidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Syrphidae; Diptera

File.Name: 7305c_7304-095.jpg
Notes: Aphid-herding Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) tending their cattle-like Aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) on an almost flowering branch of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus). The ants tend the aphids for their sweet honeydew exudations. These ants are "talking" - communicating with their antennae by touch and smell. I wonder what they talk about? (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2007)
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus

File.Name: 7306a_7303-084.jpg
Notes:
Spider Love! Male and female spiders snuggling in a 16 leg embrace as they transfer genetic material to create a new generation of spiderlings in the woods. That's the male on the right with a slender abdomen, and the female on the left with a large abdomen full of eggs. I guess these are Cobweb Weavers aka Combfooted Spiders (Theridiidae) spiders in the native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) shrub in front of our house. See my [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2007)

I have another series of Spider Love photos starting at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/9242198
and [Previous] to that.
CommonNames: Cobweb Weavers; Combfooted Spiders; Greenbark Ceanothus

File.Name: 7306b_7303-087.jpg
Notes: Spider Love! This male spider is taking a time-out to recharge his palps on his "sperm web". All male spiders deposit tiny drops of sperm on a special "sperm web" and then pick it up with the specialized leg-like pedipalps on their head to deliver sperm to their lady to make babies. This spider has his legs in the air, but it's about to dip its engorged palps into the web to pick up new sperm. Consummation happens when he intoduces this genetic package from palp to the female`s epigyne - the process known as "sperm induction". They get to snuggle too! (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2007)

File.Name: 7306c_7303-065.jpg
Notes: Spider love! I guess these are mating Cobweb Weavers aka Combfooted Spiders (Theridiidae) spiders in the native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) shrub in front of our house. I'm sure that's the male on top with the more slender abdomen. The female has a larger abdomen full of eggs. This sexual dimorphism between male and female makes it hard to identify the species on their own. Catching them together is the only way to be sure. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2007)
CommonNames: Cobweb Weavers; Combfooted Spiders; Greenbark Ceanothus

File.Name: 7306d_7303-072.jpg
Notes: Aphid-herding Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) tending their cattle-like Aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) on an almost flowering branch of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2007)
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: 7307a_7303-042.jpg
Notes: Pretty metallic Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) resting on a branch of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis). I can tell by the pollen stains on its face and legs that it's been working the staminate catkins of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2007)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Coyote Bush; Arroyo Willow
ScientificName: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: 7307b_7303-077.jpg
Notes: New caterpillars on a leaf of native California Figwort aka Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. I've been finding these social web-spinning caterpillars on several native plants in the same family. I guess this is the caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly. I look forward to an eclosion of butterflies later this spring! (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2007)
CommonNames: California Figwort; Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Figwort; Variable Checkerspot

File.Name: 7307c_7303-014.jpg
Notes: Fancy caterpillar on a native shrub of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Diplacus longiflorus) near the creek. It's posed above a boat-like open seed pod of the shrub. I guess this is the caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly. I've been finding the same caterpillars on Figwort and Penstemon plants, all in the in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) plant family. I look forward to an eclosion of butterflies! (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2007)
CommonNames: Bush Monkey Flower; Variable Checkerspot; Figwort
ScientificName: Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus; Euphydryas chalcedona; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus; Euphydryas chalcedona; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: 7308a_7303-027.jpg
Notes:
These little Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus) mushrooms are one of my favorites. They are too small and thin to consider eating, but they have exquisite form and color. The caps are deep wine-red with widely-spaced tinted gills. Usually the caps have a frosty white coating, but maybe it's too dry in this drought year. The stems are smooth like polished metal, but brittle. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2007)

The National Weather Service released a report today that this is the driest rain season to date in Los Angeles since records began in 1877. We're not that bad off in Santa Barbara County, but we've still only had about 1/3 normal rainfall. A "March Miracle" rain is still possible, but it feels warm and dry this week. Mushrooms will soon become scarce.

File.Name: 7308b_7303-050.jpg
Notes: This little LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - growing from the oak leaves really is brown. It has a bell-like shape and dark spores. Maybe it's some kind of Panaeolus mushroom, though it was not brittle or growing from dung. Isn't it pretty! (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2007)

File.Name: 7308c_7303-052.jpg
Notes: Well-named Red-Cracked Bolete (Boletus chrysenteron or kin) mushroom in the woods under the oaks. The pink cracks on the cap are like a fractal hot cross bun! All boletes have a sponge-like layer of tubes under the cap instead of gills. Some are delicious, but this kind is not worth collecting. It's ok to admire it though! (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2007)

File.Name: 7309a_7309-144.jpg
Notes:
Eye to eye with a brown "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) on a stem of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae family. It's the size of my little fingernail. I guess it's a "Stink Bug" (family Pentatomidae). The mouth parts are interesting. It seems to have a thin suckng tube backed by a hard guiding and piercing spear. All true bugs suck! (As oppossed to beetles that chew.) I brushed against the stem, and it fell away, so I don't think it was engaged. (Santa Ynez Valley, 9 March 2007)

Today's photos are from the woods near Lake Cachuma on my way home from school, The days are getting longer.
CommonNames: True Bug; California Peony; Stink Bug

File.Name: 7309b_7309-147.jpg
Notes:
After the party... This flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica in the Paeoniaceae family) has already shed its petals, and it's so strange inside! There are three (sometimes two) large pistils that will bear seeds, and a few stamens that haven't fallen away yet, and those strange orange "candy corn" things around the base of the flower. According to my flora, they are part of a "lobed nectary disk." (Santa Ynez Valley, 9 March 2007)

Compare my photo of the open flower last week at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/19105079

File.Name: 7309c_7309-163.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful new flower of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. With the sun behind, you can see through its petals and spot the shadow of the male stamens and the red-tipped female pistil slightly exerted from the end of the flower. I got today's photos in the woods near Lake Cachuma on my way home from school, The days are getting longer. (Santa Ynez Valley, 9 March 2007)

This plant comes up fast like a mushroom and then disappears. There is a fungal connection. This plant is a root-parasite of chaparral shrubs, probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in this place. It has some green, but the foliage is mostly red like this. I reckon it's a three-way relationship between plant-fungus-shrub in which the invisible fungus partner makes the connection between herb and shrub. I wonder if every partner benefits? Sometimes I wonder if the whole forest is connected by mycelial strands into one living thing.

Flowers are usually brightly colored to stand out from foliage to attract pollinators. Why would any plant hide its flowers in same-colored leaves?
Locality: Adenostoma fasciculatum) in this place. It has some green

File.Name: 7310a_7310-036.jpg
Notes:
Aphid-herding Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) tending her cattle-like Aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) on a flowering branch of a native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) shrub. This bearded lady ant strokes her aphids to stumulate honeydew production. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2007)

Spring is here, but this is my busy time at home. I have my school Science Fair in two weeks, and I have to prepare my own scifair project on summer flowers in our summer-dry mountains. I think it's an interesting project to document the flowers that wait to open in the dryest part of the year when most plants are done. Why are they here?
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus

File.Name: 7310b_7310-019.jpg
Notes: I guess this is a new seed leaf or cotyledon of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the woods. There are many seedlings, but few are chosen to flower and reproduce. Deer and insect parasites and foolish neighbors all take their toll. We had no flowers in the canyon last year. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2007)

File.Name: 7310c_7310-008.jpg
Notes:

File.Name: 7311a_7311-050.jpg
Notes:
The equinox is still a week away, but it must be spring! These tiny wasps or bees (order Hymenoptera) are going at it on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) near the creek. I guess that's the male above with his wings a'flutter. The intimacy of the moment shows in their touching antennae - her's pointing back and his reaching forward to touch. This photo is not "photoshopped" - the color is a refraction pattern from his rapidly beating wings. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2007)

It feels like spring today with temperatures in the 80s setting local records. I'm trying to work on my school Science Fair project on summer flowers, but I couldn't resist taking a walk on such a perfect day. The woods are dry in this drought year. It's scary to have a Red Flag Fire warning in March.

It might not show in this resampled image, but the fly's proboscis is split with a slender piece on screen-left and the larger pipe on screen-right. I guess it's picking up sats from the rock. File.Name: 7312a_7311-049.jpg
Notes:
I guess this is a California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly perched in the flowering Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) tree by the creek with the sun behind. It's a Brushfoot butterfly in the Nymphalidae family. It perches on just four legs with two tiny front legs held up in T-rex fashion. The butterfly is tattered, and it makes sense because they overwinter as adults hiding under leaves or rocks. They're not much interested in flowers. This is mating time. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2007)

The weather turned hot over the weekend. I saw several butterflies on the wing, but they didn't feel like posing. I saw Painted Ladys, some kind of Sulfur, and Brown Elfins all on the wing. So many butterflies, not enough time!

It's a "mourning" butterfly - not "morning" - despite a million google hits - because of its beautiful dark colors. I like the ripples, like the cross-grain in quality maple wood.

I've had a computer problem for a few days - I couldn't login to Fotolog or anything else, but the rest of my internet service was fine. I was getting other cryptic security messages about "certificates" with bad time stamps. Very puzzling.

Time was the clue. I finally checked my computer date/time, and noticed it was set to 2008! When I set it back to 2007, everything was fine.

I set my clock ahead an hour last weekend for the new US daylight savings time change. There's no "automatic update" on my ancient (but well-maintained) Win98 computer. I sure don't think I changed the year, but I know we humans are fallible.

I connect to the internet with Verizon DSL. A friend at school with the same sevice had the same problem. A google search found other people with the same ISP having login problems at WoW and other sites. If you have Verizon service, check your Date/Time settings!
Locality: but well-maintained) Win98 computer.  I sure don't think I changed the year

Notes:
This is my poster for the school science fair last night. It was a grand event with many interesting (and fun) student projects. I always do my own sci-fair project - so I can worry about my work rather than the kids'! (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 March 2007)

This year I went through my photos to make a collection of summer-flowering native plants. I made this poster, a slide show, and database - and presented it as a stumper:

With our typical (but globally uncommon) Mediterranean climate in the Santa Ynez Valley at school, we expect cool wet winters and hot dry summers. Spring is wildflower season, but I have more time for photos in the summer when the conditions are harsh because of our usual summer drought. Despite six months of hot weather and (almost) no rain every summer, I manage to find interesting and often beautiful native wildflowers all summer long in the mountains near my home on San Marcos Pass in the Santa Ynez Mountains.

For my science fair project, I went through my photos to make a digital herbarium and photo database of 144 species of native summer-flowering plants organized by family and species. All of these flowers appear - or at least reach their peak - between late June and October, when the conditions for wildflowers seem at their worst.

I have discovered questions rather than answers with this project. Here are my Science Fair Stumpers: What benefits do these late bloomers get by flowering during the harsh summer drought, long after the spring wildflowers are done? How do they survive? Why are there any summer flowers in our summer-dry mountains at all? Why are so many of them beautiful?
ScientificName: Mediterranean climate; digital herbarium; late bloomers
associatedTaxa: Mediterranean climate; digital herbarium; late bloomers

File.Name: 7326b_7325-039.jpg
Notes:
Strange fruit in the live oak trees...

These are insect galls on a male staminate catkin of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon. I believe the reddish horn-shaped galls are home to the larvae of tiny Gall Wasps (family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera) - maybe the Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiousus). Gall wasps insert their eggs and a dose of RNA to re-program the tree to produce the abnormal gall growth that nourishes the larvae within. These wasps have a complicated life cycle with alternating bisexual and unisexual generations forming different kinds of galls on different parts of the trees. I've noticed that these galled catkins tend to stay on the tree after flowering. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2007)

Oak tree catkins seem so ephemeral, yet they support generations of life we rarely notice. That's biodiversity!
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Gall Wasps; Two-horned Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Dryocosmus dubiousus
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Dryocosmus dubiousus

File.Name: 7329a_7325-051.jpg
Notes:
Amanita mushroom "egg" starting to hatch. I believe this is an unusual white form of the edible Coccora mushroom - Amanita calyptrata, aka A. calyptroderma and maybe A. lanei - growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods, with madrone and pine nearby. This mushroom was hidden under the woodland leaves, but I noticed a slight "mushrump" rise in the mulch and dug it up. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2007)

These mushrooms start as a featureless "egg" enclosed in a universal veil. As the mushroom cap expands, the veil tears leaving a volval patch on the cap and a cup or volva at the base. The second partial veil covers the spore-bearing gills of the mushroom. As the mushroom cap continues to expand, the skin tears away from the cap leaving a skirt-like ring on the stem.

In this photo, the universal veil is almost separated, and the partial veil is just pulling away to reveal the gills. It's an intimate act as the mushroom lowers its veils to release its spores!
ScientificName: Amanita; Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; A. lanei; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Amanita; Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; A. lanei; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 7329b_7325-061.jpg
Notes:
These are the first Amanita mushrooms I've seen in this drought year. But what are they?

I've been going through my books, and I believe this is an unusual white form of the edible Coccora mushroom - Amanita calyptrata, aka A. calyptroderma and maybe A. lanei - growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods, with madrone and pine nearby. They are big mushrooms with saucer-size caps, 6 inches (15 cm) or more across. They feel very soft, almost like marshmallows.

Typical Coccora mushrooms have yellow caps. I collected them at the same place last year, and posted photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/12925594
and [Previous] to that. This mushroom has the same soft feel and thick cottony universal veil patch on top, but it has no color at all. I'm sure this is not the deadly white Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom, but I don't plan to taste-test it! (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2007)
CommonNames: Coccora; Coast Live Oak; Coccora; Destroying Angel
ScientificName: Amanita; Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; A. lanei; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita ocreata
associatedTaxa: Amanita; Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; A. lanei; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita ocreata

This is the driest year on record for Southern California since records began in 1877. There's been less than an inch of rain in Joshua Tree this drought year. I still managed to find a few flowers on our school backpack trip last week. Nature is tough.

I managed to take 1,189 photos last week, and I kept 723 photos. I bought a new Nikon 18-200 VR lens the day before our trip. I love this new lens, though I still used my 105 micro lens for all of these close photos.

Here are ripe berries of Desert Mistletoe (Phoradendron californicum) growing as a parasite on a native Catclaw (Acacia greggii) shrub in a desert wash at Joshua Tree National Park last week. I usually see the native Phainopepla (Phainopepla nitens) birds eating the berries, but I didn't see a single bird in this drought year when the berries are scarce. The birds have been seen in the Santa Ynez Valley back home near the coast. I guess they understand the weather! (Joshua Tree National Park, 1 April 2007)
