I could feel the ancient vibes that our campsite was sacred ground. It's a Georgia O'Keeffe moment. (Joshua Tree National Park, 4 April 2007)

File.Name: 7407f_7404-419.jpg
Notes: Bush Peppergrass aka Desert Allysum (Lepidium fremontii) in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family. The little white flowers almost become invisible in the glare of the desert sun. (Joshua Tree National Park, 4 April 2007)

File.Name: 7408a_7404-465.jpg
Notes:
Look close - a pollen-dusted Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) - not a Honey Bee - working a flower of Desert Mallow (Sphaeralcea ambigua) in the Mallow (Malvaceae) family. It's a color my digital camera can't quite capture. (Joshua Tree National Park, 4 April 2007)

Today's photos are from our school backpack trip across the Pinto Basin of Joshua Tree National Park last week. This is the driest year on record for Southern California since records began in 1877. There's been less than an inch of rain in Joshua Tree in this drought year. I still managed to find a few flowers.

I have many more Joshua Tree photos, but I'll save them for the busy school days next week. This week is my spring break, and there's lots happening in the woods at home, even in a drought year!
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Honey Bee; Desert Mallow; Mallow

File.Name: 7408b_7404-495.jpg
Notes: Strange male staminate flowers of Green Ephedra aka Mormon Tea or Desert Tea - (Ephedra viridis) in the Ephedra (Ephedraceae) family. This desert shrub is a Gymnosperm that is more closely related to pines than our usual Angiosperm flowers with ovaries. Reproduction happens with these soft cones (strobili) rather than proper flowers. One of my students on our desert hike said she sneezed whenever she walked past these plants. Tea made from the dry stems has a caffeine-like kick, but I'd rather have coffee. (Joshua Tree National Park, 4 April 2007)
CommonNames: Green Ephedra; Mormon Tea; Desert Tea; Ephedra
ScientificName: Ephedra viridis; Ephedraceae; Gymnosperm; Angiosperm; strobili
associatedTaxa: Ephedra viridis; Ephedraceae; Gymnosperm; Angiosperm; strobili

File.Name: 7408c_7405-559.jpg
Notes: Wild Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a flower of Bladderpod (Isomeris arborea) in the Caper (Capparacea) family. This native shrub has an interesting distribution. It grows in desert washes and also near the beach at Point Mugu. (Joshua Tree National Park, 5 April 2007)

File.Name: 7408d_7402-227.jpg
Notes:
Sweet honey in the rock!

Julie spotted this wild Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) hive in a crack in the granite rocks near our campsite. I waited for the sun to shine into the crack for a photo, but it never did, so I used flash for this photo. Of course the desert bees find a place that's always in the shade so their hive doesn't melt! I could have tasted the honey, but it would be a painful struggle. I was happy to get the photo. (Joshua Tree National Park, 2 April 2007)

File.Name: 7408e_7405-507.jpg
Notes:
You have to study this photo to appreciate it. Look at the center...

Now you see the light desert-colored Sidewinder Rattlesnake (Crotalus cerastes) looking back at you! This beautiful but deadly venomous rattlesnake was coiled up in a desert shrub near our kitchen on our backpack trip across the Pinto Basin of Joshua Tree last week. We let it be, and it didn't bother us. The expression on this snake's face says it - don't mess with me! (Joshua Tree National Park, 5 April 2007)

File.Name: 7408f_7404-437.jpg
Notes: From the light colors and the way it moved, I guess this is a Sidewinder Rattlesnake (Crotalus cerastes). Maybe not - I can't see horns, and it was more than 3 feet (a meter) long. It gave me plenty of warning as I hiked down the wash in Joshua Tree with its startling rattle! I'm more afraid of these venomous rattlesnakes in the chaparral at home where I can't see them. (Joshua Tree National Park, 4 April 2007)

File.Name: 7409a_7409-108.jpg
Notes:
Remarkable green Bramble Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetoruw) butterfly ovipositing - laying her eggs - on a native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. I've seen these butterflies here and there for several months this winter, but they are camera-shy and hard to capture. I guess this lady was preoccupied! She doen't have tails like our typical Hairstreak butterflies, but she does rub her hindwings together in just the same way. I took many photos today, and she always has her proboscis extended when she is laying eggs, even when there are are no flowers nearby like this photo. I think we humans do the same thing when concentrating! (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2007)

It's Spring Break from school this week, and the woods are full of life despite the local drought conditions. Life is good!

File.Name: 7409b_7408-054.jpg
Notes: Beautiful butterfly with a beautiful name. This is a Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly working new flowers of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the canyon. I've been chasing these butterflies with my camera for a few weeks trying to get a photo. They don't like to pose! (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2007)

File.Name: 7409c_7409-066.jpg
Notes: Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha) in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family. It's definately spring in our mountains! (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2007)

File.Name: 7409d_7409-094.jpg
Notes: Handsome pencil-erasor size Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a dead stem of Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2007)

File.Name: 7409e_7409-021.jpg
Notes: Interesting Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) - not a Honey Bee - with long antennae snuggling into a new flower of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2007)

File.Name: 7409f_7409-083.jpg
Notes: Unusual native Easter flowers of Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis - aka F. lanceolata or F. affinis) in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family. This is an uncommon native in the Lily (Liliaceae) family that is endemic to our local mountains - rated CNPS 1B.2 - "Plants Rare, Threatened, or Endangered in California and Elsewhere". This canyon trail is the only place I've found it near home. There were just two plants, hard to photograph in the shade and wind today. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2007)
ScientificName: Fritillaria ojaiensis; F. lanceolata; F. affinis; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Fritillaria ojaiensis; F. lanceolata; F. affinis; Liliaceae

File.Name: 7410a_7410-156.jpg
Notes:
Handsome Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) in the garden showing its mating colors. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2007)

Now look closer. I can see three large gray Ticks (order Acarina, suborder Ixodides) attached to this lizard - one on the ear tympanum and two more below. Apparently these are immature forms of the same ticks that go after me in the summer. New research suggests that the lizards actually kill the bacteria that carry Lyme disease.

Our mountain ticks have a complicated life-cycle that moves from wood rat (rodent) to lizard (reptile) to human (me!). I don't quite understand the details, but I'm glad the lizards act as a filter. I want to understand this - there's info on the web.
Locality: me!).  I don't quite understand the details
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Ticks

File.Name: 7410b_7410-159.jpg
Notes: Common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) snuggling into a new flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae family. Life is good for a while, but the woods are already starting to feel dry in this drought year. It will be an interesting summer. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2007)

File.Name: 7410c_7410-157.jpg
Notes: Tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on the prickly leaves of Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine) in the garden. If you know this cosmopolitan plant than you can appreciate how small this preditor really is. I love the eyes - like aviator goggles! (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2007)

File.Name: 7410d_7408-028.jpg
Notes:
I know this is hard to see - it's hard to focus on too. That's the point! The bristles are protection against preditors.

This is a butterfly caterpillar on a leaf of native California Figwort aka Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family that came up on its own in our garden with our blessing. I took this photo on a fog-drippy morning before the sun came out, and the caterpillar's stiff hairs are perfect to hold the drops. This caterpillar is also a web-spinner, and it's webs hold more drops. I guess this will become a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly. I look forward to an eclosion of butterflies later this spring! (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2007)
CommonNames: California Figwort; Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Figwort; Variable Checkerspot

File.Name: 7410e_7409-109.jpg
Notes: Beautiful green Bramble Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetoruw) butterfly ovipositing - laying her eggs - on a native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. Look very close and you can her pushing out a new egg from her abdomen. See my [Previous] photo for another shot of this same lady butterfly. She would lay an egg or two and then move on to another plant to spread them out. I'll come back in a few weeks to look for caterpillars. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2007)

File.Name: 7411a_7411-311.jpg
Notes:
Looking into the inner heart - the sanctum sanctorum - of a Chocolate Lily (Fritillaria biflora) in the Lily (Liliaceae) family. We mortals are not supposed to see this view. The plants are only a few inches tall, and the flowers always point down to the Earth hidding their secrets. There's no room to look up. I cheated by using a little twig to prop the flower up into the sun. Compare my [Previous] photo. Maybe an act of hubris, but isn't this wildflower beautiful! (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2007)

I went up Figueroa Mountain today. I can see it from my porch across the Santa Ynez Valley to the north. There are still many flowers - many more than Joshua Tree last week - but they are fading fast in this drought year. Unfortunately, it was very windy as a little weather trough moved through today. I tried to get my photos between the gusts. I took 300+ photos, and got a few keepers.

File.Name: 7411b_7411-306.jpg
Notes: Demure flower of Chocolate Lily (Fritillaria biflora) in the Lily (Liliaceae) family growing in the grassland near the top of Ranger Peak. These plants are only a few inches tall, and the flowers always point down to the Earth hidding their secrets. But see my [Next] photo to look under the skirts. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2007)

File.Name: 7411c_7411-229.jpg
Notes:
Looking into a native flower of Catalina Mariposa Lily (Calochortus catalinae) in the Lily (Liliaceae) family - it's a rave pollination party! (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2007)

This image is NOT photoshopped! It is definately overexposed for the white flower, but just right for all the little critters inside. The red colors attract insects to the hairy yellow nectar glands, one at the base of each of the three petals. Think twice before you pick any wildflower - it might be home to this much life. Ain't nature grand!

File.Name: 7411d_7411-225.jpg
Notes: Beautiful Catalina Mariposa Lily (Calochortus catalinae) in the Lily (Liliaceae) family flowering in the grass on a very windy day. I like the sense of movement in this photo. The petals are usually white, but they often develop these lavender tints. The colored areas at the base of the petals are nectar glands inside. See my [Next] photo. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2007)

File.Name: 7411e_7411-218.jpg
Notes: Pretty native flowers of Crinkled Wild Onion (Allium crispum) in the Lily (Liliaceae) family. This plant really does taste like onion, but I'd rather find the flowers along my trail. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2007)

File.Name: 7411f_7411-241.jpg
Notes:
How windy was it today?
Well, this pretty butterfly is hanging on - it's not even trying to feed!

The butterfly is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) hanging on to native flowers of Western Wallflower (Erysimum capitatum) in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family on Figueroa Mountain today. It was very windy all day as a little weather trough moved through. I noticed a few drops, but not enough to call a sprinkle. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2007)

File.Name: 7412a_7411-316.jpg
Notes:
Brilliant new flowers of Western Wallflower (Erysimum capitatum) in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family on Figueroa Mountain yesterday with the sun behind. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2007)

We have fields of non-native invasive "wild mustards" in California, but this native mustard is something special. I tried fiddling with the colors and gave up - this photo is right from my camera after cropping and and sharpening. So are the rest of today's photos. I don't know how to work with yellow and orange colors.

It was VERY WINDY today - oak pollen and catkins everywhere! I didn't even try to take photos. It was a good day to catch up with school and computer backup stuff. What do the butterflies do when it's this windy??

My favorite photo today is my last - not "pretty" but it's a remarkable local moth!
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/20102466

File.Name: 7412b_7411-252.jpg
Notes: Elegant flower and long seed pods of Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) in the Papaveraceae family. This poppy does NOT have a disk below the flower or seed pod. This is our real California Poppy that didn't come from a wildflower seed packet. See my [Previous] two photos. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2007)

File.Name: 7412c_7411-295.jpg
Notes:
Native Tufted Poppies (Eschscholzia caespitosa) in the Papaveraceae family getting lost in the non-native Wild Oats (Avena fatua) grass near the summit of Figueroa Mountain. These are the poppies that do NOT have the disk below the flower. See my [Previous] photo. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2007)

Heh -I just got a copy of "The Invasive Species Cookbook: Conservation Through Gastronomy" by J.M. Franke, published by Bradford Street Press (1907):
   http://www.bradfordstreetpress.com/

"If you can't beat 'em, eat 'em"

Cool - but there's no recipe for using wild oats. This book need to be MUCH fatter! It really is remarkable (and a stumper) that so many invasive weeds are edible and closely related to our cultivated plants.

File.Name: 7412d_7411-258.jpg
Notes:
Golden California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) flowers and blue Sky Lupines (Lupinus nanus) near a picnic area at the summit of Figueroa Mountain yesterday. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2007)

I wonder if these poppies are really native. On the lower slopes of the mountain, the poppies are all Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) - see my next two photos. The difference is subtle at first, but obvious once you notice. Eschscholzia californica has a disk below the flower and seed pod that is visible in this photo if you look close. Eschscholzia caespitosa lacks the disk and has more subtle yellow colors.

Most poppies I see along the highway are E. californica, but away from the roads I usually find E. caespitosa. I suspect some over-zealous Forest Service employee scattered some "wildflower" seed. I wish they wouldn't do that - there's no need!
Locality: Eschscholzia caespitosa) - see my next two photos. The difference is subtle at first
CommonNames: California Poppy; Sky Lupines; Tufted Poppy
ScientificName: Eschscholzia californica; Lupinus nanus; Eschscholzia caespitosa; Eschscholzia californica; Eschscholzia caespitosa
associatedTaxa: Eschscholzia californica; Lupinus nanus; Eschscholzia caespitosa; Eschscholzia californica; Eschscholzia caespitosa

File.Name: 7412e_7411-275.jpg
Notes: Native Sky Lupine (Lupinus nanus) in the Fabaceae family with a background of Tufted Poppies (Eschscholzia caespitosa). (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2007)

File.Name: 7412f_7411-267.jpg
Notes: Remarkable little diurnal (daytime) moth with antennae worthy of Yosemite Sam! I did some web searching, and I believe this is a Longhorned Fairy Moth - lovely name - in the Adelidae family of the order Lepidoptera. It's perched on a native flower of Blue Field Gilia (Gilia capitata) in the Phlox (Polemoniaceae) family near the summit of Figueroa Mountain. I got prettier photos yesterday on the mountain, but this is my favorite critter. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2007)
CommonNames: Longhorned Fairy Moth; Blue Field Gilia; Phlox

File.Name: 7413a_7413-329.jpg
Notes:
Female Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly ovipositing - laying her eggs - on new flowers of non-native Black Mustard (Brassica nigra) in the canyon. Now I'm glad these weedy "crucifers" (family Brassicaceae) are here! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2007)

These pretty butterflies are usually camera-shy. It's a good photo tip to catch them when they're busy - like this!

File.Name: 7413b_7413-356.jpg
Notes: Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly "puddling" in the garden today - sucking the wet ground for water or minerals. These little butterflies really are blue on top, but it's hard to get that photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2007)

File.Name: 7413c_7413-342.jpg
Notes: Little Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly resting on a leaf in the woods today. I chased several of these tiny butterflies from plant to plant today, but they don't like to pose. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2007)

File.Name: 7413d_7413-368.jpg
Notes:
I'm eye-to-eye with this Grass-Skipper (order Lepidoptera - subfamily Hesperiinae - genus Hesperia) butterfly working a new Zinnia flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2007)

Heh - the skipper kinda looks like a rabbit in this photo!

File.Name: 7413e_7413-344.jpg
Notes:
Mating wasps on a leaf of native Sticky Cinquefoil (Potentilla glandulosa) in the canyon. I have to think that is the female on the left with the larger abdomen. Look at her hind legs holding her partner! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2007)

I believe these are Sawflies (family Tenthredinidae in the order Hymenoptera). They do not have constricted waists like most common wasps. Photos of mating insects are important to connect male and female as the same species. There two look very different!

This coupling happened very fast right in front of me. No time to compose a shot. It was more like tug-of-war than an act of intimacy. The wasp on the right (male?) won, dragging its mate off the leaf to the right.

File.Name: 7413f_7413-354.jpg
Notes:
Yipes - this flower is a trap!

The trap is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) ready to attack. I believe the flower is non-native Wand Mullein (Verbascum virgatum) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. This is a new weed in the mountains that is slowly spreading along Stage Coach Road down from the tavern. So far I've only seen it along the road, but now is the time to do something about it. This spider doesn't seem to care that the flower is not native! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2007)

File.Name: 7414a_7414-001.jpg
Notes:
Little female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) perched in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near the upstairs porch this evening. She's catching the last rays of sunlight today, and she's fluffed up because it's cold tonight as a weak weather trough moves in. This is the first hummer I've seen perched in the oak for months, ever since that very cold week last winter that cut back our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2007)

I had no time for photos today. Spring Break is just about gone. I spent the day at school catching up on "teacher stuff". Tomorrow is busy too. Yipes - I'll be busy until June!

File.Name: 7414b_7413-346.jpg
Notes:
Yellow form of Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. The flower heads are usually brilliant red, but now and then a plant swings to this pretty color that's not quite yellow or orange or salmon or anything else I have a name for. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2007)

This color form is not recognized as a species difference, and the CalFlora photo collection doesn't show it. Compare my recent photo of the usual red form at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/19723671

File.Name: 7414c_7411-313.jpg
Notes:
I can't resist another photo of the brilliant new flowers of Western Wallflower (Erysimum capitatum) in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family on Figueroa Mountain last week with the sun behind. There are introduced invasive "wild mustards" everwhere in California, but these natives are special. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2007)

Compare my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/20104452

File.Name: 7414d_7411-297.jpg
Notes: Many beautiful flower spikes of native Silver Bush Lupine (Lupinus albifrons?) in the Fabaceae family near the summit of Figueroa Mountain last week. We have a different bush lupine at home, and there are other species near the beach and the desert interior, and sometimes one species comes in both blue and yellow forms. You need a hand lens and good light to get all the different species straight. "Bush Lupine - oh wow" is usually good enough! (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2007)

File.Name: 7414e_7411-235.jpg
Notes:
Elegant native Red-skinned Onion (Allium haematochiton) flowers in the Lily (Liliaceae) family growing in thin soil on a sepentine outcrop. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2007)

Tech note for mcgobitta:

When I learned our wildflowers, Allium was included in the Amaryllis (Amaryllidaceae) plant family rather than the Liliaceae because the flowers come in umbels. The Jepson Flora for California (1993) simplified and combined the groups - it needs an update. Now there is an Alliaceae family in the 2003 APG II classification. The plants didn't change, just the names. I mostly use what's current, but I'm stubborn about a few oldies.

Didn't Bart Simpson say something like "God bless the person who invented synonomy"?
Locality: 1993) simplified and combined the groups - it needs an update. Now there is an Alliaceae family in the 2003 APG II classification. The plants didn't change

File.Name: 7414f_7413-335.jpg
Notes: Postpartum depression? This is the same female Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly that I captured ovipositing - laying her eggs - in my [Previous] photo. Here she is resting nearby afterwards. She let me get very close to capture a glorious full-frame image. I mean you no harm Little Wing! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2007)

File.Name: 7415a_7410-178.jpg
Notes:
Perfect native Blue-Eyed Grass (Sisyrinchium bellum) wildflowers in the Iris (Iridaceae) family growing in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2007)

Wildflowers are scarce in this drought year, but I've been looking for them this week during Spring Break. I've been busy with school stuff today. I head back to school tomorrow, as a teacher. This is the busy time!

File.Name: 7415b_7410-212.jpg
Notes: The new Apple (Malus pumila) flowers in the garden look like bursting popcorn kernels. The rose-like buds are my favorite part. It's been very windy for a few days - I hope we get some apples this year! (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2007)

File.Name: 7415c_7410-195.jpg
Notes: Unusual flowers of native Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Lily (Liliaceae) family growing at a place in the mountains that was cleared and burned a few years ago as a fire break. I often see the leaves of this native bulb under the chaparral, but they only flower when the land is cleared by design or wildfire. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2007)

File.Name: 7415d_7410-189.jpg
Notes: This is the first open flower I've seen this year of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis var. redivivus) in the Storax (Styracaceae) family. The flowers are fragrant and beautiful and unusual. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2007)

File.Name: 7415e_7407-011.jpg
Notes: This tiny diurnal (daytime) micro-moth (order Lepidoptera) would fit on a pencil erasor with room to spare - but it's so beautiful with those metallic scales on its wings. This is about as close as I can get - a tight crop near 1:1 magnification. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2007)

File.Name: 7415f_7408-046.jpg
Notes: Tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) with lunch - a tiny fly (order Diptera) with speckled wings, maybe a true Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae). Focused this close with a macro lens and no depth of field, I get to pick the subject. Those amazing spider eyes are perfect. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2007)

File.Name: 7416a_7407-016.jpg
Notes:
I studied my books tonight, and I believe these pretty mushrooms are Spring Agrocybes (Agrocybe praecox aka Pholiota praecox) or kin in the Bolbitaceae family. That's really a group of species that are very hard to tell apart without a microscope. The caps are creamy-smooth, and cracked from our dry weather. The stems have a ring but no cup or volva. You can see the rich "cigar-brown" spore deposit on the lower caps in the photo that have fallen from the higher caps. These mushrooms are in the garden, but I've found more in the woods along the road. These are said to be edible, but not recommended. That's good enough for me! (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2007)

Everyone asks if the mushroom is edible. I think it's more important to see that it's beautiful!

File.Name: 7416b_7410-166.jpg
Notes: I guess this pretty hand-sized mushroom is Entoloma rhodopolium or one of many kin in the Rhodophyllaceae family. It has a dry brown cap and "pink" or flesh-colored spores that you can see on the gills and stem. This mushroom has a "farinaceous" odor like linseed oil. One common name is Poisonous Entoloma. It's not edible. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2007)

File.Name: 7416c_7410-165.jpg
Notes:
I'm pretty sure this mushroom is "The Blusher" (Amanita rubescens) growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. It has skirt-like ring on the stem, but I didn't notice any cup at the base - the volva is said to be "friable" so it easily crumbles away. The cap has mealy white warts left from the universal veil, and it developes reddish "blushing" colors like this. It looks like a pizza! It's said to be edible when cooked, but I'm not tempted by any mushrooms in the Amanita genus that includes our most deadly fungi. That could be a fatal mistake! (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2007)

If you don't understand this language of "veil" and "volva" and "ring", then get a good book and study before you consider eating any wild mushroom! Even better than a meal: the more you learn about mushrooms, the more you will see.

File.Name: 7417a_7407-006.jpg
Notes: Our foundling house cat Narcissa found this adventive non-native Catnip (Nepeta cataria) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family that appeared in the garden. It looks like a meaningful relationship! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2007)

File.Name: 7417b_7407-002.jpg
Notes: New flower buds of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae family with the sun behind to brighten the red bristles. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2007)

File.Name: 7417c_7413-323.jpg
Notes: Radiant new flower of native Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha) in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family on the dry side our the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2007)

File.Name: 7418a_7410-198.jpg
Notes: These new flowers of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) shrubs in the Poppy (Papaveraceae) family look like little solar collector dishes. Maybe they are! (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2007)

File.Name: 7419a_7411-319.jpg
Notes:
Elegant native Catalina Mariposa Lily (Calochortus catalinae) in the Lily (Liliaceae) family with a background of Crinkled Wild Onion (Allium crispum) also in the Lily (Liliaceae) family. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2007)

The dark spots at the base of the petals are nectar glands. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/20078767
CommonNames: Catalina Mariposa Lily; Lily; Crinkled Wild Onion; Lily
ScientificName: Calochortus catalinae; Liliaceae; Allium crispum; Liliaceae

File.Name: 7420a_7420-135.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful blue flowers of native Parry's Larkspur (Delphinium parryi ssp. maritimum) in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family, with the sun behind. (Santa Ynez Valley, 20 April 2007)

I stopped for a few photos in the woods near Lake Cachuma on my way home after school today. We had an inch of much-needed rain this morning to help keep the wildflowers going in this very dry drought year!

File.Name: 7420b_7420-120.jpg
Notes:
Little woodland Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) getting nectar from the green glands of a native Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) flower in the Lily (Liliaceae) family. The flower is built like a carousel around the central column of stamens, and the ant did go round and round tasting the sweet nectar. (Santa Ynez Valley, 20 April 2007)

Obviously these flowers need a much BIGGER insect to help with pollination. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/7669357

File.Name: 7420c_7420-131.jpg
Notes: Flowers of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. All flowers are the sexual parts of plants, and they sometimes take an active role. The new bisexual flower in the back holds its female y-shaped style high above the male pollen-bearing stamens waiting to be dusted with pollen from another plant. As the flower gets older - look up front - the style drops lower so that self-pollination can occur in a pinch. I've seen this "sexual dance" between style and stamen in many different plants. (Santa Ynez Valley, 20 April 2007)

File.Name: 7421a_7421-035.jpg
Notes:
You know what it's like to move a big sofa through a small door...

This heroic little House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bird is trying to move a big stick through a small hole into it's new house on the porch railing. It took a few tries! I could almost hear the bird think as it gripped the branch in different places for leverage. The wren made it on the next try. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2007)

We have three wrens making nests at home. This nest is right outside my office, on our upstairs deck. The birds don't seem to mind me getting photos. I was standing with my camera and tripod just 12 feet (4 meters) away.

File.Name: 7421b_7421-013.jpg
Notes: Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly (order Lepidoptera) perched on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the canyon - but it's really interested in the Lotus plants in the Pea (Fabaceae) family growing nearby. These butterflies are spotted gray underneath, but they really are blue on top. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2007)
ScientificName: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Lepidoptera; Salvia mellifera; Lotus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Lepidoptera; Salvia mellifera; Lotus; Fabaceae

File.Name: 7421c_7421-008.jpg
Notes:
A fly is a fly, but some flies are different!

I guess this bristly critter is a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae, order Diptera). The females lay their eggs in or on other insects. The larvae enter and eat and eventually kill their host. Sounds horrible, but this is real organic pest control happening in the woods every day. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2007)

File.Name: 7421d_7421-023.jpg
Notes: Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae or kin in the order Hymenoptera) hiding under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the canyon. These wasps have no interest in us. They lay their eggs in or on other insect larvae, and their larvae consume them. Remember the movie "Alien"? It's like that. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2007)

File.Name: 7421e_7421-019.jpg
Notes: Native woodland Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the canyon. When I took the photo, I thought the ant was taking water from yesterday's rain, but now I see it's comsuming the gelatinous egg mass of some other critter. The closer you look, the more you see! (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2007)

File.Name: 7421f_7421-011.jpg
Notes: Strange mushroom-like gall on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. I believe this is the bisexual gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis aka C. pomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. The galls are polythamnous, with several larvae per gall. The parthenogenic females will emerge to produce "oak apple" galls on the oaks that will produce both male and female wasps to make another generation of these mushroom-like galls. Nature is strange and wonderful! (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2007)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; C. pomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; C. pomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 7422a_7421-037.jpg
Notes:
This little House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bird finally got the big stick into the house, but it didn't know what to do with the extra bit hanging out. After the bird flew away, I played the "Deus ex machina" and broke off the branch. The birds came back and added the broken stem to their nest. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2007)

No time for photos today. I'm busy and there was light rain all day - perfect weather in this drought year!

File.Name: 7423a_7423-051.jpg
Notes:
Cute little peppercorn-size black Weevil (family Curculionidae in the order Coleoptera) on a flower bud of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) - in the Fabaceae family. Cute??? Well, they are pests, but they look like little elephants or anteaters with their long snouts and nifty antennae. They chew like beetles rather than suck like aphids. They are doing some damage to the lupines on the dry side of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2007)

This is as close as I an get. This photo is a 500x500 pixel crop from my 3008x2000 original, with my macro lens cranked into 1:1 magnification. By my reckoning - counting pixels and figuring my camera physical sensor size - this weevil is 2.6 mm from snout to rear. It's a pleasure to have some sunlight time in the garden now when I come home from school.

File.Name: 7423b_7421-039.jpg
Notes:
One more photo (for now) of the House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bird bringing on more stick to outfit its new house on the porch railing. Every spring these wonderful birds nest in the birdhouses at our home in places where we walk every day. We have three nest so far. They're good with us as neighbors. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2007)

I get birdsong every morning right outside my study window. Life is good.

File.Name: 7424a_7420-144.jpg
Notes: This is a Looper or Inchworm caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (family Geometridae in the order Lepidoptera) on a stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. These caterpillars move in loops because they only have two sets of prolegs at the rear rather than the usual five. For such a little critter, it's eating big chunks of this leaf! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2007)
CommonNames: Looper; Inchworm; Geometrid Moth; Wild Sweetpea; Pea

File.Name: 7424b_7420-149.jpg
Notes:
Be the stick!
It's a zen thing.

I guess this is the larva of a Geometrid Moth (family Geometridae in the order Lepidoptera) on a stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. These caterpillars walk funny as loopers or inchworms because they only have two sets of prolegs at the rear rather than the usual five. If you didn't already guess, that's the head on top with its legs folded underneath. It really looks like a stick! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2007)

File.Name: 7425a_7421-028.jpg
Notes: These flowers of native Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha) are high up in a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Heath (Ericaceae) family. The Clematis vine has climbed higher than I can reach into the tree. I got this photo with my 500 mm telephoto lens. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2007)

File.Name: 7426a_7426-068.jpg
Notes:
Immature "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) perched on flowers of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the Purslane (Portulacaceae) family, with the sun and a yellow Zinnia flower behind in the garden when I got home from school today. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2007)

This bug has 5-segmented antennae, so I guess it's a "Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae. Most of tese bugs don't stink at all - they smell like the herb cilantro!
CommonNames: True Bug; Miner's Lettuce; Purslane; Stink Bug
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Claytonia perfoliata; Portulacaceae; Zinnia; Pentatomidae
associatedTaxa: Hemiptera; Claytonia perfoliata; Portulacaceae; Zinnia; Pentatomidae

File.Name: 7427a_7426-078.jpg
Notes:
Strange little Lace Bug in the family Tingidae of the order Hemiptera on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. These little insects are only 2 or 3 mm long, maybe 1/8 inch. If you know insects, you can recognice it as a hemipteran, but they have a remarkable net-like lattice-work on their wings - and in this critter, the pronotum extends over its head like a hood. I wonder if they can fly? The wings remind me of shogun-era samurai armor! (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2007)

These little "suckers" are damaging the pea and lupine plants on the dry side of our garden. One of my books says they inject a liquid when laying eggs that hardens to form a bump. I wonder if those black asphat-like drops are more eggs.

File.Name: 7427b_7427-247.jpg
Notes: Pretty little caterpillar on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. I guess it's an early-stage Western Tussock Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Lymantriidae, genus Orgyia). The long hairs must protect these caterpillars from parasitic wasps and flies. These also make it hard to get focus unless you use manual focus. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2007)
CommonNames: Wild Sweetpea; Pea; Western Tussock Moth
ScientificName: Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae) family. I guess it's an early-stage Western Tussock Moth (order Lepidoptera; Lymantriidae; Orgyia
associatedTaxa: Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae) family. I guess it's an early-stage Western Tussock Moth (order Lepidoptera; Lymantriidae; Orgyia

File.Name: 7428a_7428-039.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working the non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) flowers in the garden. This butterfly looks freshly emerged. I've been finding their caterpillars in the woods, but this is the first adult I've managed to capture with my camera.

I took my usual walk in the woods this morning, but I found today's butterflies in the garden when I got home.
Locality: Asteraceae) flowers in the garden. This butterfly looks freshly emerged. I've been finding their caterpillars in the woods

File.Name: 7428b_7428-047.jpg
Notes: This is a Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly working the non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) flowers in the garden. It's hard to get a photo with their wings open. I'll try again tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2007)

File.Name: 7428c_7428-042.jpg
Notes: Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly working a non-native Coreopsis flower in the garden. This is our plainest butterfly. It looks almost moth-like, but the antennae knobs give it away. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2007)

File.Name: 7429a_7429-073.jpg
Notes:
Check out the attitude in the upper bug's eye - what are YOU looking at!

I found this mating pair of coin-sized Assassin Bugs in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera on a flower stalk of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family near home. Even in this intimate moment, they look nasty! I believe these are Spotted Assasin Bugs (Rhynocoris ventralis). They are said to have one of the most painful bites of any local insect, and you can see their mighty probosces (sp?) if you look close. It must be hard for such perfect solitary preditors to get close enough together to mate! But once they go at it, they don't mind me getting very close. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2007)

I don't usually advertise, but I have lots of good photos today - take a look!
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Rhynocoris ventralis
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Rhynocoris ventralis

File.Name: 7429b_7429-080.jpg
Notes: Tiny Bee Fly (order Diptera, family Bombyliidae) working a non-native yellow flower head of Coreopsis (Asteraceae) in the garden. These flies move like little helicopters. They hover over the flowers instead of landing, and they move from flower to flower very fast. I took many photos today to get this keeper. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2007)

File.Name: 7429c_7429-093.jpg
Notes:
I admit - I'm fond of flies. I see so many different kinds of flies at home in the mountains away from the city. They might be my favorite insects for their variety, but they sure are hard to identify from a photo!

This little fly (insect order Diptera) is mosquito-like, but I'm sure it's not a mosquito. My first thought with those big eyes and slender abdomen is a Dance Fly in the family Empididae. It's working a flower head of non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2007)

File.Name: 7429d_7429-082.jpg
Notes:
Interesting little fly perched on a flower bud of non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) in the garden - not every fly is "just a fly"! My first thought was "Syrphid" fly, but I got my books out and looked on the web. I guess this is a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae, order Diptera) - maybe genus Gymnosoma or kin. I hope tansimplearytb will help keep me honest!

Tachinid flies are parasitic on other insects in the woods, but the typical ones are bristly, so it wasn't my first thought. My best source for insect ID is becoming http://www.bugguide.net/.

File.Name: 7429e_7428-041.jpg
Notes:
Butterfly social life is hard too.

Look close! The Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly in the lower left is chasing away the second Gabb's Checkerspot in the upper right in a blur. The plant is a non-native Coreopsis flower in the garden. I don't know if this is competition or courting, and maybe they're confused too! You know how it is... (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2007)

File.Name: 7429f_7429-067.jpg
Notes: What can I say? This newly emerged Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly looks so proud and perfect. It's resting on a stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2007)

File.Name: 7430a_7429-065.jpg
Notes: Newly emerged Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly resting on a leaf (with tendril) of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family in the woods. This is a Brushfoot butterfly in the Nymphalidae family. All insects have six legs, but look closer at this butterfly. It is perching on just four legs, with two tiny bright orange front legs held up in T-rex fashion. Many of our most distinctive butterflies share this trait of perching on four legs with two in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2007)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Wild Sweetpea; Pea; Brushfoot
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae) family in the woods. This is a Brushfoot butterfly in the Nymphalidae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae) family in the woods. This is a Brushfoot butterfly in the Nymphalidae
Families: Fabaceae) family in the woods. This is a Brushfoot butterfly in the Nymphalidae

File.Name: 7430b_7429-069.jpg
Notes: This Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly (order Lepidoptera) looks plain with wings folded up on flowers of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. But look closer to spot its proboscis on the left. The butterfly is cheating the flower by entering under the calyx at the base of the flower rather than going through the throat and helping pollination. Many different kinds of insects seem to know this shortcut! (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2007)
ScientificName: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Lepidoptera; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Lepidoptera; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae

File.Name: 7430c_7429-078.jpg
Notes:
Another photo of the mating pair of coin-sized Assassin Bugs in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera that I found on a flower stalk of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. See my [Previous] photo for another shot. This photo is the shady side, but it shows the shape and colors of the upper insect. I believe these are Spotted Assasin Bugs (Rhynocoris ventralis). They are said to have one of the most painful bites of any local insect. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2007)

Don't try this at home!
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Rhynocoris ventralis
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Rhynocoris ventralis

File.Name: 7501a_7428-012.jpg
Notes: Little Beetle (order Coleoptera) silhouetted on a new flower of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis var. redivivus aka S. redivivus) in the Storax (Styracaceae) family. The flowers are fragrant and beautiful and unusual. I believe that "rare" for native plants really means "rarely common". These rare native shrubs are common in the canyon, just starting to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2007)
ScientificName: Coleoptera) silhouetted on a new flower of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis; redivivus; S. redivivus; Styracaceae
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera) silhouetted on a new flower of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis; redivivus; S. redivivus; Styracaceae

File.Name: 7501b_7428-007.jpg
Notes: Little spider (Arachnida) hiding in the shade under a concave leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the canyon. The spider might be a little Orb-weaver (family Araneidae), though you can only see a piece of its web here. It's hard to get a photo in the shade with such contrasty light on the shiny leaf. The spider has a tiny fly as prey. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2007)

File.Name: 7501c_7427-237.jpg
Notes: Little spider (Arachnida) hiding under the tendrils of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. This is a tight crop of a close photo. The spider's translucent legs look like they're made of glass. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2007)

File.Name: 7502a_7429-059.jpg
Notes:
Little Beetle (order Coleoptera) on a petal of native Blue Dicks (Dichelostemma capitatum), aka Brodiaea for the squeamish, in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family. The short wing covers and protruding abdomen suggest a Blister Beetle in the family Meloidae. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2007)

It's a busy night...
ScientificName: Coleoptera) on a petal of native Blue Dicks (Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Meloidae
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera) on a petal of native Blue Dicks (Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Meloidae

File.Name: 7503a_7429-061.jpg
Notes: Perfect new Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly resting on a leaf (with tendril) of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. It was spread-out like a solar collector catching rays from the sun, and it really didn't want to move. I guess it's freshly emerged from its chrysalis. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2007)

File.Name: 7503b_7429-097.jpg
Notes:
I just noticed this mature chrysalis of a Swallowtail (genus Papilio) butterfly on the post right outside our front door. I guess I've been walking past it every day for weeks without noticing!

By its size, I guess this is an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly. I've seen a few adults on the wing. No food plant is nearby, but the caterpillars travel before pupating. The sculpted chrysalis is fascinating. Look close to see the impressions of eye and antenna and wings, and other structural features as the caterpillar reassembles itself into a beautiful butterfly. I took this photo a few days ago, and checked again tonight. The chrysalis is still there, maybe a bit darker in color. I'll keep watching. I hope the new butterfly emerges this weekend when I'm home! (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2007)

File.Name: 7503c_7428-029.jpg
Notes:
First flower of native Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus) that I've found in the canyon. Note the little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) straddleing the stamens for pollen. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2007)

This blackberry-relative is common and almost weedy in the Sierras and Oregon, but it's scarce here in the Santa Barbara mountains. I've never found fruit. Maybe the birds get them first, or maybe this plant is a rarely-reproducing remnant of wetter Pleistocene times. This summer will be a test for these plants in this very dry rain year.

File.Name: 7504a_7426-077.jpg
Notes: Tiny fly (order Diptera) perched on a flower of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae family. This fly wasn't interested in feeding. I watched it ovipositing - laying her eggs - on the petals of the flower. Look close and you can see that her abdomen is extended in preparation. These flowers only last a few days. I wonder what prey her eggs will target? (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2007)

File.Name: 7504b_7429-056.jpg
Notes: New native Larkspur (I guess Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum but cp. D. parryi) wildflowers in the canyon. The blue color is amazing. I tried to adjust saturation, but it's best right from my camera. These natives have weak stems, and the flowers are tangled with a Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) vine. These are the only larkspur flowers I've seen near home in this drought year. They've been damaged by little insects. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2007)
ScientificName: Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum; D. parryi; Lathyrus latifolius
associatedTaxa: Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum; D. parryi; Lathyrus latifolius

File.Name: 7505a_7505-055.jpg
Notes: Mating pair of Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterflies (order Lepidoptera) resting on a native Rush (family Juncaceae) stem in the woods. It's spring, and there is love in the woods! I reckon that's the male on the right. You can see a bit of its blue upper wings showing through the tear in its wing. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2007)

File.Name: 7505b_7505-042.jpg
Notes: I guess this is an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly in the woods today, though I suppose it could be a Lupine Blue (P. lupinus). Note the iridescent sparkles on the dark submarginal band along the hind wing. It's perched on a grass stem in the woods, probably that "bad grass" - Smilo Grass (Piptatherum miliaceum). (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2007)
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; Lupine Blue; Smilo Grass
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; P. lupinus; Piptatherum miliaceum
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; P. lupinus; Piptatherum miliaceum

File.Name: 7505c_7505-087.jpg
Notes: Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly working a non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) flower in the garden. There's lots of these uncommon butterflies in this drought year. Go figure. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2007)

File.Name: 7505d_7426-076.jpg
Notes: Tiny fly (order Diptera) perched on a flower of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae family. It's ovipositing - laying her eggs - on the wing petal of this papilionaceous flower. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2007)

File.Name: 7506a_7505-007.jpg
Notes: Common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a volunteer flower of Borage (Borago officinalis in the Boraginaceae family) growing in the garden. This bee is multitasking! It's probing the flower for nectar with its proboscis and collecting pollen with its hind legs at the same time. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2007)

File.Name: 7506b_7505-079.jpg
Notes: I'm pretty sure this is a Leafcutter Bee (family Megachilidae in the order Hymenoptera) working a new flower of native Sticky Cinquefoil (Potentilla glandulosa) in the in the Rosaceae family growing in the canyon. These wild bees collect pollen on their abdomens instead of their legs. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2007)

File.Name: 7506c_7505-071.jpg
Notes: Mating pair of Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterflies (order Lepidoptera) with a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) serving as a fig leaf. See my [Previous] photo of the same butterflies. I got too close with my camera and they flew away united together. I got a second chance for a photo, but I hope I didn't disturb the moment too much! (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2007)

File.Name: 7507a_7507-130.jpg
Notes:
Perfect Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly working new flowers of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. This shrub came up as a seedling in our semi-wild garden next to our house - that's the brown background. The little flowers don't look like much, but they support much life. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2007)

It's a treat to come home from school and still have light for a photo in the garden!

File.Name: 7507b_7506-120.jpg
Notes:
Little hummingbird near the ground working new flowers of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. The green leaves are Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae family. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2007)

Maybe this is a female Allen's or Rufous Hummingbird (genus Selasphorus) because of the yellow colors? Look close to see how pollination happens. The male stamens of the flower are touching the head of the bird, but the longer female style is held further back. See my [Previous] photo. As the flower ages, the style will curve lower to receive pollen from another hummingbird. In time, the style will curve down low enough to receive its own pollen as self-pollination is better than none. Always have a backup plan!

I got this photo looking down from the upstairs porch using my 500 mm long lens.
Locality: genus Selasphorus) because of the yellow colors? Look close to see how pollination happens. The male stamens of the flower are touching the head of the bird
ScientificName: Salvia spathacea; Lamiaceae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Selasphorus
associatedTaxa: Salvia spathacea; Lamiaceae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Selasphorus

File.Name: 7507c_7505-010.jpg
Notes: New flower of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. The forked female receptive style (on the left) is held back from the pollen-bearing male stamens (on the right) to avoid self-pollination. In time, the female style moves forward. This is a classic hummingbird flower. See my [Next] photo to see how it works. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2007)

File.Name: 7508a_7506-128.jpg
Notes: I believe this pretty snake is a California Striped Racer (Masticophis lateralis). We try to keep our house cat Narcissa indoors at this time of year when the birds and reptiles are so vulnerable, but she managed to get outside and attacked this little snake. You can see some damage behind the snakes' head where it crosses the big loop of its body. After I got this close photo, the snake slithered away, so I hope it's ok. Note the milky eye. I reckon this snake was molting and vulnerable when our cat found it. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2007)

File.Name: 7509a_7505-023.jpg
Notes: This is not a snake like my [Previous] photo - but a Snakefly (order Neuroptera, family Raphidiidae, genus Agulla) insect perched on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon. These insects are aware. You can look them in the eye, and they follow your every move! I guess this is a female with a fat abdomen full of eggs, and that large spine in the rear is an ovipositor to deposit eggs in tree bark. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2007)

File.Name: 7510a_7509-137.jpg
Notes:
Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) in the air working flowers of a non-native Sage (genus Salvia in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2007)

What can I say?

File.Name: 7511a_7509-142.jpg
Notes:
Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) in the air working flowers of a non-native Sage (genus Salvia in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family) in the garden. See my [Previous] two photos to appreciate how deep this hummer is delving into the flower. I love her little feet! (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2007)

We've been collecting SageSalvia in the garden for a few years, but I'm not sure what this specimen is. Looking at the photos in my "Book of Salvias" (Betsy Clebsch, 1997), the flowers look like Salvia interrupta) - native to the Atlas Mountains in Morocco - but our garden plant seems larger and wilder. I'm not sure what it is. The hummingbirds like it!
ScientificName: Calypte anna; Salvia; Lamiaceae; Salvia; Salvia interrupta
associatedTaxa: Calypte anna; Salvia; Lamiaceae; Salvia; Salvia interrupta

File.Name: 7511b_7509-152.jpg
Notes: Close crop of a female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) in the air working flowers of a non-native Sage (genus Salvia in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family) in the garden. You can see how pollination happens - the sexual parts of the flower are making contact with the hummer's bill and head, so that the little hummingbird will carry pollen from flower to flower. See my [Next] and [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2007)

File.Name: 7512a_7512-033.jpg
Notes: Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) snuggling into a beautiful new flower of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis var. redivivus aka S. redivivus) in the Storax (Styracaceae) family in the canyon. Note the full pollen basket on the bee! These uncommon shrubs are common in our canyon, but I find few insects working the fragrant flowers. I really don't understand this remarkable native shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2007)
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Styrax officinalis; redivivus; S. redivivus; Styracaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Styrax officinalis; redivivus; S. redivivus; Styracaceae

File.Name: 7512b_7512-047.jpg
Notes:
There are so many interesting flies in the woods other than the nasty ones!

I guess this is a Dance Fly in the family Empididae of the order Diptera - but I wouldn't bet the farm on it. The little fly is working a new flower of native Sticky Cinquefoil (Potentilla glandulosa) in the in the Rosaceae family growing in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2007)

File.Name: 7512c_7512-057.jpg
Notes:
Sometimes a fly is not just another fly!

This is a remarkable bristly Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae in the order Diptera) working new flowers of Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. You can see its inflated pencil-like proboscis entering a flower for nectar. These flies are almost honey bee size. It's hard to get a photo because they move from flower to flower so fast. I was focused on another insect to the right when this critter flew right into my frame. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2007)

File.Name: 7513a_7513-071.jpg
Notes:
Little butterfly caterpillar (Lepidoptera) hanging on to a stem of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass. I don't know what butterfly this larva will become. Go ask Alice! (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2007)

No time for photos today - I'm putting together my school slideshow from the thousands of photos I've taken at school this year apart from these Treebeard photos. I took a break today in the garden, but my hands are getting shaky from too much computer time. I got this photo using my camera self-timer. Summer is coming!

File.Name: 7513b_7505-093.jpg
Notes: Pretty butterfly chrysalis - you know the order: egg, caterpillar, chrysalis, adult - hanging on a munched leaf of native California Figwort aka Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family in the garden. We let the caterpillars have this plant, and I look forward to new butterflies. I guess this will emerge as a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2007)
CommonNames: California Figwort; Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Figwort; Variable Checkerspot

File.Name: 7514a_7512-001.jpg
Notes:
Pretty Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly doing lariat (la riata) tricks with its proboscis while working a non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) flower in the garden. Something about these paticular garden flowers is good for checkerspot butterflies! (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2007)

See my [Previous] two photos for two different checkerspots working the same flowers. This is biodiversity in the garden, right outside the door. We have to study to notice the diversity, but we'll miss it when its gone. Protect the garden. Hm hoom!
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; la riata; Coreopsis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; la riata; Coreopsis; Asteraceae

File.Name: 7514b_7513-060.jpg
Notes: I studied my books tonight, and I believe this is an Edith's Checkerspot (Euphydras editha) butterfly working the non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) flower in the garden. It looks like a smaller Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) in the garden, but it moves differently. Looking close at the photo, I can see it has different colors and dark antennal clubs. Compare my [Next] and [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2007)
ScientificName: Euphydras editha; Coreopsis; Asteraceae; Euphydryas chalcedona
associatedTaxa: Euphydras editha; Coreopsis; Asteraceae; Euphydryas chalcedona

File.Name: 7514c_7505-101.jpg
Notes: Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly working a non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) flower in the garden. Compare my next two photos to learn about two different checkspots working the same flowers. Something about these garden flowers is good for checkerspot butterflies! (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2007)

File.Name: 7515a_7513-073.jpg
Notes: Tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hiding in the little Thyme (Thymus vulgaris or kin in the Lamiaceae family) flowers in the garden. These spiders sit like little mousetraps ready to spring on insect visitors to the flowers. Awesome camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2007)
